tEn: Evan Spiegel: From Introvert to Snapchat Billionaire
tZh: 埃文·斯皮格尔：从内向者到 Snapchat 亿万富翁
sEn: Evan Spiegel, co-founder of Snapchat, shares his journey from building his own computer as a kid to turning down a $3 billion offer from Mark Zuckerberg, and the principles that drove his success.
sZh: Snapchat 联合创始人埃文·斯皮格尔分享了他从童年组装电脑到拒绝扎克伯格 30 亿美元收购的历程，以及驱动他成功的核心原则。

consensus.en: Maximize your rate of learning; the best way to have a good idea is to have lots of ideas.
consensus.zh: 最大化学习速度；拥有好想法的最佳途径是拥有大量想法。

consensus.en: Kindness is essential for a creative culture; it enables people to share crazy ideas without fear.
consensus.zh: 善良是创意文化的关键要素，它让人们能够毫无顾虑地分享疯狂的想法。

consensus.en: Focus on building a platform or ecosystem, not just a feature, to make copying difficult.
consensus.zh: 专注于构建平台或生态系统，而不仅仅是单一功能，以增加被抄袭的难度。

consensus.en: Take some money off the table early to allow founders to swing for the fences without personal risk.
consensus.zh: 早期适当变现部分股份，让创始人能够无后顾之忧地全力冲刺。

consensus.en: Embed company values into hiring, promotion, and performance evaluation early to maintain culture.
consensus.zh: 尽早将公司价值观融入招聘、晋升和绩效评估中，以维护企业文化。

contrarian.en: Turning down a $3 billion offer from Facebook was the right decision; they loved their product and believed in its future.
contrarian.zh: 拒绝 Facebook 30 亿美元的收购是正确的决定；他们热爱自己的产品并相信其未来。

contrarian.en: Snapchat's design team of nine people is flat and generates 99% bad ideas, but that 1% drives innovation.
contrarian.zh: Snapchat 的设计团队只有 9 人且结构扁平，99% 的想法都不好，但剩下的 1% 推动了创新。

contrarian.en: Content moderation is not censorship; it's a values-based choice that creates a comfortable environment for users.
contrarian.zh: 内容审核并非审查制度；它是基于价值观的选择，旨在为用户创造舒适的环境。

contrarian.en: Meta copying Snapchat's features is frustrating because their products make people unhappy, unlike Snapchat.
contrarian.zh: Meta 抄袭 Snapchat 的功能令人沮丧，因为他们的产品让人不开心，而 Snapchat 则不然。

contrarian.en: The best leaders are T-shaped: deep expertise in one area and broad understanding of the whole business.
contrarian.zh: 最好的领导者是 T 型人才：在某一领域有深厚专长，同时对整个业务有广泛理解。

turns.en: You became the world's youngest billionaire at the age of 25. You've got Mark Zuckerberg offering you $3 billion. That was a fateful day for sure, but we decided that we'd rather go it alone. Was there ever a day where you doubted that decision? Evan Spiegel is the co-founder behind one of the world's biggest social media platforms, Snapchat. He turned disappearing messages into a multi-billion dollar empire, redefining how we connect online. Evan, you don't do many podcasts, do you?
turns.zh: 你在 25 岁时成为全球最年轻的亿万富翁。马克·扎克伯格曾向你出价 30 亿美元。那确实是决定命运的一天，但我们决定单干。你有没有哪一天怀疑过那个决定？埃文·斯皮格尔是 Snapchat 的联合创始人，Snapchat 是全球最大的社交媒体平台之一。他把阅后即焚的消息变成数十亿美元的帝国，重新定义了我们的在线连接方式。埃文，你不常上播客，对吧？

turns.en: I don't do much public speaking at all, but I want to share a bit more. So, let's go back to those early days. I was an introvert growing up and I loved to build stuff. At school, I had built my own computer. And once you start realizing that things that look really complicated on the surface aren't that difficult, you start wondering, you know, what else you can build. So, that led to building Snapchat at 21. I was in undergrad at Stanford and we'd raised $485,000 at a $4.25 million valuation. What a deal. But back then there were a lot of apps that would get popular really quickly and then sort of fade away. And a lot of people told us that we should sell it. They said you're just sending photos back and forth. How is this going to grow for the long term? But the growth of Snapchat was atypical to say the least. It was like this virus and it was reaching 75 million users on a monthly basis.
turns.zh: 我几乎不做公开演讲，但我想多分享一些。那么，让我们回到早期。我小时候是个内向的人，喜欢动手做东西。在学校里，我自己组装了一台电脑。一旦你开始意识到表面上看起来很复杂的东西其实没那么难，你就会开始想，你还能造出什么。这最终让我在 21 岁时创立了 Snapchat。我当时在斯坦福读本科，我们以 425 万美元的估值筹集了 48.5 万美元。真是一笔好交易。但那时有很多应用会迅速走红，然后又逐渐消失。很多人告诉我们应该卖掉它。他们说你们只是在来回发送照片，这怎么能长期增长？但 Snapchat 的增长至少可以说是非典型的。它像病毒一样传播，每月达到 7500 万用户。

turns.en: So I wondered if you had any advice on the fundamental principles of success.
turns.zh: 所以我想知道，你对成功的基本原则有什么建议吗？

turns.en: How much people care about what they do and the ability to move quickly is the predictor of success. And at Snapchat we have a really small design team. It's nine people who are constantly generating an incredible number of ideas and products and features because 99% of ideas are not good, but 1% is.
turns.zh: 人们对自己所做的事情有多在意，以及快速行动的能力，是成功的预测因素。在 Snapchat，我们有一个非常小的设计团队，只有九个人，他们不断产生大量的想法、产品和功能，因为 99% 的想法都不好，但 1% 是好的。

turns.en: I want to know what they teach at Stanford because the success rate of creating some of the world's preeminent entrepreneurs is really high. There were a lot of very good lessons. The first one is this has always blown my mind a little bit. 53% of you that listen to this show regularly haven't yet subscribed to the show. So, could I ask you for a favor before we start? If you like the show and you like what we do here and you want to support us, the free simple way that you can do just that is by hitting the subscribe button. And my commitment to you is if you do that, then I'll do everything in my power, me and my team, to make sure that this show is better for you every single week. We'll listen to your feedback. We'll find the guests that you want me to speak to and we'll continue to do what we do. Thank you so much, Evan. When you look back over your earliest years and you try and make sense of the dots that connected in hindsight, I guess, as Steve Jobs once said, what are those dots?
turns.zh: 我想知道斯坦福教了什么，因为培养出一些世界顶级企业家的成功率非常高。有很多非常好的经验。第一点总是让我有点惊讶：53% 的定期收听本节目的人还没有订阅。所以，在我们开始之前，我能请你帮个忙吗？如果你喜欢这个节目，喜欢我们在这里做的事情，并且想支持我们，一个免费简单的方法就是点击订阅按钮。我向你承诺，如果你这样做，我和我的团队会尽一切努力，让这个节目每周都变得更好。我们会听取你的反馈，找到你希望我采访的嘉宾，并继续做我们正在做的事情。非常感谢你，埃文。当你回顾早年经历，试图理解那些在事后看来相互关联的点时，我想，正如史蒂夫·乔布斯所说，那些点是什么？

turns.en: There were a couple fateful choices that my parents made that I think had a huge impact. One was that they never let me watch TV. They didn't want me to spend my time doing that, but at the same time would allow me to get whatever book I wanted. And that was a really formative experience for me. Reading a book you get to use your imagination a lot to try to paint the characters in your own mind and that was really helpful. And because I wasn't watching TV I had a lot of time on my hands, so I liked to build stuff. When I was young I would make a little fake hotel in our living room and have a guest experience where my parents could come and try to stay at our hotel. I got to use my imagination a lot at home and my parents never made me feel bad about turning the house upside down, moving chairs around to express myself and make stuff.
turns.zh: 我父母做了几个决定性的选择，我认为影响巨大。一个是从不让我看电视。他们不想让我把时间花在那上面，但同时允许我买任何我喜欢的书。那对我来说是非常有塑造性的经历。读书时，你需要大量运用想象力，在脑海中描绘人物，这非常有帮助。因为不看电视，我有很多空闲时间，所以我喜欢做东西。小时候，我会在客厅里搭一个假旅馆，让父母来体验入住。我在家可以尽情发挥想象力，父母从不因为我弄得家里乱七八糟、搬动椅子来表达自己和做东西而责备我。

turns.en: Did you feel like you fitted in when you were a kid?
turns.zh: 你小时候觉得自己合群吗？

turns.en: No, not at all. I was more of an introvert growing up. So, I think sometimes that made it harder for me to feel like I fit in.
turns.zh: 一点也不。我小时候比较内向。所以，我觉得这有时让我更难感到合群。

turns.en: And when did computers come into the picture for you?
turns.zh: 那你是什么时候开始接触电脑的？

turns.en: I guess I was exposed to my first computer. Gosh, probably kindergarten, first grade, maybe around that period of time. My godfather brought over one of the early Macintoshes to show our family and I got to try out things like Kidpics and stuff like that. Then later on in school, I went to the computer lab a lot. I really wanted my own computer. So the big breakthrough was when my mom said, if you build your own computer, you can have it. We won't let you connect it to the internet, but if you build your own computer, you can have it to play with. So by sixth grade, I had a teacher who helped me take all the different pieces you need and put them all together to build a computer. I think the act of putting together these pieces, turning it on, getting Windows up and running, just made me realize why it seems so complicated on the outside when you're just looking at that tower, that box, right? You haven't yet opened it up and seen what's inside. I think it can seem really confusing or complicated but as soon as you realize it's not that hard to put it all together and get started, there's something really empowering about that feeling.
turns.zh: 我想我第一次接触电脑，天哪，大概是在幼儿园或一年级左右。我的教父带来了一台早期的 Macintosh 电脑给我们家看，我试用了 Kidpics 之类的软件。后来在学校里，我经常去计算机实验室。我非常想要一台自己的电脑。所以最大的突破是我妈妈说，如果你自己组装一台电脑，你就可以拥有它。我们不会让你联网，但如果你自己组装，你就可以玩。所以到了六年级，有一位老师帮我拿了所有需要的零件，把它们组装成一台电脑。我认为把这些零件拼起来、开机、让 Windows 运行起来的过程，让我明白了为什么从外面看那个机箱、那个盒子会显得那么复杂，对吧？你还没打开它看到里面。我觉得它可能看起来很令人困惑或复杂，但一旦你意识到把它组装起来并开始使用并不难，那种感觉真的很有力量。

turns.en: What does that feeling teach you?
turns.zh: 那种感觉教会了你什么？

turns.en: I think this is much more the case now because if you go on YouTube you can learn how to do pretty much anything. But once you start realizing that things that look really complicated or confusing on the surface aren't that difficult, you start wondering what else you can build or create, or how else you can experiment with something that seems impossible from the outside, but really it's just not that hard.
turns.zh: 我认为现在更是如此，因为如果你上 YouTube，几乎可以学会做任何事情。但一旦你开始意识到表面上看起来很复杂或令人困惑的东西其实没那么难，你就会开始想还能造出什么、创造什么，或者还能如何尝试那些从外面看似乎不可能，但实际上并不难的事情。

turns.en: And you got bullied in school, right? Was it sixth grade that you got bullied or was it sometime thereafter?
turns.zh: 你在学校被欺负过，对吧？是六年级还是之后？

turns.en: Yeah, middle school was not the easiest time for me.
turns.zh: 是的，中学对我来说不是最轻松的时期。

turns.en: Why?
turns.zh: 为什么？

turns.en: As I mentioned, I sort of had trouble fitting in. I didn't do a lot of activities that some other schoolmates did like sports and stuff. I played a little bit on the tennis team. But I think the combination of not really playing sports with friends, spending a lot of time in the computer lab at lunch or after school, just led to me feeling a bit socially isolated at what is a tricky time for lots of kids.
turns.zh: 正如我提到的，我有点难以融入。我不像其他同学那样参加很多活动，比如运动之类的。我打过一点网球。但我觉得，不和朋友一起运动，午休或放学后花大量时间在计算机实验室，这些因素加在一起让我在社交上感到有些孤立，而这对很多孩子来说都是一个棘手的时期。

turns.en: What were you like as a kid? Were you confident?
turns.zh: 你小时候是什么样子的？你自信吗？

turns.en: I don't know if I was confident in myself per se, but I definitely was confident in my ideas.
turns.zh: 我不知道我是否对自己本身自信，但我绝对对自己的想法自信。

turns.en: Like I was willing to take a stand for ideas that I thought were different or I was willing to explore ideas that didn't seem popular at the time because I thought it was important. I was talking to my dad, who has been staying with us for a while, and I asked him, 'What stories do you think I could tell about growing up?' And he said, 'Oh, you should tell them. You were definitely like a contrarian.' I asked, 'What do you mean by contrarian?' He said, 'Don't you remember you wrote that article that was like an exposé of the math program? You basically interviewed all these teachers, kids, and parents, and wrote this whole exposé about how the math program could be better.' It was sort of maybe better left unsaid in that environment, but the school, to their credit, supported me and let me publish it. I think they created an environment where kids could challenge authority, which was something I learned was okay.
turns.zh: 我愿意为自己认为不同的想法挺身而出，也愿意探索当时看来不受欢迎的想法，因为我觉得那很重要。我跟我爸聊过，他最近一直住在我家，我问他：“你觉得我成长过程中有什么故事可以讲？”他说：“哦，你应该讲讲。你绝对是个反叛者。”我问：“反叛者是什么意思？”他说：“你不记得你写了那篇揭露数学课程的文章吗？你采访了所有老师、孩子和家长，写了一整篇揭露文章，指出数学课程如何可以改进。”在那个环境下，那件事也许最好不提，但学校很支持我，让我发表了。我觉得他们创造了一个允许孩子挑战权威的环境，这让我明白挑战权威是可以的。

turns.en: I guess that's a principle as well of many of the people that I meet like you: they're okay with pushing against convention. In certain moments in your life, you make these decisions which one would say are contrarian bets. I can see them all over your story, but clearly that was something innate in you from a fairly young age as you look back. You don't do many podcasts, do you?
turns.zh: 我想这也是我遇到的很多像你这样的人的一个原则：他们不介意挑战常规。在你人生的某些时刻，你会做出一些可以说是反常规的赌注。我在你的故事里到处都能看到这一点，但显然，回顾过去，这在你很小的时候就与生俱来了。你不怎么做播客，对吧？

turns.en: I don't do much public speaking at all. It's a 2025 New Year's resolution for me, though. So, we'll see. I'm trying to share a bit more.
turns.zh: 我几乎不怎么公开演讲。不过，这是我 2025 年的新年决心。所以，走着瞧吧。我正在努力多分享一些。

turns.en: Why?
turns.zh: 为什么？

turns.en: I think it's really important that people understand our company and what we stand for, why we make the decisions that we do. Part of that is getting to know me and Bobby. I started this business 13 years ago, and we have made a bunch of different choices along the way. But I think unless we talk about them, nobody knows. So it's really important for us to share how we make decisions, our design philosophy, and that kind of thing.
turns.zh: 我认为让人们了解我们的公司、我们的立场以及我们做决策的原因非常重要。其中一部分就是让大家了解我和 Bobby。我 13 年前创办了这家公司，一路上我们做了很多不同的选择。但我觉得，如果我们不说出来，没人会知道。所以，分享我们的决策方式、设计理念等等，对我们来说真的很重要。

turns.en: It's a really interesting time to be a CEO generally. Even 10 or 15 years ago, CEOs of major companies that so many people use and love weren't doing podcasts. They might release press releases, and their marketing team would run the comms. But there's been this big shift towards leadership transparency, where leaders are expected to be glass boxes. I think even beyond that, media has really reshaped to focus on individuals. Individuals are what people are interested in; they're the ones who have distribution. So the center of gravity has shifted away from the entity like the business to focus more on the individual characters and storytellers. So you went off to university—you went to Stanford University, which is an incredible university—and you went to ultimately try and pursue product design at Stanford. Why did you choose product design at that stage in your life? What was it that was calling you about that course?
turns.zh: 总的来说，现在做 CEO 是一个非常有趣的时期。即使是在 10 或 15 年前，那些许多人使用和喜爱的知名公司的 CEO 们也不会做播客。他们可能会发新闻稿，由营销团队负责公关。但现在，领导力透明度发生了巨大转变，领导者被期望成为透明人。我认为不仅如此，媒体已经重新聚焦于个人。人们感兴趣的是个人，他们才是拥有传播力的人。所以重心已经从企业这样的实体转移到了个人角色和讲故事的人身上。那么，你上了大学——你去了斯坦福大学，一所了不起的大学——最终在斯坦福攻读产品设计。为什么在你人生的那个阶段选择了产品设计？这门课程吸引你的是什么？

turns.en: What's really cool about product design is the basic concept: you don't need to wait around for an idea to fall out of heaven or get struck by lightning. You can systematically create new ideas by listening to people, empathizing with them, prototyping solutions to the problems they share with you, and then iterating on those solutions by bringing them back to them and saying, 'What do you think? Does this solve your problem?' So for me, to be able to combine my love of making things with this process for making things that could be useful to people—making new products—that was really exciting to me. The product design school was created by a really visionary guy named David Kelly. I had the opportunity to take a class from him, and it was really an incredible experience.
turns.zh: 产品设计最酷的地方在于它的基本概念：你不需要等待灵感从天而降，或者被闪电击中。你可以通过倾听人们的声音、与他们共情、为他们提出的问题制作解决方案原型，然后带着这些方案回去问他们“你觉得怎么样？这能解决你的问题吗？”来系统地创造新想法。所以，对我来说，能够把我对制作东西的热爱与这个制作对人们有用的东西——创造新产品的过程结合起来，这真的让我很兴奋。产品设计学院是由一位非常有远见的人创立的，他叫 David Kelly。我有机会上过他的课，那真是一次不可思议的经历。

turns.en: What is product design for someone like me that has no idea what they teach in such a course? Is it because my head says like designing physical products?
turns.zh: 对于像我这样完全不知道这门课教什么的人来说，产品设计是什么？是因为我脑子里想的是设计实体产品吗？

turns.en: A lot of the product design school at Stanford is oriented around physical products, of course. Now it's so much more than that, but when I was there at the time, it was under the mechanical engineering department, very oriented around physical products, understanding materials, but all part of this framework of how do we understand the problems that people are facing, how do we empathize with them, and then how do we design solutions that solve those problems.
turns.zh: 斯坦福的产品设计学院很大程度上是围绕实体产品展开的，当然。现在它远不止于此，但当时我在那里的时候，它隶属于机械工程系，非常侧重于实体产品、理解材料，但所有这些都属于一个框架：我们如何理解人们面临的问题，如何与他们共情，然后如何设计出解决这些问题的方案。

turns.en: Did you learn entrepreneurship through this time as well? Because I think in your sophomore year you took a class on entrepreneurship and venture capital, right?
turns.zh: 你在这段时间也学到了创业精神吗？因为我想你在大二的时候上了一门关于创业和风险投资的课，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah, that was really a game changer. That class, called Entrepreneurship and Venture Capital, is a series of case studies basically led by entrepreneurs who come in and present the story of creating their business and lessons learned, and then it's an open Q&A. I got to listen to their amazing stories and ask them questions, and that was super inspiring to me.
turns.zh: 是的，那真的是一个转折点。那门课叫“创业与风险投资”，基本上是一系列案例研究，由企业家们来讲述他们创建企业的故事和学到的经验教训，然后是开放问答。我听到了他们精彩的故事，并向他们提问，这对我来说非常鼓舞人心。

turns.en: Do you remember anything you took away from those classes that ended up being really important for you in terms of an idea or a philosophy or anything?
turns.zh: 你记得从那些课上学到的任何东西，最终在想法、理念或其他方面对你非常重要吗？

turns.en: I think the biggest thing that I took away from my time at Stanford and from that class was the focus on going after really, really big opportunities. One of the things that's so different growing up here in LA: I think a lot of the business community that I was exposed to is more focused on cash flow—how quickly can this business turn a profit, how can we do that really predictably, how much cash are we going to generate? At Stanford, the business culture is entirely oriented around how big that opportunity is. Is that a huge opportunity? Is that opportunity big enough? Because if you're not going after something that could reach billions of people, that's not that interesting. That was a totally different way of thinking for me, combined with the venture capital approach, which is really to invest a lot of money early and scale quickly, and then build out the business later after you've achieved scale and mass adoption.
turns.zh: 我认为我在斯坦福和那门课上学到的最重要的东西是专注于追求真正巨大的机会。在洛杉矶长大，这里非常不同：我觉得我接触到的很多商业社区更关注现金流——这个业务能多快盈利，我们如何能非常可预测地做到这一点，我们能产生多少现金？在斯坦福，商业文化完全围绕这个机会有多大。这是一个巨大的机会吗？这个机会足够大吗？因为如果你不去追求可能触及数十亿人的东西，那就不那么有趣了。这对我来说是一种完全不同的思维方式，再加上风险投资的方法，即早期投入大量资金并快速扩张，然后在实现规模和大众采用之后再建立业务。

turns.en: I've always wondered what they teach at Stanford, especially as it relates to business, because the success rate of creating some of the world's preeminent entrepreneurs is really, really high. So you're telling me one of the key ideas is big ambitions.
turns.zh: 我一直想知道斯坦福教什么，尤其是与商业相关的，因为培养出一些世界顶尖企业家的成功率真的非常高。所以你是说其中一个关键理念就是远大的抱负。

turns.en: Yeah. And I think it makes sense because it's so hard to create a business; your odds of success are so low.
turns.zh: 是的。而且我认为这很有道理，因为创业太难了；成功的概率太低了。

turns.en: So it's really important that you go after something really big. So that if you're successful that at the end of the day there's a huge opportunity at the end of the rainbow. So in my head I go, well, if it's really really big, then the chance of failure is probably going to go up. So you know, I could open a coffee shop, right? And my chance of success is pretty decent. But if I go after building a new social network, which is something only a psychopath would do, then my odds of success are what, one in a gazillion.
turns.zh: 所以，追求一个真正宏大的目标非常重要。这样如果你成功了，最终就能在彩虹尽头获得巨大的机会。我心里想，嗯，如果目标真的非常非常大，那么失败的可能性可能也会上升。所以，你知道，我可以开一家咖啡店，对吧？我成功的几率还算不错。但如果我要去打造一个新的社交网络——这种事只有疯子才会做——那么我成功的概率是多少呢？十亿分之一吧。

turns.en: I think what is exciting though about the technology business is the way that it scales. And so I think what's different than your coffee shop example is once you build a great service, once we build Snapchat one time, it can scale to 850 million people around the world, right? Whereas you'd have to go build a new coffee shop on every street corner to scale the business. And so once I think you start seeing the world in terms of the potential to scale and the potential to build a product or service that can reach billions of people, it really changes the opportunities you identify or the things that you want to build.
turns.zh: 不过，我认为科技业务令人兴奋的地方在于它的规模化方式。所以，和你开咖啡店的例子不同，一旦你构建了一个优秀的服务——比如我们一次性地打造了 Snapchat——它就能扩展到全球 8.5 亿人，对吧？而你要想扩大咖啡店业务，就得在每个街角都开一家新店。所以，我认为一旦你开始从规模化潜力以及打造能触达数十亿人的产品或服务的潜力来看待世界，它真的会改变你识别出的机会，或者你想构建的东西。

turns.en: Interesting. So the question that most of us, especially in the UK, we often don't think about building businesses that have the potential to reach huge scale, in part because we don't have as much of a robust technical track record in terms of building great unicorn tech companies in Europe as you guys do over here. One of the real challenges in Europe is how small the different markets are in each country. And so I think what's really interesting is when I talk to entrepreneurs in Europe, oftentimes they're very focused on growing first in their country and using that market as a stepping stone. But all the countries in Europe are quite different. They're different cultures and different languages. And so sometimes entrepreneurs can spend too much time trying to grow in Europe rather than what I've seen out of some companies. In Australia, for example, they're on an island. The first thing that these entrepreneurs are thinking about is like, how do I go grow in the US? How do I go grow in China? How do I go grow in a really really big market and get to scale really quickly? And then I can go reinvest and grow in Europe or grow in other countries where it might be more difficult to grow.
turns.zh: 有意思。那么，我们大多数人，尤其是在英国，通常不会考虑建立有潜力达到巨大规模的企业，部分原因是我们没有像你们这里那样，在打造伟大的独角兽科技公司方面拥有那么扎实的技术记录。我认为欧洲的一个真正挑战是每个国家的市场都非常小。所以，我觉得非常有趣的是，当我与欧洲的创业者交谈时，他们往往非常专注于先在自己的国家发展，并把那个市场作为跳板。但欧洲所有国家都相当不同，有不同的文化和语言。所以，有时创业者会花太多时间试图在欧洲发展，而不是像我在一些公司看到的那样。例如，在澳大利亚，他们在一个岛上。这些创业者首先想到的是：我如何在美国发展？我如何在中国发展？我如何在一个真正巨大的市场发展并快速达到规模？然后我就可以再投资，在欧洲或其他更难发展的国家发展。

turns.en: That is so true. Thinking about my investment portfolio, there's about 40 different companies there and almost every single one of them, without exception, has adopted the approach of 'we'll crack the UK first and then we'll go and figure out the US.' But in that transition to the US, they encounter tons of challenges with how expensive it is to succeed here, like marketing costs here, if they're in retail, how difficult it is to get into Target or Walmart here. Also, the founders end up building their lives, their families in the UK, which means that the founders can't really uproot and move to the US later in the journey. So, most of them try the US, waste a ton of money, get burnt, run back, and I've seen that story play over and over again.
turns.zh: 说得太对了。想想我的投资组合，里面有大约 40 家不同的公司，几乎每一家，无一例外，都采取了“先攻克英国，然后再去搞定美国”的做法。但在向美国过渡的过程中，他们遇到了无数挑战，比如在这里成功的成本有多高——营销成本，如果他们是零售业，进入 Target 或 Walmart 有多难。此外，创始人最终在英国建立了自己的生活、家庭，这意味着他们后来无法真正连根拔起并搬到美国。所以，他们中的大多数尝试美国市场，浪费大量资金，被烧得焦头烂额，然后逃回来，我已经看到这个故事一遍又一遍地上演。

turns.en: When you think about penetrating these international markets, do you send core team members there?
turns.zh: 当你考虑打入这些国际市场时，你会派遣核心团队成员去那里吗？

turns.en: Oftentimes, what we've done with Snapchat is actually follow the growth. So looking for countries where people have already started using the product, already love it, are giving us a bunch of feedback, and then we'll send folks there or we'll figure out how to build on the momentum or make sure it's localized properly, make sure we're working with local creators so that the content's relevant. But I think, because our service is based on communication, Snapchat doesn't really work unless you're using it with a friend. You got to use it together. What we look for is just that momentum where friends are using it to communicate with one another and then figure out how to build on top of that with the content ecosystem or augmented reality and those sorts of things.
turns.zh: 通常，我们在 Snapchat 的做法实际上是跟随增长。也就是说，寻找那些人们已经开始使用产品、已经喜欢它、并给我们大量反馈的国家，然后我们会派人去那里，或者想办法利用这股势头，确保产品本地化得当，确保我们与当地创作者合作，使内容具有相关性。但我认为，因为我们的服务基于沟通，Snapchat 除非你和朋友一起使用，否则它真的不起作用。你们必须一起使用。我们寻找的就是那种朋友之间用它来交流的势头，然后想办法在此基础上构建内容生态系统或增强现实之类的东西。

turns.en: What was your first idea that failed?
turns.zh: 你的第一个失败的想法是什么？

turns.en: Oh, well, there were... I mean, I made an orange juicer at one point. But I think the biggest failure was Future Freshman. Bobby and I — I was really fortunate to meet Bobby. He lived across the hall from me at our fraternity at Stanford, and we shared this love of making stuff. So we had worked on a couple social ideas that were interesting, but the one thing we decided to spend a lot of time on was Future Freshman, which was designed to help kids apply to college. It was something that we'd had direct experience with, so we could empathize with how difficult the process is. We had siblings who were also applying to college. So we spent about 18 months building a full-featured website. You could select the schools you wanted to go to, it would aggregate all the essay questions and requirements and make it really easy to apply. But it was very clear by the end of that 18 months or so that it was going to be really difficult for us to win. We were up against a company called Naviance, which had their own software suite and they had a really good idea: they went to all the college counselors around the US in high schools and said, 'Hey, tell everyone to use Naviance, make sure your students and parents are using our platform.' So they got a lot of distribution through all the different schools. Obviously you're going to use the platform that's being recommended by your college counselor, not an app made by two kids out of Stanford. So we had a real distribution disadvantage. And then we also realized that even if we were wildly successful and we got the million students a year who applied to four-year colleges, we would have to then reacquire another million students the next year. So we had this realization that it was going to be really hard to build a big business, and that we really ought to try something different, and most importantly try to build something that wouldn't take 18 months to build before we got great feedback. So to try to build something really simple that people could try and that we could collect feedback on faster.
turns.zh: 哦，嗯，有过……我是说，我曾经做过一个橙汁机。但我认为最大的失败是 Future Freshman。鲍比和我——我非常幸运遇到了鲍比。他住在斯坦福我们兄弟会的走廊对面，我们都有对制作东西的热爱。所以我们之前做过几个有趣的社交想法，但我们决定投入大量时间的一件事是 Future Freshman，它旨在帮助孩子申请大学。这是我们有过直接经验的事情，所以我们对这个过程有多困难感同身受。我们有兄弟姐妹也在申请大学。所以我们花了大约 18 个月构建了一个功能齐全的网站。你可以选择你想去的学校，它会汇总所有的论文题目和要求，让申请变得非常容易。但到那 18 个月结束时，很明显我们很难获胜。我们面对一家叫 Naviance 的公司，他们有自己的软件套件，而且他们有一个非常好的主意：他们去找全美各地高中的大学辅导员，说：“嘿，告诉大家使用 Naviance，确保你的学生和家长使用我们的平台。”所以他们通过所有不同的学校获得了大量分发。显然，你会使用大学辅导员推荐的平台，而不是两个斯坦福孩子做的应用。所以我们有真正的分发劣势。然后我们还意识到，即使我们非常成功，每年获得一百万申请四年制大学的学生，我们第二年还得再获取另一百万学生。所以我们意识到，要建立一个大型企业真的很难，我们真的应该尝试一些不同的东西，最重要的是，尝试构建一些不需要花 18 个月就能得到很好反馈的东西。所以，尝试构建一些非常简单的东西，让人们可以尝试，并且我们可以更快地收集反馈。

transcript.secZh: 首个失败创意：未来新生

turns.en: So two points there. How do you know when to quit? You've kind of assembled a couple of principles there, but even it's I think it's difficult in business because you can be getting lots of negative feedback but that doesn't necessarily mean that the idea is something that you should quit.
turns.zh: 所以这里有两个要点。你怎么知道什么时候该放弃？你已经总结了一些原则，但即使在商业中，我认为这也很困难，因为你可能会收到大量负面反馈，但这并不一定意味着这个想法你应该放弃。

turns.en: I think the most important thing is to look at the fundamental structure of the business. In the case of Future Freshman, we had two fundamental problems: one was distribution, and the other was that we had to reacquire customers every year. Those are structural issues that are very hard to overcome. So I think when you see structural problems like that, it's a sign to quit. But if the feedback is about execution or product features, that's different. You can iterate on that.
turns.zh: 我认为最重要的是看业务的基本结构。在 Future Freshman 的案例中，我们有两个根本问题：一个是分发，另一个是我们每年都要重新获取客户。这些都是很难克服的结构性问题。所以我认为当你看到这样的结构性问题时，就是放弃的信号。但如果反馈是关于执行或产品功能的，那就不同了。你可以迭代改进。

transcript.sec: Knowing when to quit a business

turns.en: Maybe it means you should pivot or iterate or just keep going. But how did you know what were the stars that align that told you to quit that business?
turns.zh: 也许这意味着你应该转型、迭代或继续前进。但你是怎么知道哪些迹象告诉你该放弃那个业务的？

turns.en: I think for us it was that we didn't love the product enough. I think if you really love the product, you know, and you love what you're building, what you're doing, you can fight through just about anything. I mean, that was really the case with the early days of Snapchat. We loved building, using the product. We were using it all day with our friends. So we could really, we just had an attachment to it that we never really developed with Future Freshman because we weren't applying to college. So we didn't have that same connection with the product that we ended up developing with Snapchat.
turns.zh: 我认为对我们来说，是我们不够热爱这个产品。我觉得如果你真的热爱产品，热爱你正在构建和做的事情，你几乎可以克服任何困难。Snapchat 早期就是这样。我们热爱构建和使用产品，整天和朋友一起用。所以我们真的对它有一种依恋，这种依恋是我们在 Future Freshman 上从未有过的，因为我们自己并不申请大学。所以我们没有那种最终在 Snapchat 上建立起来的与产品的连接。

turns.en: And why does that end up mattering so much? That like love and passion for the thing that you're building?
turns.zh: 那为什么这一点如此重要？就是对你正在构建的东西的热爱和激情？

turns.en: Because I think that's what, you know, the love and passion for what you're building and the love and passion for the people you're working with, like that's what allows you to get through all the challenges that come when you try to build a business. I think if you don't love what you're doing, I mean, I just absolutely love what I do and the team that we get to work with and of course the products that we make, the community we serve. And I think without that just love for what you do, it can be hard.
turns.zh: 因为我认为，对你所构建的东西的热爱和激情，以及对你共事的人的热爱和激情，正是让你能够克服创业过程中所有挑战的东西。我觉得如果你不爱自己做的事——我绝对热爱我所做的事、我们合作的团队、我们创造的产品以及我们服务的社区。我认为没有这种热爱，事情会变得很难。

turns.en: And the other thing you said was you felt that you should go after a business that didn't take like 18 months, like two years of your time to build before you got it to market. Why is that for entrepreneurs that are listening that maybe have spent years perfecting something in their bedroom that hasn't gone to market yet? Why was that insight so important to you for your next venture?
turns.zh: 你提到的另一件事是，你觉得应该追求一个不需要花 18 个月或两年时间才能推向市场的业务。对于正在听、可能花了好几年在卧室里完善一个尚未上市的产品创业者来说，为什么这个洞察对你下一个创业项目如此重要？

turns.en: I think getting that feedback from your customers as quickly and early as possible is critical, even if it's on the back of a napkin. And like, "Hey, here's what I'm thinking. This is what it's going to look like." What do you think about that idea? Because it's very hard to know whether or not you have a good idea unless you can put something in front of people and have them use it. I mean, that's almost one of the cardinal rules of the product design program that you, I guess, we willfully ignored, which is that you should really rapidly prototype and get feedback as quickly as possible so that you know you're on the right track. I mean, even in the early days, Snapchat before it was called Snapchat was called Picaboo. It was more focused on disappearing messages. Very quickly we learned that wasn't interesting to people. They wanted to communicate with pictures. They wanted to talk with pictures. So when we called the app Snapchat, we explained that it was 10 times faster than sending a photo via text message. People were like, "Oh, I want that. That's something that I'll use every day." And so it was just really interesting to get the feedback really early on with an initial version of the app that took a couple months to create.
turns.zh: 我认为尽快尽早从客户那里获得反馈至关重要，哪怕只是在餐巾纸背面画个草图，说“嘿，我在想这个，它大概长这样，你觉得这个主意怎么样？”因为除非你能把东西摆在人们面前让他们使用，否则很难知道你的想法好不好。这几乎是产品设计课程的基本规则之一，但我们当初大概故意忽略了——你应该快速原型化并尽快获得反馈，以确保方向正确。即使在早期，Snapchat 之前叫 Picaboo，更侧重于消失消息。我们很快发现人们对这个不感兴趣。他们想用图片交流，用图片说话。所以当我们把应用命名为 Snapchat 时，我们解释说它比通过短信发送照片快 10 倍。人们说：“哦，我想要那个，那是我每天都会用的东西。”所以，在早期通过一个只花了几个月创建的应用版本获得反馈，真的非常有趣。

transcript.secZh: 快速客户反馈的重要性

turns.en: I think this is a really interesting point that a lot of founders don't realize that even companies like yours, they start with an initial hypothesis which is nearly always wrong. But kind of when you hear these stories, you hear like had an idea in my basement and then pursued it and then it became a billion dollar business. But there's something in the like humility and the realization that your initial idea might be wildly off and that your job isn't to like be right, it is to be successful and they're like two different things, right?
turns.zh: 我认为这是一个非常有趣的观点，很多创始人没有意识到，即使是像你这样的公司，也是从一个几乎总是错误的初始假设开始的。但当你听到这些故事时，你听到的是“我在地下室有了一个想法，然后去追求它，最后它变成了一个价值十亿美元的业务”。但其中有一种谦逊和认识，即你最初的想法可能大错特错，你的工作不是要正确，而是要成功，这是两件不同的事，对吧？

turns.en: I totally agree. And I think the challenge—someone, I think this was like one of those Vanity Fair parties a million years ago. The souvenir was a lighter and on one side it's like "The director is always right" and on the other side it's like "The customer is never wrong." And I think that's like always the interesting challenge with a business: that you have to stay true to your vision, the reason why you're building a product, your philosophy, but at the same time, your customers are the people you serve and ultimately how they feel about your product is right, whether or not you agree with it.
turns.zh: 我完全同意。而且我认为这个挑战——我记得很久以前在一次《名利场》派对上，纪念品是一个打火机，一面写着“导演永远是对的”，另一面写着“顾客永远不会错”。我认为这始终是商业中有趣的挑战：你必须忠于自己的愿景、构建产品的原因和理念，但与此同时，你的客户是你服务的人，最终他们对产品的感受就是对的，无论你是否同意。

transcript.sec: Transition from Future Freshman to Snapchat

turns.en: So, how did you get past Future Freshman to your next business? What was the journey from there? So, you'd met your co-founder at that point, Bobby. And how did you then move over to the idea of Snapchat?
turns.zh: 那么，你是如何从 Future Freshman 过渡到下一个业务的？从那里开始的旅程是怎样的？那时你已经遇到了你的联合创始人 Bobby。你是如何转向 Snapchat 这个想法的？

turns.en: I think one of the things that was really helpful is that I did a semester abroad in Cape Town. And I think taking a step back and being there sort of gave me perspective about what we were working on. I was still working on Future Freshman while I was there, but it really gave me more perspective. And I think I just realized this is going to be really hard and I don't really love what we're doing. We got to find something else.
turns.zh: 我认为非常有帮助的一件事是我在开普敦做了一个学期的海外学习。退一步并在那里给了我一些关于我们正在做的事情的视角。我在那里时仍在做 Future Freshman，但它确实给了我更多视角。我想我只是意识到这将会非常困难，而且我并不真正热爱我们正在做的事情。我们必须找到别的东西。

turns.en: Was that more than anything a feeling you had? Like a feeling of just I'm not enjoying doing this every day, opening my emails, thinking about this problem.
turns.zh: 那更多是一种感觉吗？就像一种“我不喜欢每天做这件事、打开邮件、思考这个问题”的感觉？

turns.en: Yeah. I think it's so important to listen to those feelings. Yeah. We were very good at not listening to them. I think because parents and other pressures, right, to continue doing something.
turns.zh: 是的。我认为倾听这些感受非常重要。是的。我们非常擅长不倾听它们。我想是因为父母和其他压力，对吧，让你继续做某件事。

turns.en: So then how did you get from there, from being in Cape Town, to the idea for Snapchat?
turns.zh: 那么你是如何从开普敦那里，到 Snapchat 这个想法的？

turns.en: Well, I came back from Cape Town. I moved into a dorm at Stanford. One of my buddies who had been Reggie, one of my friends who had been living in our fraternity before, was also in that same dorm. So we were hanging out and one day he was like, "Man, I wish I could send a disappearing photo." I was like, "That's a super interesting idea." And we looked it up. There were a couple other apps that were doing some similar stuff at the time, but they were very security focused. They weren't really focused as much on fun. So that's a super interesting idea. And you could see really quickly that it was simple enough that we could build it and get feedback really quickly. And I think there were a couple important design choices that we made at the time. One was opening to the camera. We really wanted to be the fastest way to share a moment. And at the time, the iPhone had like a shutter animation. So you would tap the camera to open it and it would take forever to open up the camera, super slow. Oh, and there was a big toggle. You had to choose between the camera and video, right? So there's all this friction in using the camera. So we decided we're going to open to the camera.
turns.zh: 嗯，我从开普敦回来，搬进了斯坦福的一个宿舍。我有个朋友 Reggie，之前住在我们兄弟会，也住同一个宿舍。我们在一起玩，有一天他说：“哥们，我真希望能发一张会消失的照片。”我说：“这是个超级有趣的想法。”我们查了一下，当时有几个其他应用在做类似的事情，但它们非常注重安全，不太注重乐趣。所以那是个超级有趣的想法。而且你很快就能看出它足够简单，我们可以快速构建并快速获得反馈。我认为当时我们做了几个重要的设计选择。一个是打开直接进入相机。我们真的想成为分享瞬间最快的方式。当时，iPhone 有一个快门动画，你点击相机打开它，打开相机要花很长时间，超级慢。哦，还有一个大开关，你必须在相机和视频之间选择，对吧？所以使用相机有很多摩擦。所以我们决定直接打开相机。

transcript.secZh: 阅后即焚与截屏检测的起源

transcript.sec: The origin of disappearing photos and screenshot detection

turns.en: We're going to get rid of that animation and you're going to be able to go straight into capturing what's happening in front of you before the moment disappears. So, that I think that was a really important choice that we made. And then of course the choice to let people choose how long they wanted to let someone see their snap, but with the caveat that you could always take a screenshot. And that was probably one of the most important pieces of feedback we got in the initial day. So we built the prototype of the app. I took it to my design class. Here's this new app. It's called Pikaboo. You can set a photo that disappears. You know, this is really different than social media. Social media is all about permanence and you're trying to look popular and collect all these likes and comments and pretty pictures. You know, that's the 1% of moments in your life. And here's Snapchat. This is or Peek-a-boo at the time. Here's Peek-a-boo. This is for the other 99%. Right? All the other moments that you might be embarrassed to post to all your friends, but that you want to share with your best friend or, you know, your family. Everyone's like, "This is never going to work because you can always take a screenshot. This makes no sense." So, it doesn't go away. You can take a screenshot. And I think one of the big inventions and that's why I think it's so important to get this feedback. One of the big inventions that we made later that summer when Bobby and I were working out at my dad's house, we invented a way to detect if someone had taken a screenshot. And so we would send a little notification back that said, "Hey, you know, your friend took a screenshot." And I think that was part of what made the service fun that, you know, you could set how long it would appear for your friend, but if they wanted to save it, they could take a screenshot, but you would know that they saved it. And I think that was one of sort of the early feedback loops of the product that helped make people feel comfortable using it for picture messaging.
turns.zh: 我们决定去掉那个动画，让你能直接捕捉眼前正在发生的事，在瞬间消失之前。所以，我认为这是我们做出的一个非常重要的选择。当然，还有那个选择：让人们自己决定对方能看到自己的快照多久，但前提是对方随时可以截图。这可能是我们在最初那天收到的最重要的反馈之一。我们构建了应用的原型，我把它带到了我的设计课上。这是一个新应用，叫 Pikaboo。你可以设置一张会消失的照片。你知道，这和社交媒体非常不同。社交媒体关乎永久性，你试图看起来受欢迎，收集点赞、评论和漂亮的照片。那是你生活中 1% 的瞬间。而这是 Snapchat，或者说当时的 Peek-a-boo。这是 Peek-a-boo，它是为另外 99% 的瞬间准备的。对吧？所有那些你不好意思发给你所有朋友，但想和你的好朋友或家人分享的瞬间。每个人都觉得：“这永远行不通，因为对方总是可以截图。这毫无意义。”所以，它不会消失。你可以截图。我认为其中一个重大发明——这也是为什么我觉得获得这些反馈如此重要——那年夏天晚些时候，当 Bobby 和我在我爸爸家工作时，我们发明了一种检测是否有人截图的方法。然后我们会发回一个小通知，说：“嘿，你的朋友截了图。”我认为这正是让这项服务有趣的部分原因：你可以设置照片为你的朋友显示多久，但如果他们想保存，他们可以截图，但你会知道他们保存了。我认为这是产品早期反馈循环的一部分，帮助人们在使用它进行图片消息传递时感到舒适。

turns.en: How long was that journey between you having that conversation with Reggie about wanting photos to disappear and the moment when you knew Snapchat was going to be a big deal? How long is that gap?
turns.zh: 从你和 Reggie 那次关于让照片消失的对话，到你意识到 Snapchat 会成为一个大事件的那一刻，这段旅程有多长？这个时间差是多久？

turns.en: I would say it took until maybe certainly the following school year for me. So over that summer, Bobby and I went to my dad's house, worked a lot on the service, renamed it Snapchat, got a lot of feedback when we were using it with our friends, everyone wanted like, "Hey, can I add a caption? Can I draw on it?" Because in the original version, it was just a photo. But because people were using it to communicate, we needed to add things like captions and drawing. So, I think Snapchat launched in the app store about September of 2011. And it probably wasn't until late that fall, maybe even into the beginning of 2012 that I remember sitting in the back of my classroom. And we had a snap counter that would count the total number of snaps ever sent. In the early days it was like hundreds or a thousand. I'd refresh the page and the number wouldn't change. But by the beginning of 2012, every time I refreshed the snap counter page, the number would go up and it would jump by one or two or 10. So it was clear that people were using the service and communicating. And that's when I was like, "Ah, this is fun. Not only are we loving it with our friends, but there are more people using it too."
turns.zh: 我想大概要到下一个学年吧。那年夏天，Bobby 和我去了我爸爸家，在这项服务上做了很多工作，把它改名为 Snapchat，在和朋友们一起使用时得到了很多反馈。每个人都想要：“嘿，我能加个标题吗？我能画点东西吗？”因为在最初版本里，它只是一张照片。但由于人们用它来交流，我们需要添加标题和绘画等功能。所以，Snapchat 大概在 2011 年 9 月上线了应用商店。可能直到那年秋天晚些时候，甚至到 2012 年初，我记得我坐在教室后面。我们有一个快照计数器，统计所有发送过的快照总数。早期只有几百或一千。我刷新页面，数字不会变。但到了 2012 年初，每次我刷新快照计数器页面，数字都会增加，一次跳一两个或十个。所以很明显，人们在使用这项服务并交流。那时我就想：“啊，这很有趣。不仅我们和朋友们喜欢它，还有更多人在使用它。”

turns.en: One of the things you said there is that you were using people around you, your friends, to give you feedback on what features you should add next. You said people wanted to write on and they wanted to add captions. As a founder, that must be quite hard because you're getting lots of feedback to change lots of things all the time. How do you know what to filter as good feedback that you'll implement versus a distraction or bad feedback? Is there a framework at all that you have had to deploy?
turns.zh: 你刚才提到，你利用身边的人——你的朋友——来给你反馈，告诉你下一步应该添加什么功能。你说人们想在上面写字，想添加标题。作为创始人，这一定很难，因为你总是收到大量反馈，要求改变很多事情。你怎么知道哪些是好的反馈、应该实施，哪些是干扰或坏反馈？你是否有一个必须使用的框架？

turns.en: So, I think all feedback is good feedback. All feedback is valuable. I think what you do with it is what matters. So, for example, let's take the use of the caption tool for example. We could have added a super clunky caption tool that took forever to use that was like social media where you add the caption at the bottom of the photo and tap it and hashtag stuff. The way that we decided to implement captions to make it easier for people to communicate is all you have to do is tap on the photo right after you took it. So, you'd snap take the photo, tap, the keyboard would pop up. There was a little caption bar that still allowed you to see the photo behind it instead of it being sort of attached to the photo below it. It was right on top of the photo. And then as soon as you hit enter, you could jump to the page where you select which friends you wanted to send it to. So I think what was more important than hearing feedback of, "Hey, I want a way to add a caption or express more in the snap," the way that we implemented that feedback and designed something really fast and easy to use is why that black bar caption is now synonymous with Snapchat and is well known around the world.
turns.zh: 所以，我认为所有反馈都是好反馈。所有反馈都有价值。我认为关键是你如何处理它。例如，以标题工具的使用为例。我们本可以添加一个非常笨拙的标题工具，使用起来很慢，就像社交媒体那样，你在照片底部添加标题，点击它，加标签之类的。我们决定实现标题的方式是让人们更容易交流：你只需在拍照后点击照片。所以，你拍下照片，点击，键盘就会弹出。有一个小标题栏，它仍然让你能看到背后的照片，而不是像附在照片下面那样。它就在照片上方。然后一旦你按回车，你就可以跳到选择发送给哪些朋友的页面。所以，我认为比听到“嘿，我想要一种方式在快照中添加标题或表达更多”的反馈更重要的是，我们实现这个反馈并设计出快速易用的东西，这就是为什么那个黑色标题栏现在成了 Snapchat 的代名词，并且闻名世界。

turns.en: So it was a year from the idea to the day when you raised capital for the first time roughly. Talk me through that. So how much did you raise about raising the capital and what was the business like at that time in terms of users and downloads?
turns.zh: 所以从想法到第一次融资，大概是一年时间。跟我讲讲这个过程。你融了多少钱？当时业务在用户和下载量方面是什么情况？

turns.en: Yeah, I don't remember the exact user statistics, but what was really helpful is that we had about a year of data. So, if you remember back then there were a lot of apps that were sort of like a flash in the pan, like they would get popular really quickly and then sort of fade away. And so, venture investors would kind of jump into these apps and then the apps would get really popular and then kind of fade. And so when we were raising money, one of the things that really helped us is we had a year's worth of data to basically show, hey, when people start using this product to talk to their friends, they keep doing it because it's really fun and it's better than text message based communication. Visual communication is way more fun, more powerful, more expressive than text-based communication. And people use it consistently once they learn how. And that was really important to the investors who were worried is this just another flash in the pan type service.
turns.zh: 是的，我不记得确切的用户统计数据，但非常有帮助的是我们有一年的数据。如果你回想一下，当时有很多应用像昙花一现，它们会很快流行起来，然后又逐渐消失。风险投资者会跳进这些应用，然后应用变得非常流行，然后又淡出。所以当我们融资时，对我们帮助很大的一件事是我们有一年的数据，基本上可以展示：嘿，当人们开始使用这个产品与朋友交流时，他们会一直用下去，因为它真的很有趣，而且比基于文本的消息交流更好。视觉交流比文本交流更有趣、更强大、更具表现力。一旦人们学会如何使用，他们就会持续使用。这对那些担心这只是另一个昙花一现的服务的投资者来说非常重要。

turns.en: Um, so, so we really just led I think we had like three maybe five slides of just the data. Um, do you remember feedback you got from investors at that early stage? I think this is important because all founders are going to get the email that tells them that they're not on to something.
turns.zh: 嗯，所以我们当时真的只是用大概三到五页幻灯片来展示数据。你还记得早期从投资者那里得到的反馈吗？我觉得这很重要，因为所有创始人都会收到那种邮件，告诉他们自己做的事情没什么前途。

turns.en: Yeah, I think the biggest piece of feedback was just like, hey, this seems like something that these really big powerful tech companies are just going to copy and um, you know, it's they're really tough to compete with. So, you know, we we're not really sure we want to invest in something that's going up against these really really big powerful tech companies.
turns.zh: 是的，我觉得最大的反馈就是：嘿，这看起来像是那些非常强大的大型科技公司会直接抄袭的东西，而且你知道，跟它们竞争非常困难。所以我们不太确定是否要投资一个跟这些巨头对抗的项目。

turns.en: I mean, there's some wisdom in that. Certainly a lot of foresight in that one. Yeah. Because the odds anyway of building a a social networking app are extremely low. It's we were saying before we started recording that you've got to be almost like delusional to think that you can.
turns.zh: 我觉得这话有一定道理，确实很有先见之明。因为无论如何，打造一个社交网络应用的成功概率极低。我们开始录制前也说过，你得几乎有点妄想才能相信自己能做到。

turns.en: I think at that time too, you know, Snapchat came last after Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, like you name it. All of those services had come first. And so I think the idea that like, you know, and that was still at the time when people believed that network effects meant that you couldn't compete, right? Whoever has the biggest biggest network is going to win. No one else is going to be able to compete. And so I think there was that concern that, you know, oh, if these other competitors are much bigger and they have network effects, how are you ever going to grow uh grow and and compete? So that was a big piece of feedback. And then I think there was just a whole other group of people who didn't really understand what the service was um and so weren't that interested.
turns.zh: 我觉得那时候也是，Snapchat 是在 Facebook、Twitter、Instagram 等等之后才出现的，所有这些服务都更早。当时人们还相信网络效应意味着你无法竞争，对吧？谁的网络最大谁就赢，别人都没法竞争。所以就有这种担忧：如果其他竞争对手大得多，还有网络效应，你怎么增长和竞争？这是一个很大的反馈。然后还有另一群人，他们根本不理解这个服务是什么，所以也不太感兴趣。

turns.en: But Jeremy at Lightseed reached out. He had his profile picture was a photo of him with Obama. Uh and I was like, "Oh, okay. Must be like a real you." Now with AI, you never know. But but back then I was like, "Okay, must be legit." So we met up with them and and one of his partners that uh I guess his daughter used Snapchat and loves Snapchat and so they understood the service and what it was about and how she was using with her friends and so they ended up investing $485,000 at a $4.25 million valuation.
turns.zh: 但 Lightseed 的 Jeremy 联系了我们。他的头像是一张他和奥巴马的照片。我当时想：“哦，好吧，这应该是真人。”现在有了 AI，你永远不知道，但那时候我想：“好吧，应该是真的。”所以我们见了他们，他的一位合伙人——他女儿用 Snapchat 而且很喜欢——所以他们理解这个服务是什么，以及她怎么和朋友用，最后他们投了 48.5 万美元，估值 425 万。

turns.en: What a deal in hindsight. How many users did you have at the time when you raised that capital?
turns.zh: 事后看来真是笔好交易。当时你们有多少用户？

turns.en: Uh I would have guess about a h 100,000 or something like that. And the valuation was $4 million roughly. 4.25.
turns.zh: 我猜大概 10 万左右吧。估值大约是 400 万，425 万。

turns.en: In that first year up until the point that you raised that money, did you ever doubt that Snapchat was going to work? And I guess to understand the question a bit more, you almost have to add a goal or ambition to it. So, I'm presuming you wanted it to be and thought it could be a company. Did you ever doubt that it would be? Was there anything that ever happened in that first year?
turns.zh: 在第一年，直到你筹到那笔钱之前，你有没有怀疑过 Snapchat 会成功？为了更好理解这个问题，你几乎得加上一个目标或野心。所以我假设你希望它成为一家公司，也觉得它可以。你怀疑过吗？第一年发生过什么吗？

turns.en: There was one moment where we accidentally took down the Snapchat infrastructure for three days. So the service stopped working entirely for three days. Actually it something broke and it took us three days to fix it. And we were like we're we're done. I mean what are we going to do? You know uh the service has been down for three days. It's a messaging service you know. Uh so people haven't been able to talk to their friends like is anyone going to use it? Um, and when we turned it back on, people just started using it again. And that gave us a lot more conviction and that, you know, we had a product that people really just loved using.
turns.zh: 有一次我们意外导致 Snapchat 基础设施宕机了三天，服务完全停了三天。其实是出了点故障，我们花了三天才修好。当时我们想：完了。我们该怎么办？服务已经宕了三天，这是个通讯服务，人们没法跟朋友聊天了，还会有人用吗？但当我们重新上线后，人们又开始用了。这给了我们更大的信心，让我们知道我们有一个人们真正喜欢用的产品。

turns.en: How and why was it growing? Was it a marketing campaign or was it organic?
turns.zh: 它是怎么增长的？是营销活动还是自然增长？

turns.en: The only thing that we ever saw work was, you know, friends using it with friends, telling their friends about it and wanting to, you know, learn how to use it because communicating with photos was a new thing. I mean people were hadn't been talking with pictures before and even the way people thought about photos it was like a photo is for saving a precious moment right like that's or like a family photo like that's really how people were thinking about photos at the time you weren't able like that was just coming out of the digital camera like plug it into your computer and upload the photos era. So there was this massive, I think, behavioral change of people realizing like, wow, like no, a picture is worth a thousand words. And now that I can take it instantly on my phone and and send it with my friend in, you know, uh, a couple hundred milliseconds, like we can talk with pictures and instead of just use pictures to save memories.
turns.zh: 我们唯一看到有效的方式就是朋友之间互相使用，告诉朋友，并想学习怎么用，因为用照片交流是件新鲜事。以前人们不用图片聊天，甚至对照片的看法也是：照片是用来保存珍贵时刻的，或者家庭照之类的。当时刚走出数码相机时代，要插到电脑上上传照片。所以我认为这是一个巨大的行为转变：人们意识到，哇，一张图胜过千言万语。现在我可以瞬间在手机上拍下来，几百毫秒内发给朋友，我们可以用图片聊天，而不仅仅是用图片保存记忆。

turns.en: I don't think we really remember that. Isn't that crazy that we don't remember? It wasn't that long ago. I know, but we just don't remember. Like, as you were saying, I was like, Yeah, you couldn't like send a photo to your friend to talk. Yeah. I was like I was trying to think of the app that I could have used back then to do that and there just like isn't one. Yeah. But now I'm dating myself, you know. It's terrible. No, that's crazy. We We forget that because it's so common place now on every app. You You can And I guess they they ultimately got that from you. Copied that from you.
turns.zh: 我觉得我们真的不记得了。我们居然不记得，是不是很疯狂？其实也没过多久。我知道，但我们就是忘了。就像你说的，我心想：对，你当时没法发张照片给朋友聊天。我试着想当时有什么应用能做到，结果一个都没有。但现在我暴露年龄了，真糟糕。不，这太疯狂了。我们忘了，因为现在每个应用都这么普遍了。我想他们最终是从你这里学到的，抄袭了你。

turns.en: I've got this photo um from the early days. Do you remember this photo? Oh, this is awesome. This picture is great. What is this picture? Where are you? What are you doing? Who is that?
turns.zh: 我有一张早期的照片。你记得这张照片吗？哦，太棒了。这张照片真棒。这是什么照片？你在哪里？在做什么？那是谁？

turns.en: Well, this was actually our first office, which was great. It was called the Blue House in Venice, 523 Oceanfront Walk. And actually, you know, things sort of uh had had reached a breaking point at my dad's house. I think there were seven or eight of us living there. You know, one one night, his girlfriend at the time, now wife, came in and one of, you know, one of our teammates was like sleeping on the couch with a blanket she had bought him for Christmas and she was just like, I think there's like we've had enough here. So, we we had to get an office uh and and move out. And we were going to dinner in Venice and we walked past this old blue house and it had a four lease sign on it. We were like, "Wow, this would be wild. We could have an office on the beach on the Venice boardwalk." Um, let's let's call them. And we called them. They wanted a crazy amount per square foot in rent. We couldn't afford at the time, but we ultimately waited uh a bit longer and and were able to to negotiate that down quite a bit and moved in to the Blue House. And I think the best thing about the Blue House was that the the Venice Boardwalk is one of the most popular destination tourist destinations in the in California, maybe even in the world. I mean, it's more than 10 million people a year come to the Venice Boardwalk. And we had a big ghost logo, our big app icon outside and all day long people would come up and talk to us about the app or give us feedback or need help with their account. And so we were just immersed in people from all over the world who were using Snapchat and wanted to come talk to us about it. ult ultimately became like a little too much.
turns.zh: 这其实是我们的第一个办公室，很棒。它叫威尼斯蓝屋，地址是 Oceanfront Walk 523 号。实际上，在我爸家的情况已经到了临界点。我记得有七八个人住在那儿。有一天晚上，他当时的女友、现在的妻子进来，看到我们一个队友睡在沙发上，盖着她买给他当圣诞礼物的毯子，她就说：我觉得我们受够了。所以我们得找个办公室搬出去。我们去威尼斯吃晚饭时路过一栋旧蓝屋，上面挂着出租牌子。我们想：哇，这太酷了，我们可以在威尼斯海滨步道上的海滩边有个办公室。我们打了电话，他们每平方英尺要价高得离谱，我们当时付不起，但后来等了一段时间，把租金谈下来不少，就搬进了蓝屋。我觉得蓝屋最好的地方是，威尼斯海滨步道是加州甚至世界上最受欢迎的旅游目的地之一，每年有超过 1000 万人来。我们在外面挂了一个大大的幽灵标志——我们的应用图标，整天都有人过来跟我们聊应用、给反馈、或者需要账户帮助。所以我们完全沉浸在来自世界各地的 Snapchat 用户中，他们想跟我们聊。最后有点过头了。

transcript.sec: Early days in the Blue House

turns.en: But in the beginning, it was just so amazing to be right there on the boardwalk with so many people. And so how many people could fit into the Blue House?
turns.zh: 但一开始，就在那条海滨步道上和那么多人在一起，真是太棒了。那么蓝房子能容纳多少人？

turns.en: I think at max capacity it was 20 something. I think we were 20-some people by the time we moved out. Maybe 30. It was pretty cramped. I mean, I was playing footsie with people under the table. We were pretty smooshed.
turns.zh: 我觉得最大容量是 20 多人。搬走的时候大概有 20 多人，可能 30 人。相当挤，我都在桌子底下和人碰脚了。我们挤得很紧。

turns.en: And how many users did you have at that time for people to be coming up on the boardwalk and having conversations? You must have been pretty popular at that point. It must have been millions, I would guess.
turns.zh: 那时候你们有多少用户，才会有人走上步道来聊天？那时候你们肯定很受欢迎。我猜得有几百万人了吧。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: And you'd dropped out of university by that point, obviously. Tell me about that decision, because that's not... I know the app's growing and everything, but to drop out of a prestigious university can't be a super easy decision.
turns.zh: 显然那时候你已经从大学辍学了。跟我讲讲那个决定吧，因为我知道应用在增长，但要从一所名校辍学，肯定不是个轻松的决定。

turns.en: I really felt I had no choice. I didn't have enough credits to graduate. I mean, I was doing the product design program, the engineering program. I had a lot more classes that I had to finish. And ultimately we'd raised $485,000 from investors, and I was spending all day trying to pass these... I was taking I think 20 credits at the time, and trying to work on our business. I just couldn't do both at the same time. So I was like, hopefully one day I'll be able to go back. I actually did end up going back and got my degree in 2018, which was awesome. But I just couldn't do both at the same time.
turns.zh: 我真的觉得别无选择。我的学分不够毕业。我读的是产品设计和工程专业，还有很多课要完成。而且我们刚从投资者那里融了 48.5 万美元，我整天都在忙着通过那些课——当时我大概修了 20 个学分——同时还要打理公司。我实在没法同时做两件事。所以我想，希望有一天能回去。后来我确实回去了，2018 年拿到了学位，这很棒。但我就是没法同时兼顾。

turns.en: Why did you go back and get your degree?
turns.zh: 你为什么回去拿学位？

turns.en: I really did not want to have that debate with our kids where they're like, 'But dad, you dropped out. You don't have a degree. Why do I need one?' I think colleges can be really valuable. It's not for everybody, but it made a huge impact in my life. So I wanted to be able to show how important that is to our kids.
turns.zh: 我真的不想以后跟孩子们争论，他们说：‘但是爸爸，你辍学了，你没有学位。为什么我需要？’我认为大学可以非常有价值，不是对每个人都适用，但它对我的人生产生了巨大影响。所以我想能够向孩子们展示这有多重要。

transcript.sec: Advice for kids in the age of AI

turns.en: I was thinking earlier when we were talking about college, university. The world was different when you went to university. And you've got these four boys now, the oldest I think is 14 years old. If he wants to be like dad, say he wanted to follow in your footsteps, is there anything else based on how the world is currently that you'd be advising him to learn before the age of 21? Are there any topics or degrees that you would be pushing him towards now if he wanted to be like dad, if that was his decision?
turns.zh: 我之前在想，我们刚才聊到大学。你上大学的时候世界不一样了。现在你有四个儿子，最大的好像 14 岁。如果他想像爸爸一样，比如说他想追随你的脚步，根据现在的世界，你会建议他在 21 岁之前学些什么吗？如果他想像你一样，你会推荐他学什么专业或拿什么学位？

turns.en: I think one of the most important things today is really nurturing creativity. I mean, I think creativity is really the X factor, certainly in the age of AI, right? And so, I think nurturing creativity, finding ways to develop those skills. For example, Flynn who's 14, he loves drawing. He's unbelievably talented at drawing. And I think sometimes he's like, 'Well, I don't know if there's a career in drawing,' but I think sometimes he doesn't see that drawing is just the way that he's expressing creativity. Drawing is the beginning of that journey of exercising those muscles in your brain that allow you to visualize something that other people don't see. And that's one tool, one skill he can use to express what's in his brain. But I think exercising that muscle, that creativity is so important. I think creativity is just becoming more and more rare ultimately because so much of our society is oriented around things we can measure. Creativity is so hard to measure, and so I think it can be really tough to find the dedication to invest in developing creativity when it's uncertain what the outcome is. But that's really what I would encourage him or so many people to do. We're all born creative. We're all born with this ability to express ourselves. And it's only over time I think that we stop practicing that ability or we become fearful of expressing ourselves.
turns.zh: 我认为当今最重要的事情之一就是培养创造力。我的意思是，创造力是真正的 X 因素，尤其是在 AI 时代，对吧？所以，培养创造力，找到发展这些技能的方法。比如 Flynn，他 14 岁，非常喜欢画画，而且画得惊人地好。有时候他会想：‘画画能当职业吗？’但他没意识到，画画只是他表达创造力的方式。画画是锻炼大脑肌肉的起点，让你能想象出别人看不到的东西。这是他用来表达脑中想法的一个工具、一项技能。但我认为锻炼那块肌肉、那种创造力非常重要。我觉得创造力正变得越来越稀缺，因为我们的社会很大程度上围绕着可衡量的东西运转。创造力很难衡量，所以在结果不确定的情况下，很难有决心去投资培养创造力。但这正是我会鼓励他或很多人去做的事。我们生来就有创造力，生来就有表达自己的能力。只是随着时间的推移，我们停止了练习，或者变得害怕表达自己。

turns.en: We think through job titles at that age, don't we? We think what's the job title that I should be aiming at. So doctor, lawyer, etc. versus we don't necessarily think as much about collecting useful long-term skills. I'd say I've got my girlfriend's little brother now who's racking his brain trying to pick a job title for the next like 60 years of his life. I'm like, it doesn't work like that, you know? And the world is changing so quickly now as well. It probably makes more sense to try and get some fundamental skills that will translate. Plus, job titles are totally ridiculous anyway. In the early days, we would just make up anyone who joined the team, we would just make up their title. It would have nothing to do with anything.
turns.zh: 在那个年纪，我们都会想职位头衔，对吧？我们会想自己应该瞄准什么头衔，比如医生、律师等等，而不太考虑积累有用的长期技能。我女朋友的弟弟现在正绞尽脑汁想选一个未来 60 年的职位头衔。我就说，不是这样的，你知道吗？而且世界变化这么快。更合理的做法是掌握一些能迁移的基本技能。再说了，职位头衔本来就挺荒谬的。早期的时候，任何加入团队的人，我们都会随便编个头衔，跟实际工作毫无关系。

turns.en: Yeah. Job titles are ridiculous when the team is small, right? Just in general, right? Because I think people anchor to job titles to confer status, right? And I think ultimately, amazing impact, creativity, great ideas come from anywhere, right? And the more that you focus your organization around hierarchy, I think the less you're focusing on the right things, which are how are we making sure great ideas are coming from anywhere, getting surfaced, and being built.
turns.zh: 是啊。团队小的时候职位头衔很荒谬，对吧？一般来说也是这样。因为我觉得人们用头衔来锚定地位。但最终，巨大的影响力、创造力和好主意可以来自任何地方。组织越围绕层级来运作，就越不关注真正重要的事：如何确保好主意从各处涌现、被发掘并得以实现。

turns.en: But hierarchy comes into place when things start to get big and we need to put processes and reporting lines in place. How do you defend against that?
turns.zh: 但事情变大之后，层级就出现了，我们需要流程和汇报线。你怎么抵御这种趋势？

turns.en: Well, I think you're getting at the fundamental problem that all companies end up having. And I think there's a great book called Loonshots which I really love that actually gets at this issue directly. And basically what the author Safi Beall found was that essentially very big companies, once they get a lot larger, they have a lot of customers to serve, they need to build all this organizational infrastructure, and ultimately that comes with hierarchy. But the ones that continue to innovate, that are very successful at innovating consistently over long periods of time, also have very small, very flat teams that don't have any hierarchy at all that are really, really focused on innovating and on trying new things. And ultimately, the companies that are really successful find a way to build a relationship between the huge organization that is supporting all these customers and needs to be operationally rigorous and metrics focused, and this very small group of people who are trying crazy things. And he gives a lot of examples. One of the ways that the United States was able to win World War II, they had these crazy group of scientists that were trying new things like radar and stuff like that at the time.
turns.zh: 嗯，我觉得你触及了所有公司最终都会面临的根本问题。有一本很棒的书叫《Loonshots》，我非常喜欢，它直接讨论了这个问题。作者 Safi Beall 发现，大公司一旦变得非常大，要服务大量客户，就需要建立组织基础设施，这最终会带来层级。但那些持续创新、长期非常成功的公司，也拥有非常小、非常扁平、完全没有层级的团队，他们专注于创新和尝试新事物。最终，真正成功的公司会找到一种方式，在支持客户、需要运营严谨和关注指标的大型组织，与尝试疯狂想法的小团队之间建立关系。他举了很多例子。美国赢得二战的方式之一，就是有一群疯狂的科学家在尝试雷达之类的新东西。

transcript.secZh: 连接小设计团队与大组织

transcript.sec: Bridging small design teams and large organizations

turns.en: But then they were taking those ideas, bringing them to the military, which is a huge, very structured, hierarchical organization and saying, "What do you guys think about this? How can you play with this? What are your ideas? What are your feedback? Take this into battle. Put it on an airplane. See what happens." and then give that feedback back to this very unstructured, flat, small group of inventors and scientists. And by really focusing on the relationship between those parts of the organization, ultimately companies can figure out how to build a strong relationship between the two and then innovate over time. So, how have you done that at Snapchat?
turns.zh: 但他们随后把这些想法带到军方——一个庞大、高度结构化、层级分明的组织——并问：“你们觉得这个怎么样？你们怎么玩？你们的想法是什么？你们的反馈呢？把这个带上战场，放到飞机上，看看会发生什么。”然后再把反馈带回那个非常非结构化、扁平化的小型发明家和科学家团队。通过真正关注组织这两部分之间的关系，公司最终能学会如何建立牢固的联系，并持续创新。那么，你在 Snapchat 是怎么做的？

turns.en: At Snapchat, we have a really small design team. I think it would surprise people. It's nine people really. It is totally flat, so there's no fancy titles. Everyone is a product designer. The way the team works is very focused around making things. That's the entire job. In fact, your very first day when you start, we have design critiques once a week for a couple hours. Your very first day you have to present something. So you have to make something and present it. And what that does, I think is really interesting and powerful, is that ultimately, of course, on your first day when you have no context for what the company's working on, no idea what's going on, how on earth are you supposed to come up with a great idea? I mean it's almost impossible. But you have to show an idea your first day. And so ultimately on your very first day your worst fear has come true: we're sitting there all together and we're looking at an idea that's ultimately not that great. I mean sometimes they're pretty good but ultimately not that great. And that, I think, opens the door to creativity because you've already experienced it. You already failed. The idea wasn't good. And you know what ultimately happens on our own design team is that 99% of ideas are not good but 1% is. And we really abide by that concept that the best way to have a good idea is to have lots of ideas. So the team is just constantly generating an incredible number of ideas and products and features and that sort of thing. And ultimately our job is to try to figure out what the great ones are and then, most importantly, build a strong relationship between this little team that's coming up with all this stuff all the time and our much bigger engineering organization, our bigger product organization who also have all sorts of amazing ideas and are also innovating in their own way, and build a flywheel between the two where we can ultimately make a lot of new products and then consistently make them better.
turns.zh: 在 Snapchat，我们的设计团队非常小，可能出乎大家意料——实际上只有九个人。团队完全扁平化，没有花哨的头衔，每个人都是产品设计师。团队的工作方式完全围绕“做出东西”展开，这就是全部工作。事实上，入职第一天，我们每周会有一次设计评审会，持续几个小时。第一天你就必须展示一些东西，所以你必须做出点什么并呈现出来。这带来的效果非常有趣且强大：当然，入职第一天你对公司正在做什么毫无头绪，完全不知道情况，怎么可能想出好主意？几乎不可能。但你必须第一天就展示一个想法。于是，第一天你最害怕的事就发生了：大家坐在一起，看着一个并不怎么好的想法——有时还不错，但通常不怎么样。我认为这恰恰打开了创造力的大门，因为你已经经历了最糟的情况：你失败了，想法不好。而最终在我们设计团队里，99% 的想法都不好，但 1% 是好的。我们非常信奉一个理念：产生好想法的最佳方式就是拥有大量想法。所以团队不断产生海量的想法、产品和功能。我们的最终任务是找出那些真正优秀的，然后最重要的是，在这个不断产出的小团队与规模大得多的工程组织和产品组织之间建立牢固的关系——他们也有各种精彩想法，也在以各自的方式创新——并在两者之间构建一个飞轮，让我们能持续推出大量新产品并不断改进它们。

turns.en: So many questions there that I'm very curious about. The first one is: do you measure the amount of ideas that that small design team are producing?
turns.zh: 这里有很多让我非常好奇的问题。第一个是：你们会衡量那个小设计团队产出的想法数量吗？

turns.en: No, but I do know when we need more.
turns.zh: 不会，但我知道什么时候需要更多。

turns.en: Okay. Okay. Fine. So, you've got a sort of an intuitive feeling. It's a small team. You can stay close. And then how do you get the bigger organization to cooperate with the smaller design team when the bigger organization have their own incentives, they have their own planning cycles, they have their own egos as all humans do. How do you get them to work together?
turns.zh: 好的，明白了。所以你有一种直觉。团队小，你能保持紧密联系。那么，当更大的组织有自己的激励、自己的规划周期、自己的自尊心——就像所有人一样——你如何让大组织与小设计团队合作？你如何让他们协同工作？

turns.en: For us, the bridge organization is probably our product organization. And they really helped connect the dots between the engineering folks and the design folks. And a lot of this stuff actually mirrors the relationship that Bobby and I had in the very early days where I was more design focused. I had a bit of an engineering background and had taken some CS, but I was more design focused. And Bobby is an unbelievable computer scientist, right? He took math and computer science at school, but he also loved design. And so we had this really powerful relationship where I could talk with him about new ideas and design and he could talk about the engineering constraints. So when, for example, we were inventing this notion that you would tap to take a photo and hold to record a video — at the time that was a really big deal to help people more easily use their cameras. Now every camera on a smartphone is tap for photo, hold for video. But the engineering complexity required to enable that design was something we really talked about and worked through because the way the design and the animations had to work and the way you held your finger really mattered with the way we were flipping between the video feed and capturing a still image. And it was that dialogue that ultimately resulted in a new product and a new thing that people could use. So we mirrored a lot of that and tried to build that relationship across the organization constantly over time where there is a real dialogue and an understanding and an appreciation both for design and engineering, often facilitated by our product organization.
turns.zh: 对我们来说，桥梁组织大概是我们的产品组织。他们真正帮助连接了工程团队和设计团队。很多方面其实反映了早期 Bobby 和我的关系：我更偏设计，虽然有点工程背景，学过一些计算机科学，但主要侧重设计；而 Bobby 是一位了不起的计算机科学家，他在学校学数学和计算机科学，但也热爱设计。所以我们之间有一种非常强大的关系——我可以和他讨论新想法和设计，他可以谈工程约束。比如，当我们发明“点击拍照、长按录像”这个概念时——当时这意义重大，能帮助人们更轻松地使用相机。现在每部智能手机的相机都是点击拍照、长按录像。但实现这个设计所需的工程复杂性，我们真的深入讨论并解决了，因为设计和动画的运作方式，以及你手指的按压方式，都直接影响我们在视频流和静态图像捕捉之间的切换。正是这种对话最终催生了一个新产品，一个人们可以用的新功能。所以我们大量复制了这种模式，并持续在整个组织内建立这种关系——通过真正的对话、理解和相互欣赏，无论是设计还是工程，而产品组织常常在其中起到促进作用。

turns.en: In terms of that small design team, you said you have a critique session once a week. What is a critique session?
turns.zh: 关于那个小设计团队，你说你们每周有一次评审会。评审会是什么？

turns.en: So it's just where we look at work. That's all we do. People just share new work. So for a couple hours we'll just look at all the new ideas and new designs that have come out of the last week from that team. And these can be anything. Really anything. Oftentimes they're oriented around solving a problem. So kind of coming back to that product design philosophy: what problem are we trying to solve? How can we empathize with our community? Okay, our creators are having friction posting to Snapchat. It's confusing the way that they're reading their story replies or that's not working the right way. How can we make that easier? And then we'll just look at a ton of ideas.
turns.zh: 就是大家一起看作品，仅此而已。人们分享新作品。花几个小时看过去一周团队产出的所有新想法和新设计。可以是任何东西，真的任何东西。通常它们围绕解决某个问题。回到产品设计理念：我们试图解决什么问题？如何与我们的社区共情？比如，创作者在 Snapchat 上发帖遇到障碍，他们阅读故事回复的方式令人困惑，或者某些功能不正常。我们如何让它更简单？然后我们就会看一大堆想法。

turns.en: And are these — you said there's eight or nine people. Are they working in isolation within that team or are they working as one team?
turns.zh: 还有，你说有八九个人。他们是各自独立工作，还是作为一个团队协作？

turns.en: They very often are getting feedback from one another. Oftentimes are tackling projects together in small teams, but all come together on a regular basis.
turns.zh: 他们经常互相反馈，也经常以小组形式共同处理项目，但会定期全体集合。

turns.en: I love this point you were making about the key thing that you've discovered is that the game is more ideas, not trying to find a perfect idea. More ideas, more feedback.
turns.zh: 我很喜欢你提到的这个观点：你发现关键是要有更多想法，而不是试图找到一个完美的想法。更多想法，更多反馈。

turns.en: Yeah. More ideas, more feedback. You increase your failure rate, you get more feedback. It does kind of go contrary to what people think when they're building a business. They think the game is to have the perfect idea.
turns.zh: 对。更多想法，更多反馈。你提高失败率，获得更多反馈。这确实与人们创业时的想法相反——他们以为关键是要有完美的想法。

turns.en: But those are all people who've never built a business before.
turns.zh: 但那些都是从未创过业的人。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 没错。

turns.en: Because eventually you learn something, right? You learn that you're not that good at guessing. Yeah. And I think ultimately you have to maximize your rate of learning. I mean that's that's just critical. Maximize your rate of learning.
turns.zh: 因为最终你会学到一些东西，对吧？你会意识到自己并不擅长猜测。是的。而且我认为最终你必须最大化你的学习速度。我的意思是，这至关重要。最大化你的学习速度。

turns.en: Let's go back to those early days. You're in that office. When you think about the people in that photo that were part of the first sort of 20, how important in hindsight is hiring?
turns.zh: 让我们回到早期那些日子。你在那间办公室里。当你想到那张照片里属于最初大约 20 个人的那些人时，事后看来，招聘有多重要？

turns.en: I think it's everything. I think it's everything. And these were really just wonderful people. I mean still, in many cases close friends. And I think interestingly, there was a moment I realized David, Daniel, Bobby, and a couple other of our original engineers, all of them were musicians as well. And it was really interesting this moment because the early folks who were working on the engineering side of Snap were unbelievably creative and unbelievably talented. And it was an interesting aha moment because I think often times people think of the disciplines as separate, like oh there's designers and then over there there's engineers. And I think so much of the magic actually is when those disciplines combine or cross over, or people who really love and appreciate both, especially for a company that's aspiring to be creative absolutely in everything that it's doing.
turns.zh: 我认为招聘就是一切。就是一切。这些人真的非常出色。我的意思是，至今很多情况下仍是亲密的朋友。而且有趣的是，有一个时刻我意识到大卫、丹尼尔、鲍比以及其他几位最初的工程师，他们也都是音乐家。那一刻非常有意思，因为早期在 Snap 从事工程工作的人创意无限、才华横溢。这是一个有趣的顿悟时刻，因为我认为人们常常把学科分开看待，比如设计师在那里，工程师在那里。而我认为，真正的魔力恰恰在于这些学科的结合或交叉，或者那些真正热爱并欣赏两者的人，尤其对于一家渴望在所做的一切中充满创意的公司而言。

turns.en: On this point of hiring, did you make any hiring mistakes in those early days?
turns.zh: 关于招聘这一点，你在早期犯过任何招聘错误吗？

turns.en: Oh absolutely.
turns.zh: 哦，当然。

turns.en: And what were those mistakes? Not necessarily people, but the frameworks were off, or the way that you hired these people? What caused the mistakes?
turns.zh: 那些错误是什么？不一定是人，而是框架出了问题，或者你招聘这些人的方式？是什么导致了这些错误？

turns.en: I think occasionally in the early days we almost overindexed on the wrong types of experience, if that makes sense. So one of the things we really wanted to do was bring in people who were very experienced leaders who had run much bigger teams. That was like, if we want to build a big company, we got to find people who have run big companies and big teams. And so one of the early engineering leaders who joined our team, I think he was coming from working on a team of 300 or something like that at Amazon, to coming to a team of eight at Snapchat. But we were really thinking ahead about how we can hire people who can actually help us scale here and build something really big. And I think that sort of focus on leadership experience and experience leading at scale was really valuable. I think what was oftentimes a bit less valuable in those early days was almost more people who had very specific domain expertise. So there were people who would come into an interview and be like, well I think what you guys should do is add likes because every other platform has likes. So if you just add likes then people will use your service more, and not really coming with the same open-mindedness and curiosity about why is Snapchat doing it differently? Like why don't you have likes and comments? How are you thinking about the service differently, and how could I change and grow and adapt to the way you're thinking about it to help you grow faster? And so I think now one of the things we're always looking for in the interview process is adaptability. It's amazing to have prior experience, but the question is how do you apply that prior experience to a new context and change and adapt the way that you see things, change your perspective, to be able to meet the needs of our business, which is different than other businesses.
turns.zh: 我认为在早期，我们有时会过度看重错误类型的经验，如果这说得通的话。所以我们非常想做的一件事就是引进那些经验丰富的领导者，他们管理过更大的团队。这就像，如果我们想建立一家大公司，就必须找到那些管理过大公司和大团队的人。于是，早期加入我们团队的一位工程负责人，我想他之前在亚马逊管理着一个大约 300 人的团队，然后来到 Snapchat 管理一个 8 人团队。但我们确实在提前思考，如何招聘那些能真正帮助我们在这里扩展并打造出真正庞大事业的人。我认为这种对领导经验和规模化领导经验的关注非常有价值。而在早期，价值往往较低的反而是那些拥有非常具体领域专长的人。有些人来面试时会说，我觉得你们应该添加点赞功能，因为其他每个平台都有点赞。如果你们加了点赞，人们就会更多地使用你们的服务。他们并没有带着同样的开放心态和好奇心去思考：为什么 Snapchat 要与众不同？为什么你们没有点赞和评论？你们对服务的思考方式有何不同？我该如何改变、成长并适应你们的思维方式，以帮助你们更快成长？所以现在，我们在面试过程中始终寻找的一个特质就是适应能力。拥有过往经验很棒，但问题在于你如何将这些经验应用到新的环境中，改变并调整你看待事物的方式，转变你的视角，以满足我们业务的需求——我们的业务与其他业务不同。

turns.en: What are the other factors? If you were to make a perfect Snapchat employee now, what would their personality be? Their psychology, their attributes.
turns.zh: 其他因素是什么？如果你现在要打造一个完美的 Snapchat 员工，他们的个性会是什么样的？他们的心理特质，他们的属性。

turns.en: We have three values and three leadership behaviors. Three values are kind, smart, and creative. That's been the values since the very beginning really, because Bobby and I were just having a conversation like what kind of people do we want to work with? Kind, smart, creative. Like great. But since then, and we can spend some more time talking about this, I think what was really fascinating over time was to learn, and by the way 10 years ago people were not talking about kindness at work. I mean people would be like sorry what? No kind, no kind smart creative, like why kindness? What we found was that the relationship between kindness and creativity is really really important because unless people feel comfortable coming up with crazy ideas, unless they feel comfortable that if they say they have some new idea and it actually isn't that great that they're not going to be laughed at, that they'll be supported, right? Unless you have that sort of supportive culture, it's very hard to be creative. And so we learned over time that actually wow kindness is the essential ingredient if you want to have a creative culture. But anyways, kind, smart, creative. Smart, pretty self-explanatory. And then when it comes to leadership behaviors, there's three leadership behaviors or attributes we look for.
turns.zh: 我们有三个价值观和三个领导力行为。三个价值观是：善良、聪明、有创造力。这从一开始就是我们的价值观，因为鲍比和我当时就在讨论，我们想和什么样的人一起工作？善良、聪明、有创造力。就这样。但自那以后——我们可以多花点时间谈谈这个——我认为随着时间的推移，真正引人入胜的是学到，顺便说一句，10 年前人们在工作场合根本不谈论善良。我的意思是，人们会反应说，抱歉什么？不善良？善良、聪明、有创造力？为什么是善良？我们发现，善良与创造力之间的关系极其重要，因为除非人们觉得提出疯狂的想法很自在，除非他们觉得如果自己说出一个新想法而它其实并不那么好时，不会被人嘲笑，而是会得到支持，对吧？除非你拥有那种支持性的文化，否则很难有创造力。所以我们逐渐认识到，哇，善良实际上是创造力的关键要素，如果你想拥有一种创意文化的话。总之，善良、聪明、有创造力。聪明，不言自明。然后关于领导力行为，我们有三个我们寻找的领导力行为或特质。

turns.en: I just want to pause on that point of kind. Do you make a distinction between someone being nice and being kind? Because in your environment, you also mentioned that you do these critique sessions and you're giving people critical feedback, and if a culture gets a little bit too kind, then isn't that going to inhibit innovation and feedback?
turns.zh: 我想在“善良”这一点上暂停一下。你区分一个人是“nice”（客气）和“kind”（善良）吗？因为在你的环境中，你也提到你们会进行这些批评会议，给人们提供关键反馈，如果文化变得过于“kind”，那难道不会抑制创新和反馈吗？

turns.en: We always differentiate between kind and nice. There are a couple examples that I think help with that. So for one, I think it's really kind to tell somebody that they have something stuck in their teeth. You have something stuck in your teeth, you want to know about it, right? It might make you feel awkward. Certainly as the person pointing it out, it's a little awkward. If you want to just be nice, you pretend nothing is going on and you just say, oh, nice to meet you, whatever. But if you're really being kind and you want to help that person, you say, you know, you got something stuck in your teeth. You got to take care of that. And I think that helps distinguish between niceties and being kind and really wanting to help somebody. I think another great example is if somebody's really struggling at work or they're struggling to grow or they're struggling to perform their duties at Snap, the nice thing to do is maybe just make them feel good about it. Oh, don't worry, I'm sure it'll be okay. The kind thing to do is really help them succeed, right? Say, hey, this isn't working because you're doing X, Y, and Z. Here are some things to do to think about that differently.
turns.zh: 我们总是区分“kind”和“nice”。有几个例子可以帮助说明。首先，我认为告诉别人他们牙缝里有东西是真正的善良。你牙缝里有东西，你想知道，对吧？这可能会让你感到尴尬。当然，作为指出这一点的人，也有点尴尬。如果你只是想表现得客气，你会假装什么都没发生，然后说，哦，很高兴见到你，之类的。但如果你是真的善良，想帮助那个人，你会说，你知道吗，你牙缝里有东西，你得处理一下。我认为这有助于区分客套和真正的善良，以及真心想帮助别人。另一个很好的例子是，如果有人在工作中真的很挣扎，或者他们在成长上遇到困难，或者在 Snap 履行职责时遇到困难，客气的做法可能是让他们感觉好一点，哦，别担心，我相信会没事的。而善良的做法是真正帮助他们成功，对吧？比如，嘿，这行不通是因为你做了 X、Y、Z。这里有一些方法可以让你换个角度思考。

turns.en: Leadership values. You said there's three leadership values. Okay, there's three of them. The first one is T-shaped leadership. So, we talk a lot about T-shaped leadership. What we mean by that is that you have a real depth of experience, a depth of expertise in a given area and then a real breadth of understanding of the business overall and an ability to connect with lots of different types of people who think different ways because you need to be able to connect your expertise to all the different areas of our business to really drive impact as a leader.
