I mean, I think that's one of like almost the hallmarks of running a business today is it's basically impossible to do anything interesting without a team, right? The way that the world works today is very complicated and it's really important that you have folks who have deep expertise but then they have to apply it to all these other cross-functional areas you know so they have to have a familiarity with it and an ability to relate to people with different viewpoints or other areas of expertise.
turns.zh: 领导力价值观。你说过有三个领导力价值观。好的，有三个。第一个是 T 型领导力。我们经常谈论 T 型领导力。我们的意思是，你在某个领域有深厚的经验和专长，同时对整个业务有广泛的理解，并且有能力与不同类型、不同思维方式的人建立联系，因为你需要将你的专长与业务的所有不同领域连接起来，才能真正作为领导者产生影响。我认为这几乎是当今经营企业的标志之一——没有团队，基本上不可能做成任何有趣的事情，对吧？当今世界运作的方式非常复杂，拥有深厚专长的人非常重要，但他们必须将专长应用到所有其他跨职能领域，所以他们必须熟悉这些领域，并且有能力与不同观点或不同专长领域的人相处。

turns.en: So, as we proceed with these leadership principles, are you saying that in order to become a leader at Snapchat you need these three things, or are you saying everybody at Snapchat needs these three things? We think everyone is a leader. So, we do apply it broadly. But, of course, I think it's really important as we're thinking about hiring or bringing in a new leader that this is something that we talk to folks about.
turns.zh: 那么，当我们推行这些领导力原则时，你是在说，要成为 Snapchat 的领导者，你需要具备这三样东西，还是说 Snapchat 的每个人都需要这三样东西？我们认为每个人都是领导者。所以我们确实广泛地应用它。但当然，我认为在我们考虑招聘或引进新领导者时，这是我们与人们讨论的重要内容。

turns.en: So, if someone's not quite T-shaped, if they're a little bit I-shaped, is there something they can do to become a bit more T-shaped?
turns.zh: 那么，如果有人不太符合 T 型，有点像 I 型，他们能做些什么来变得更 T 型一点吗？

turns.en: Yeah, that's almost maybe the easier one, right? If you can build on a real depth of expertise by engaging with folks maybe outside of your comfort zone or in different parts of the business and build that curiosity and understanding, that helps develop that breadth of understanding. I think what's harder is if you're a generalist and you don't have that deep skill set or that deep area of expertise, it's really really hard to bring enough value to the team, right? And I think that's where people get frustrated with the idea of middle management, right? Where it's like, oh, this is just a person who knows a little about a lot but can't really help me solve this problem because they don't really know the details. They don't really understand how to help me grow as an individual or solve this tough technical problem. And so I think that's why that area of expertise is so important because it's so hard to inspire people that you're working with if you don't know a lot about the area that you're working in.
turns.zh: 是的，这可能是比较容易的一个，对吧？如果你能在深厚专长的基础上，与舒适区之外或业务不同领域的人接触，培养好奇心和理解力，这有助于发展那种广泛的理解。我认为更难的是，如果你是一个通才，没有那种深厚的技能或专长领域，那么很难为团队带来足够的价值，对吧？我认为这就是人们对中层管理概念感到沮丧的地方，对吧？就像，哦，这个人只是对很多事情略知一二，但并不能真正帮我解决这个问题，因为他们不了解细节。他们并不真正理解如何帮助我成长或解决这个棘手的技术问题。所以我认为这就是专长领域如此重要的原因，因为如果你对你工作的领域了解不多，就很难激励与你共事的人。

turns.en: And do you need to be a T-shaped leader at Snapchat now and when there was 10 of you in the bedroom or in your dad's house? Has it always been important or is that a function of being bigger?
turns.zh: 那么，现在在 Snapchat，以及当你们只有十个人在卧室或你父亲家里时，都需要成为 T 型领导者吗？它一直都很重要，还是规模变大后的产物？

turns.en: That's a great question. I wish we had been more thoughtful about the leadership values and characteristics we were looking for back then. I think, when you're working on a team of 10 or a team of 20, you're not thinking as much about what leadership characteristics are really important to us. It's more about like how do we survive tomorrow? But I think over time as we learned what leaders were really successful at Snap, we were able to look at those attributes and say, okay, these are the leaders who can really succeed here and drive a lot of value for our business.
turns.zh: 这是个好问题。我希望我们当时能更仔细地思考我们寻找的领导力价值观和特质。我认为，当你在一个 10 人或 20 人的团队中工作时，你不会过多考虑哪些领导特质对我们真正重要。更多的是关于我们如何生存到明天？但我认为，随着时间的推移，当我们了解到哪些领导者在 Snap 真正成功时，我们能够审视这些特质，然后说，好的，这些是能够在这里真正成功并为我们的业务带来巨大价值的领导者。

turns.en: Before we move on to the second two, if this Evan could have gone back to the Evan that was running a team of 10 and he could have pulled him aside and said, "Listen, here's some advice that you're going to need to know about leadership in building this team. The most critical advice I could give you at this time, and this is for all the entrepreneurs out there that are building as they're laying the foundations of a potentially very big company right now, what would you have whispered in his ear?"
turns.zh: 在我们继续讨论第二和第三点之前，如果现在的 Evan 能回到过去，找到那个管理着十人团队的 Evan，把他拉到一边说：“听着，这里有一些关于领导力和建设团队的建议，你需要知道。我现在能给你的最关键的 advice，这也是给所有正在为潜在的大公司打基础的创业者们的，你会在他耳边低语什么？”

turns.en: I would have said, "Everything's going to be okay." Really? Everything's going to be okay. I think sometimes people are too focused on making the right decision and not as focused on fixing it if they're wrong. And I think what I would have put more emphasis on is just how quickly are you changing your mind when you receive new information? How quickly are you fixing a problem or a mistake if you didn't make the right decision in the first place? And that's the feedback loop that is so mission-critical to building a business in the early days. It has very little to do. Obviously, there's existential decisions, and those can create some big problems for your business. But most decisions are not existential decisions. And the more important thing is to make a decision and then if you're wrong, fix it. And I think it's the when you're wrong, fixing it part that deserves most of the attention and also how you can identify who your great leaders are, who really talented folks on the team are because they're very quick to point out, I don't think we did that right. I think we should take this path, this other path that we maybe hadn't considered the first time. And I think it takes courage to say that in an organization rather than just say, "Oh, we're doing a great job."
turns.zh: 我会说：“一切都会好起来的。”真的吗？一切都会好起来的。我认为有时人们过于关注做出正确的决定，而不太关注如果错了如何修正。我想我会更强调的是，当你收到新信息时，你改变主意的速度有多快？如果你最初没有做出正确的决定，你解决问题的速度有多快？这就是在早期创业中至关重要的反馈循环。这关系不大。显然，有些决定关乎生死存亡，它们可能会给企业带来大问题。但大多数决定并非生死攸关。更重要的是做出决定，然后如果错了，就修正它。我认为，当你错了时，修正的部分最值得关注，以及你如何识别谁是你优秀的领导者，谁是团队中真正有才华的人，因为他们会很快指出，我认为我们做得不对。我认为我们应该走这条路，这条我们第一次可能没有考虑过的路。我认为在组织中说出这样的话需要勇气，而不是只说：“哦，我们做得很好。”

turns.en: Yeah. And when you're back there and you've made a mistake, there's something you've done wrong in hindsight, did you know in your because because one of the things that I think of when I was a first-time founder building a student notice board was I would get feedback and the feedback would be saying you're wrong about this. You need to change. And I think at sometimes there was a part of me that knew, but I was like too scared to act upon it. So I kind of like gaslit myself to just keep going. And I think a lot of founders do that. I know this because they come to me in my portfolio and they say, "Ah, Steve, there's this guy we've hired and he's been there now for a year and he's just not cutting it." I'm like, "Why the are you telling me?" And they're procrastinating avoiding the conversation, but clearly they know. Clearly they know it's not right. It's funny you say that because anytime someone comes to ask me about that type of people advice, like, "What do you think we should do?" You know, do you think that I'm like sounds like you've already made up your mind.
turns.zh: 是的。当你回到过去，你犯了一个错误，事后看来你做错了什么，你是否知道……因为我想起我第一次创业做学生公告板时，我会收到反馈，反馈说你这点错了，你需要改变。有时我内心知道，但我太害怕而不敢行动。所以我有点像自我欺骗，继续走下去。我认为很多创始人都会这样。我知道这一点，因为他们会来找我，在我的投资组合里说：“啊，Steve，我们雇了这个人，他已经干了一年了，就是不行。”我就想：“你为什么告诉我？”他们在拖延，回避谈话，但他们显然知道。显然知道不对。你这么说很有趣，因为每当有人来问我那种关于人的建议，比如“你觉得我们该怎么办？”你知道，我觉得听起来你已经下定决心了。

transcript.sec: Acting on feedback and changing direction

turns.en: So yeah, I think it is really important to act on that feedback, not be afraid to change direction quickly if you realize you made a mistake. But as you point out, it's hard to do, and sometimes it is worth seeing if your bet plays out. You don't want to thrash the team and change your mind all the time. So sometimes it is worth seeing things through a little bit before you change.
turns.zh: 所以，我认为根据反馈采取行动非常重要，如果发现自己犯了错，不要害怕迅速调整方向。但正如你所说，这很难做到，有时值得看看你的赌注是否奏效。你不想让团队反复折腾、不断改变主意。所以，有时候在改变之前，还是值得先坚持一会儿。

turns.en: Is there anything else you would have said to that younger Evan in that advice at your dad's house?
turns.zh: 你还有什么想对那个在你父亲家里、更年轻的自己说的吗？

turns.en: At that point, before we had scaled to thousands of people, I think we could have been much more clear on the culture — the kind, smart, creative piece — and really embedded that in the team prior to scaling. Because one of the biggest challenges we confronted was, as we went from 20 people to 2,000 people, we basically imported all these different cultures from all sorts of different companies. We imported an Amazon contingent — they really love their six-page documents. We imported a Google contingent — they're very focused on consensus-based decision-making. We imported a contingent from Meta as well. And I think we were too slow to be really clear about what our values were and what that looked like in practice, what those behaviors looked like. I think if we had earlier and faster — so when we're evaluating performance, we look at our values: kind, smart, creative. We have specific behaviors attached to that that are actually research-backed. We did a whole study to understand which of those behaviors are really tied to performance and those values. But that gives people a really clear framework for the expectations for how to behave at Snap and our unique culture. There was a moment in time where I felt like we were losing control of our culture, and I wasn't happy with our company and the team. I remember I was complaining to a friend of mine. I was just like, 'Man, I just don't like it. I don't like my job. I don't like what our company's become.' And she just looks at me and says, 'Then fix it.' And I was like, 'Great point.' It had changed and grown so quickly that it was really hard to stay true to our values. But I took that advice to heart and started trying to fix it with our team — getting really clear about the values, getting really clear about the behaviors, holding a higher bar, and saying, 'Hey, if you're not into the kind, smart, creative thing, that's okay. There are other companies with different cultures, but that really matters to us here.'
turns.zh: 在那个时候，在我们扩展到数千人之前，我认为我们本可以更清楚地定义文化——友善、聪明、有创意——并在扩张之前真正将其融入团队。因为我们面临的最大挑战之一是，当我们从 20 人发展到 2,000 人时，我们基本上从各种不同的公司引入了各种不同的文化。我们引入了亚马逊派——他们非常喜欢六页文档。我们引入了谷歌派——他们非常注重共识决策。我们也引入了来自 Meta 的一批人。我认为我们太慢于明确我们的价值观是什么，以及这在实践中、在行为上意味着什么。如果我们更早、更快地做到这一点——比如在评估绩效时，我们看我们的价值观：友善、聪明、有创意。我们附带了具体的行为，这些行为实际上是有研究支持的。我们做了一整项研究来了解哪些行为真正与绩效和这些价值观相关。这给了人们一个非常清晰的框架，让他们知道在 Snap 应该如何表现以及我们独特的文化。有一个时刻，我觉得我们正在失去对文化的控制，我对我们的公司和团队不满意。我记得我在向一个朋友抱怨。我说：‘老兄，我就是不喜欢。我不喜欢我的工作。我不喜欢我们公司变成的样子。’她看着我说：‘那就去修好它。’我说：‘好主意。’公司变化和增长得太快了，很难坚守我们的价值观。但我把那个建议牢记在心，开始和团队一起努力修复——明确价值观，明确行为，提高标准，并说：‘嘿，如果你不喜欢友善、聪明、有创意这套，没关系。还有其他公司有不同文化，但这对我们来说真的很重要。’

turns.en: Do you wish you had made a culture bible in the early days? I'm thinking practically: what should a founder do at that stage with a small team to prevent what happened to you in terms of the culture becoming a bit too pick and mix?
turns.zh: 你希望早期就有一本文化圣经吗？我在想实际层面：创始人在那个阶段、团队还小的时候，应该怎么做才能防止像你那样文化变得有点大杂烩？

turns.en: I think it's less about the culture bible and more about how you apply whatever your values are to your hiring processes, to your promotion processes, to whether or not people still work at the company. We were too slow to embed those values in our performance evaluation. So I think if we had been way faster at just saying, 'Hey, these are our values and what we stand for. This is what it looks like in practice. And if you're not living up to that, this isn't the right home for you.' That would have helped shape the culture a lot faster, because immediately people see, 'Oh wow, if they're serious about their values and they're asking people to leave if they won't live up to them, well then I better get on board with the values or find another culture that fits my personality better.' It just doesn't seem like a priority to founders, culture. I think it's such a priority, but it's hard to understand what it means. I had so many people telling me, 'Really, you got to really focus on the culture, focus on the culture.' Like, what do you mean by culture? It's actually just how people behave. I mean, that's really what we're saying: what is the collection of group behaviors that are acceptable or norms in your company? So I think instead of using this big culture word, which I was hearing a lot but not understanding how it was tactically connecting to our business, when we're talking with founders, we should just be more specific about how people are living the values of their company every day through their behaviors. And that's dictated essentially by the incentives of the organization. Because what you said is you basically introduced incentive structures: you're going to be exited or you're going to be promoted, and getting really real about that and serious. But the tough conversations come where it's like, 'Well, that person's a superstar, but they're not really living our kindness value.' And Bobby, I think, was so great on this. Bobby is like, 'Evan, there's no such thing as a brilliant jerk. If you're really brilliant, how could you possibly be a jerk?' I mean, it just — and you're like, 'Damn, I love that.' So I think this concept that if you're really that smart, how could you possibly be a jerk to people? That really informed our approach to building out our team and gives you that clarity in those moments where you're like, 'Wow, but they're so smart or so talented.' It's like, yeah, but if they're that smart and talented, why can't they just be kind to people?
turns.zh: 我认为与其说是文化圣经，不如说是如何将你的价值观应用到招聘流程、晋升流程以及员工是否继续留在公司。我们太慢于将这些价值观嵌入绩效评估中。所以我认为如果我们更快地说：‘嘿，这些是我们的价值观和我们所代表的东西。这在实践中是这样的。如果你不符合，这里不是你的合适归宿。’那会更快地塑造文化，因为人们立刻会看到：‘哦，哇，如果他们真的重视价值观，并且要求不符合的人离开，那么我最好接受这些价值观，或者找一个更适合我个性的文化。’文化对创始人来说似乎不是优先事项。我认为它非常重要，但很难理解它意味着什么。很多人告诉我：‘你真的要专注于文化，专注于文化。’我说，文化是什么意思？它实际上就是人们的行为方式。我的意思是，这就是我们所说的：你的公司里可接受的群体行为或规范是什么？所以我认为，与其用这个大的文化词汇——我听到很多，但不明白它如何与我们的业务战术性地连接——当我们与创始人交谈时，我们应该更具体地说明人们如何通过日常行为践行公司的价值观。而这基本上是由组织的激励机制决定的。因为你刚才说的是你引入了激励结构：你要么被淘汰，要么被晋升，并且对此非常认真。但艰难的对话来了：‘嗯，那个人是超级明星，但他们并没有真正践行我们的友善价值观。’而 Bobby，我认为在这方面做得很好。Bobby 说：‘Evan，没有所谓的天才混蛋。如果你真的很聪明，你怎么可能是个混蛋？’我的意思是，这——你会说：‘天哪，我喜欢这个。’所以我认为这个概念——如果你真的那么聪明，你怎么可能对人刻薄？——确实指导了我们建设团队的方法，并在那些时刻给你清晰度，比如：‘哇，但他们那么聪明或有才华。’是的，但如果他们那么聪明有才华，为什么不能对人友善呢？

turns.en: What was the worst advice you got in those early years? We talked about some of the good advice and the good advice you'd give now, but was there any really bad advice that you got that seemed to make sense but was terrible advice?
turns.zh: 早期你得到的最糟糕的建议是什么？我们谈了一些好建议和你现在会给出的好建议，但有没有什么看起来很合理但实际上很糟糕的建议？

turns.en: I think a lot of people in the early days told us that we should sell it. I mean, there was one embarrassing moment.
turns.zh: 我认为早期很多人告诉我们，我们应该卖掉公司。我的意思是，有一个尴尬的时刻。

turns.en: I remember I joined a conference call early with some of our lawyers and I don't think they knew I had joined. They were talking about how this thing is basically going to zero. This was in the early days, like it's just a fad. And I'm like, oh hey guys, are you joking? They didn't know I was on the call because I joined a minute or two early. So I think there was a lot of skepticism in the early days and a lot of people who said, hey, sell now while you can. You're competing in a really tough industry with a lot of big players and you don't know if people are going to continue to love this product. But I think what they missed was our vision for the future. They only saw what was in the public. We were working on all sorts of amazing new products that give us conviction in the future and our ability to make products that people really love. But from the outside, when you look at Snapchat, you think, people are just sending photos back and forth. How is this ever going to be a business? How is this ever going to grow for the long term?
turns.zh: 我记得我提前几分钟加入了一个与律师的电话会议，他们不知道我也在线。他们正在讨论这个东西基本上要归零了。那是在早期，他们说这只是一时热潮。我就说，嘿，你们是在开玩笑吗？他们不知道我加入了，因为我早到了一两分钟。所以早期确实有很多怀疑的声音，很多人说，趁现在还能卖就赶紧卖吧。你正在与很多大玩家在一个非常艰难的行业竞争，你不知道人们是否会继续喜欢这个产品。但我认为他们忽略的是我们对未来的愿景。他们只看到了公开的部分。我们当时正在开发各种令人惊叹的新产品，这让我们对未来以及我们打造人们真正喜爱的产品的能力充满信心。但从外部看，当你看到 Snapchat 时，你会觉得人们只是在来回发送照片。这怎么能成为一门生意？怎么能长期增长？

turns.en: But you do end up getting an offer, a very famous offer when you're 23 years old from Mark Zuckerberg at Facebook. That was a fateful day for sure. How does that begin? Is it an email, a phone call, an introduction?
turns.zh: 但你确实在 23 岁时收到了马克·扎克伯格来自 Facebook 的一个非常著名的收购要约。那确实是一个决定性的日子。它是怎么开始的？是邮件、电话，还是有人介绍？

turns.en: I think it was an initial email. I think we met at some point and they were interested in what we were doing. At that time they were working on a competitor called Poke. They were kind of talking with us, hey, we're exploring this space, what do you think? And maybe you want to join Facebook. I think they had just acquired Instagram too, probably like a year earlier or something like that. Our view was that Instagram had been wildly undervalued in that acquisition and ultimately they gave up a massive opportunity. Instagram was sold for a billion, was it?
turns.zh: 我想最初是一封邮件。我们后来见过面，他们对我们的业务感兴趣。当时他们正在开发一个叫 Poke 的竞品。他们跟我们聊，说他们在探索这个领域，问我们怎么看，也许我们想加入 Facebook。我记得他们刚收购了 Instagram，大概是一年前左右。我们的看法是 Instagram 在那笔收购中被严重低估了，最终他们放弃了一个巨大的机会。Instagram 卖了 10 亿美元，是吗？

turns.en: Yeah. A billion. And WhatsApp was 19 billion roughly.
turns.zh: 对，10 亿。WhatsApp 大概是 190 亿。

turns.en: Yeah, I think so.
turns.zh: 嗯，我想是的。

turns.en: You're 23 years old at that point. You've got Facebook, Mark Zuckerberg, offering you a lot of money. I heard that it was $3 billion they offered.
turns.zh: 你当时 23 岁。Facebook 的马克·扎克伯格给你开出了很高的价格。我听说他们出价 30 亿美元。

turns.en: Yeah, we never talked about it publicly, but yeah.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们从未公开讨论过，但确实如此。

turns.en: Is that the number?
turns.zh: 是这个数字吗？

turns.en: That's not technically the number, but it's what's been reported publicly, so we can go with that.
turns.zh: 严格来说不是那个数字，但公开报道是这么说的，我们就按这个说吧。

turns.en: Okay. But did you get an offer?
turns.zh: 好的。但你确实收到了要约？

turns.en: There was a real conversation about what it would look like and to join forces, but ultimately when we talked with our board and our investors, we decided that we'd rather go it alone.
turns.zh: 确实有过关于合作形式的认真讨论，但最终当我们与董事会和投资者沟通后，我们决定还是单干。

turns.en: So I'm trying to understand as a 23-year-old if someone offers me $3 billion for an app that I've started — you said you're probably still at your dad's house or in the blue office at that point. You're in your dad's house and someone's offering you $3 billion for an app. What wisdom do you have that enables you to turn that down?
turns.zh: 所以我很好奇，作为一个 23 岁的年轻人，如果有人出价 30 亿美元收购你创办的应用——你说当时你可能还住在你父亲家里，或者在那个蓝色办公室。你就在你父亲家里，有人出价 30 亿美元买你的应用。你拥有什么智慧让你能够拒绝？

turns.en: I wish I could say it was wisdom. I think it was just that Bobby and I loved what we were doing. We loved what we were working on. We believed in the future of it and ultimately we were able to convince our investors as well that our opportunity was much bigger over time. So I think that's what gave us the confidence in making that decision.
turns.zh: 我希望我能说那是智慧。但我想只是因为鲍比和我热爱我们正在做的事情。我们热爱我们的工作。我们相信它的未来，最终我们也说服了投资者，我们的机会随着时间的推移会大得多。所以我认为这就是我们做出这个决定的信心来源。

turns.en: Did you ever get to meet with Mark or speak to Mark about it?
turns.zh: 你有没有见过马克，或者跟他谈过这件事？

turns.en: Yeah. I've known Mark for a long time.
turns.zh: 见过。我认识马克很久了。

turns.en: Was he keen to buy it?
turns.zh: 他很想买吗？

turns.en: We had some interesting conversations about what it could look like for us to work at Facebook. I want to be so sensitive to those conversations. I don't want to speak out of turn, but he's very strategic and very good at identifying — at the time they had a piece of software that was identifying fast growing apps so that they could pursue them for acquisitions.
turns.zh: 我们有过一些有趣的对话，讨论我们在 Facebook 工作会是什么样子。我不想对那些对话不敬，不想说错话，但他非常有战略眼光，非常善于识别——当时他们有一个软件，可以识别快速增长的应用，以便进行收购。

turns.en: I'm so interested to see how those things play out. It almost sounds like something you'd see in a movie where you get this call from someone like Mark Zuckerberg who's built this massive empire and you almost feel like you get summoned from your dad's house to come and meet him. You went to Facebook's offices to meet him, I'm guessing in person.
turns.zh: 我对这些事情如何发展非常感兴趣。这听起来几乎像电影里的情节，你接到像马克·扎克伯格这样建立了庞大帝国的人的电话，感觉就像被从你父亲家里召唤去见他。我猜你是亲自去 Facebook 办公室见他的。

turns.en: I think at one point I went up there. He came and met us at Cheryl's condo in Santa Monica or something like that. Cheryl had a condo there. I think that's where we first met — Bobby and I met him.
turns.zh: 我记得有一次我去了那边。他过来在谢丽尔位于圣莫尼卡的公寓见了我们。谢丽尔在那里有一套公寓。我想那就是我们第一次见面的地方——鲍比和我见了他。

turns.en: Did he tell you that he was going to copy you if you didn't sell?
turns.zh: 他有没有告诉你，如果你不卖，他就会复制你的产品？

turns.en: He just explained that he was working on Poke and that it was for picture messaging and that kind of thing.
turns.zh: 他只是解释说他在开发 Poke，用于图片消息之类的。

turns.en: I'm guessing you didn't want to join a big company at that point.
turns.zh: 我猜你当时不想加入大公司。

turns.en: It was less about not wanting to join a big company. I think fundamentally we wanted to build a business that was different. If you go back to our first blog post, we talked about wanting to offer an alternative to social media. We felt that social media was about being pretty and perfect, and we wanted a way to communicate with our friends that was fun. The company ethos, the values, the visions were so divergent. It was very hard to imagine that we could keep doing what we loved in the way that we loved doing it as a part of that organization because they're just oriented in a very different way.
turns.zh: 与其说是不想加入大公司，不如说我们从根本上想建立一家不同的企业。如果你回头看看我们的第一篇博客文章，我们谈到想要提供一种社交媒体的替代方案。我们觉得社交媒体是关于漂亮和完美，而我们想要一种与朋友交流的有趣方式。公司的精神、价值观和愿景是如此不同。很难想象作为那个组织的一部分，我们还能以我们热爱的方式继续做我们热爱的事情，因为他们的方向完全不同。

turns.en: Was there ever a day where you doubted that decision?
turns.zh: 你有没有哪一天怀疑过那个决定？

turns.en: No. Not even a moment.
turns.zh: 没有。一刻也没有。

turns.en: Not a moment.
turns.zh: 一刻也没有。

turns.en: No.
turns.zh: 没有。

transcript.secZh: 董事会支持与出售股票

turns.en: Were all of the board supportive?
turns.zh: 董事会都支持吗？

turns.en: Yeah. All of our investors were supportive. They would have made a lot of money. But they did something very smart early on in a prior financing round around that time or before then, where Bobby and I were each able to sell $10 million of stock. So we each had 10 million bucks and we were like, wow, we made it. We have enough money forever. That allowed us to just swing for the fences. At that point you're like, let's just go for it. So there wasn't that feeling of, oh no, I'm not going to be able to buy a house or have a family. We were like, we each got 10 million bucks. Let's go for it.
turns.zh: 是的。所有投资者都支持。他们本来可以赚很多钱。但他们在早期一轮融资中做了一件非常聪明的事，大概在那时或之前，鲍比和我每人得以出售了 1000 万美元的股票。所以我们每人有 1000 万美元，我们觉得，哇，我们成功了。我们永远有钱了。这让我们可以放手一搏。那时你会想，就这么干吧。所以没有那种“哦不，我买不起房子，养不起家”的感觉。我们想，每人有 1000 万美元，干吧。

turns.en: There's a lesson in there as well for founders who are considering taking some off the table.
turns.zh: 这对那些考虑套现的创始人来说也是一个教训。

turns.en: I got a voice note from a founder actually listening to it this morning and I responded to her this morning.
turns.zh: 我今天早上还收到一位创始人的语音留言，我今早回复了她。

turns.en: A friend of mine whose business was I think it was at the top of the market in 2020 was set for an IPO and her board and investors and everyone was telling her that it's going to be a billion dollar business and that she should carry on going. She approached me and asked me if I would buy some shares off her. I took a look at the business and I valued it at a quarter of a million sorry a quarter of a billion so 250 million very different numbers. And her investors around her were telling her it was worth something else. So she had sent me a voice note which is now 4 years later this morning saying thank you for that Steven because although we didn't end up doing a deal with you, you put this idea in my head that I could be being basically having a story sold to me. So what I ended up doing a couple of months after our conversation is I sold some shares and obviously you know what happened in 2020 with the markets and eventually everything comes crashing down and she says I would be losing my mind now because the company's struggling and obviously the markets have changed if I hadn't have taken some off the table. And she was sending a voice note four years later to say thank you for putting that seed in their head. And I hear the same with you. I hear that you took some money off the table. It changed your decision framework. But also you just never know.
turns.zh: 我有个朋友，她的公司在 2020 年市场顶峰时准备 IPO，董事会和投资者都告诉她这会是个十亿美元级别的生意，让她继续冲。她来找我，问我能不能买她一些股份。我看了看这家公司，估值是 2.5 亿——抱歉，是 2.5 亿，不是 250 万，数字差很远。而她身边的投资者告诉她值另一个价。四年后的今天早上，她给我发了一条语音，说：“谢谢你，Steven，虽然我们最后没做成交易，但你让我意识到我可能被灌输了一个故事。”我们聊完之后几个月，她卖掉了一些股份。后来 2020 年市场崩盘，她说如果当时没套现一部分，现在公司挣扎、市场变了，她会疯掉。她四年后发语音感谢我种下了那个念头。我听说你也有类似经历——你套现了一些钱，这改变了你的决策框架。但你也永远不知道未来会怎样。

turns.en: Yeah. And I think you have to be, to your point, you have to be careful about approaching these situations as like zero sum, like either we're going to go big or with the risk that we'll lose it all, or we'll sell the company. I think there are all sorts of creative solutions that allow founders to take some money off the table, take care of their families, and still swing for the fences and build a big business. And venture capitalists are really aligned with the swing for the fences philosophy. Growth investors maybe less so as the business gets bigger, but when you have venture capitalists, I mean, they're looking for 10x, 100x return. So I think to find a formula that works for founders that allows them to take care of their families but also swing for the fences is a valuable approach.
turns.zh: 是的。而且我认为，正如你所说，你必须小心不要把这些情况看作零和博弈——要么做大，要么冒全盘皆输的风险，或者卖掉公司。其实有很多创造性的解决方案，让创始人既能套现一部分、照顾家庭，同时还能全力冲刺、打造大企业。风险投资人非常认同全力冲刺的理念。成长型投资者可能随着公司变大而没那么激进，但风险投资人追求的是 10 倍、100 倍的回报。所以，找到一种适合创始人的模式，让他们既能照顾家庭又能全力冲刺，我认为是很有价值的做法。

transcript.secZh: 与马克·扎克伯格的友谊

turns.en: Are you and Mark friends? You said you know him.
turns.zh: 你和马克是朋友吗？你说你认识他。

turns.en: Last time I saw him was at the Senate hearings I think last year.
turns.zh: 我上次见他是在去年的参议院听证会上。

turns.en: Those look fun. Should try it sometime.
turns.zh: 那些听证会看起来挺有意思的。改天我也该试试。

turns.en: No chance. No chance.
turns.zh: 没门。绝对没门。

transcript.sec: Life as a young billionaire CEO

turns.en: By 2014, when you were 24 years old, 40% of US adults were using Snapchat every day. And by 2015, Snapchat was reaching 75 million users on a monthly basis. At that point, what's life like for you as a CEO, as a founder? This was 2015. You became the world's youngest billionaire at the age of 25, just four years after launching Snapchat with an estimated net worth of $4 billion at the time.
turns.zh: 到 2014 年，你 24 岁时，40% 的美国成年人每天都在用 Snapchat。到 2015 年，Snapchat 月活用户达到 7500 万。那时候，作为 CEO 和创始人，你的生活是什么样的？那是 2015 年。你在 25 岁时成为全球最年轻的亿万富翁，推出 Snapchat 仅四年后，当时净资产估计为 40 亿美元。

turns.en: Life was pretty good. I met my wife in 2014, which was a game changer for me. And why now? She's an incredible woman and really gave me a huge sense of stability and a massive amount of support. She really cares about wellness. That's something that she's really passionate about. So it's like I live with a wellness coach basically every single day. And to have that sort of stability and support system while going through building our business was just profoundly helpful.
turns.zh: 生活挺不错的。我在 2014 年遇到了我的妻子，这对我来说是一个转折点。为什么是现在？她是一位了不起的女性，给了我巨大的稳定感和大量支持。她非常关注健康，这是她真正热爱的事情。所以我基本上每天都和一个健康教练住在一起。在创业过程中拥有这样的稳定和支持系统，真的非常有帮助。

turns.en: How do you manage that though? How do you manage a romantic relationship when you are piloting a rocket ship?
turns.zh: 你是怎么做到的？当你驾驶一艘火箭飞船时，如何管理一段恋爱关系？

turns.en: I think one of the things that was really helpful is she's incredibly accomplished herself. She has her own business that she is working on called Core Organics, which is an organic skincare business. So she really understands that it's hard to be an entrepreneur and was always really supportive of my work and my commitment to my work and our team. And so I think that was almost something that brought us together, not something that pushed us apart. And I think it's interesting. I talk to a lot of people who sometimes say, 'Hey, my relationship's been under strain because I'm working so much.' And what I realize when I have those conversations with people is very often times they met their spouse when they weren't working as much. They met their spouse 10 years ago, 15 years, 20 years ago, when they were just getting started in their career. And I think it can be difficult when you have a much bigger job and it's all time consuming and your spouse is like, 'Remember when we first met and you weren't working all that much? You were spending more time with me.' From the minute I met my wife, we were both working flat out. And so I think this expectation that we both work all the time to support our business, support our family, do what we love to do was kind of just built into the relationship.
turns.zh: 我认为其中一个非常有帮助的因素是，她自己也非常有成就。她有自己的生意，叫 Core Organics，是一个有机护肤品品牌。所以她非常理解创业的艰辛，一直非常支持我的工作以及我对工作和团队的投入。所以我认为这反而让我们更紧密，而不是把我们分开。我觉得这很有意思。我和很多人聊过，他们有时会说：‘嘿，我的关系因为工作太多而紧张。’我发现，在那些对话中，他们通常是在自己工作不那么忙的时候遇到配偶的。他们是在 10 年、15 年、20 年前遇到配偶的，那时他们的事业刚刚起步。我认为，当你有一份更大的工作、时间被完全占据时，配偶可能会说：‘还记得我们刚认识时你没那么忙吗？你陪我更多。’而从我遇到我妻子的那一刻起，我们俩都在拼命工作。所以，我们都一直工作来支持事业、家庭、做我们热爱的事情，这种期望从一开始就融入了我们的关系。

turns.en: But then how do you make time to see each other? Do you need to put systems in place to make sure that you're not just both at the office the whole time?
turns.zh: 但你们怎么腾出时间见面呢？是否需要建立一些机制，确保你们不会整天都待在办公室？

turns.en: One of the things that's really been helpful that we started doing a couple years ago is just having Sunday family day. Always family day. And that means everyone's at home. So if our kids want to have friends over or whatever, that's totally fine. But they're not going to their friends' houses or everyone's together. And just dedicating that time to our family is really important.
turns.zh: 几年前我们开始做的一件事非常有帮助，就是设立周日家庭日。永远是家庭日。这意味着所有人都在家。如果孩子们想邀请朋友来玩，完全没问题。但他们不会去朋友家，而是大家在一起。把这段时间专门留给家人，真的非常重要。

turns.en: What role does she play in giving you feedback?
turns.zh: 她在给你反馈方面扮演什么角色？

turns.en: Oh wow. She gives very candid feedback. Which is a gift, right? It's a gift. And I think she's really passionate about areas that are different than what I'm passionate about. And so she's able to give me feedback in different areas or things that I really wouldn't even consider.
turns.zh: 哦，哇。她给的反馈非常坦诚。这是一种天赋，对吧？是天赋。而且她热衷的领域和我不同，所以她能在不同的方面给我反馈，或者提出我根本不会考虑的事情。

turns.en: Is there any tough feedback that she shared with you that you can share with me?
turns.zh: 有没有什么她给你的严厉反馈，你可以和我分享？

turns.en: You know, I think one of the challenges that I have sometimes is I can have a very harsh tone. Like even if what I'm saying is really trying to be helpful or kind or whatever. I was raised by my dad who was a litigator, my mom was a tax lawyer. I grew up listening to my dad have very intense conversations on the phone all the time. And so in business mode, I can be very direct. And I don't think it's helpful. And I don't think people want to listen when you have a sharp or aggressive tone. And so I think she's always just encouraging me like, hey, you could say the same thing but in a slightly different way. And people will hear it.
turns.zh: 你知道，我有时的一个挑战是我说话语气可能很严厉。即使我说的话本意是想帮忙或友善。我父亲是诉讼律师，母亲是税务律师。我从小听父亲在电话里进行非常激烈的对话。所以在工作模式下，我可能非常直接。我认为这没有帮助。当你语气尖锐或咄咄逼人时，人们不想听。所以她总是鼓励我，说你可以用稍微不同的方式说同样的话，人们就会听进去。

turns.en: You know, my girlfriend says that to me because I think sometimes I fail to context switch out of work, Steve, to then and you know, because you'll be getting emails and texts at home sometimes and my girlfriend might come up and she might say something and the way I respond is almost how I would respond as if I was at the office, but and it just and I have to say she's always right because there is a part of my tone which I think was conveying the emotion I was feeling from the thing I was doing to her to try and con to get her to leave me alone. This is something we can both work on. It's crazy. It's crazy that Matt is so much at home the way you say something. 100%. And having an empathy and a and a kindness. Um, and you you've had four children as well, which is something that I've not had. So, for me, that's an extra responsibility on top of the rocket ship of Snapchat, the relationship, and now four kids as well.
turns.zh: 你知道吗，我女朋友也这么跟我说，因为我有时候没法从工作中切换出来，Steve。你在家也会收到邮件和短信，我女朋友走过来跟我说什么，我回应的方式几乎就像在办公室一样。我得说她总是对的，因为我语气里带着我手头事情的情绪，想让她别打扰我。这是我们俩都需要改进的地方。真疯狂，Matt 在家说话的方式也这样。100%。还有同理心和善意。你有四个孩子，我还没有。所以对我来说，这是在 Snapchat 这艘火箭船、感情关系之上又多了一份责任，现在还有四个孩子。

turns.en: Yeah. But it's the greatest in the world. I mean, the greatest thing in the world to have kids. really literally nothing better on the planet. I mean, I have not found anything close. Why? Because the love and connection that you have with your children is unlike anything else that you'll ever experience. I mean, it's it's profound, you know.
turns.zh: 是的，但这是世界上最棒的事。我是说，有孩子是世界上最好的事。地球上真的没有比这更好的了。我还没找到任何接近的体验。为什么？因为你和孩子之间的爱与连接，是你从未体验过的。非常深刻。

turns.en: How do you both juggle the four children, the businesses, the relationship?
turns.zh: 你们俩是怎么平衡四个孩子、事业和感情的？

turns.en: Well, I think Miranda probably juggles it better than I do. I think um and she's really committed to spending time with our children. I mean that's something that's so important to her and it's important uh to me. I don't think you know I I spent a lot of time actually over the years just I found people who are extremely successful and just like asked them like hey how do you raise great kids like you've been extremely successful. How'd you do it? And my basic takeaway from like a hundred of these conversations was basically that parents that are actually committed that can spend that time with their kids and do it themselves, engage with their kids themselves, they tend to have really fruitful relationship with their kids, and their kids seem to turn out really great. And I it breaks my heart that there are so many parents that can't spend that direct one-on-one time with their kids or I guess in my case one on four time uh with with their kids because that seems to be kind of the key ingredient is that connection with your parents being there.
turns.zh: 我觉得 Miranda 可能比我平衡得更好。她非常投入地花时间陪伴我们的孩子，这对她来说很重要，对我也很重要。多年来，我花了很多时间找到那些极其成功的人，问他们：你是怎么培养出优秀孩子的？你那么成功，是怎么做到的？我从大概一百次对话中得到的基本结论是：真正投入、能亲自花时间陪伴孩子、与孩子互动的父母，往往与孩子有非常丰硕的关系，孩子也成长得很好。让我心碎的是，有那么多父母无法直接一对一地陪伴孩子——或者对我来说是一对四——因为这似乎是关键：与父母的连接和在场。

turns.en: Do you own a business or do you work in marketing? If that's you, listen up for a valuable opportunity from our show sponsor LinkedIn. I'm an investor in about 40 odd companies and while they operate in different industries, they all face one challenge. Maybe the most important challenge when it comes to marketing, getting their brand in front of the right audiences. We can probably all think of times when we've been scrolling on social media and received an ad that absolutely wasn't relevant to us. LinkedIn has now grown to a network of over a billion professionals, including 130 million decision makers, which means you can specifically target your buyers by job title, seniority, industry, even company revenue. So, stop wasting your marketing budget on the wrong audience and start targeting the right crowd through LinkedIn now. And because you're part of my community, LinkedIn is offering you a $100 credit for your next campaign. Head to linkedin.com/diary to claim yours now. That's linkedin.com/diary. And of course, terms and conditions apply and only available on LinkedIn ads.
turns.zh: 你是企业主还是做市场营销的？如果是，请听好我们节目赞助商 LinkedIn 带来的宝贵机会。我投资了大约 40 多家公司，虽然它们行业不同，但都面临一个挑战——也许是营销中最关键的挑战：让品牌触达正确的受众。我们可能都遇到过在社交媒体上刷到完全不相关的广告。LinkedIn 现已拥有超过 10 亿专业人士的网络，其中包括 1.3 亿决策者，这意味着你可以按职位、资历、行业甚至公司收入精准定位买家。所以，别再浪费营销预算在错误受众上，现在就开始通过 LinkedIn 瞄准正确人群。因为你是我们社区的一员，LinkedIn 为你下一次广告活动提供 100 美元额度。前往 linkedin.com/diary 领取。网址是 linkedin.com/diary。当然，条款和条件适用，仅限 LinkedIn 广告。

turns.en: I saw your LinkedIn page. I saw your bio. Do you know what I'm going to say? I think so. Yeah. In your LinkedIn bio, it says that you're the I think VP of product at Meta. Is it not my joke?
turns.zh: 我看了你的 LinkedIn 页面，看了你的简介。你知道我要说什么吗？我想我知道。是的。你的 LinkedIn 简介上写的是 Meta 的产品副总裁吧？这不是我的玩笑吗？

turns.en: We uh we uh have appropriated that joke from I think Cara Swisser who who originated it. But I think now that they've copied ephemeral messaging and stories, everything, you know, a lot of the stuff we've done in augmented reality, of course, now they say they're working on glasses, um which, you know, we've been working on for over a decade. I think, um I think I've I think I've earned that that title. I don't know.
turns.zh: 我们借用了这个玩笑，我想最初是 Cara Swisser 说的。但现在他们复制了阅后即焚、故事、一切功能，还有我们在增强现实上做的很多东西，当然现在他们说在做眼镜，而我们已经做了十多年。我想我已经赢得了那个头衔。我不知道。

turns.en: That must piss you off because that would piss me off. Like I can sit on a podcast and I can say now I wouldn't be bothered. You know, the thing is that blah blah blah blah blah. But no, that would piss me off because you just told me that there's a lot of people that are going to great creative lengths to think of these ideas. They're having thousands of ideas. You're you're fighting to find one and then you have that moment where you present it to the world. The world says, "This is interesting." You you know the guy that came up with it. You sit next to him.
turns.zh: 那一定让你很恼火，因为那会让我很恼火。我可以坐在播客里说我现在不会在意，但事实是那会让我恼火。你刚告诉我，有很多人费尽心思去想这些创意，他们有成百上千个想法，你努力找到一个，然后向世界展示，世界说“这很有趣”。你知道想出这个点子的人，你就坐在他旁边。

turns.en: One of the things that's incredibly irritating about it is they repurpose our inventions to make products that make people feel unhappy and bad about themselves. One of the things that was so fascinating just in the last year there was a study out of the Netherland totally independent study out of the Netherlands. we weren't involved. Another one out of Australia and it was comparing I think like Instagram, Tik Tok and Snapchat and it it basically found that Snapchat there there are no negative they the study determined there were no negative health imp me mental health implications of using Snapchat but there were negative mental health implications of using Instagram and Tik Tok and in fact I think the study in the Netherlands found Snapchat actually promotes well-being and helps uh promote your relationships as well and so I think what's really frustrating is when people think because they've copied some of our features that the products are the same or that they do the same thing when our product is designed in a way that's very different that's designed to support your relationships with your close friends and family and ultimately is something that supports your your well-being and so what we never want anyone to think is if they're using stories on Instagram that that's the same as Snapchat and you know even though they stole the the the name of the product or the way that you know some of some of the functionality of it the way that Snapchat is designed on the whole is something that can have a really positive impact in in people's lives And that's not something that people feel when they use Instagram.
turns.zh: 最令人恼火的一点是，他们挪用我们的发明，制造出让人感到不快乐、自我感觉糟糕的产品。去年有一项完全独立的荷兰研究，我们没有参与，还有一项澳大利亚研究，比较了 Instagram、TikTok 和 Snapchat。研究结论是，Snapchat 没有负面的心理健康影响，而 Instagram 和 TikTok 有负面影响。事实上，荷兰的研究发现 Snapchat 实际上促进了幸福感，也有助于增进人际关系。所以真正令人沮丧的是，当人们以为他们复制了我们的一些功能，产品就一样了，或者效果相同，但我们的产品设计方式截然不同——它旨在支持你与亲密朋友和家人的关系，最终支持你的幸福感。我们绝不想让任何人认为，在 Instagram 上使用故事就等于 Snapchat。尽管他们偷了产品名称或某些功能，但 Snapchat 的整体设计可以对人们的生活产生非常积极的影响，而 Instagram 的用户感受不到这一点。

turns.en: Did you ever consider sending Mark an email or like a message or a phone call when they first started copying some of your core features like the story feature?
turns.zh: 当他们刚开始复制你们的核心功能比如故事功能时，你有没有考虑过给 Mark 发邮件、发消息或打电话？

turns.en: No. No, we we didn't do that. I think, you know, one of the things that I really admire about Kevin Cyrum is when they copied the the stories feature, they they stopped pretending that they were doing anything different. I mean, with with things like Poke, they tried to sort of pass it off as their own creation. Oh, we're do, you know, doing it a little bit differently. with, you know, with stories.
turns.zh: 没有，我们没有那样做。我真的很欣赏 Kevin Systrom 的一点是，当他们复制故事功能时，他们不再假装自己在做不同的事。像 Poke 这样的东西，他们试图把它伪装成自己的创作，说“我们做得有点不同”。但故事功能，他们就没再装了。

turns.en: Kevin Systrom came out and just said, 'Hey, we think this is a really great feature. This is a really great product and we're going to steal it and put it in Instagram and we think you're going to love it.' And the honesty at least was admirable. Do you feel a sense of injustice when someone steals your idea like that?
turns.zh: 凯文·斯特罗姆公开说：‘嘿，我们认为这是一个非常棒的功能。这是一个非常棒的产品，我们要把它抄过来放到 Instagram 里，而且我们觉得你会喜欢的。’至少这种坦诚是值得钦佩的。当有人像这样窃取你的创意时，你会感到不公吗？

turns.en: No, not at all. I mean, that is sort of the — I'm sure you know the saying, 'Great artists steal.' I think one of the things about our industry is that people are constantly being inspired by one another. In the very early days, I went to visit ByteDance when they only had Toutiao, a newsfeed app essentially, but it was backed by AI and ML. When I saw that, it was really inspiring to me, and we made a big change to our product. We actually separated out all the creator and publisher content from stories from friends, and we said, 'Unlike social media where you're seeing content based on what your friends like or comment on, on Snapchat we're going to do ML-driven recommendations.' So you'll have content from your friends, but then you'll have this whole other world of content from creators and publishers recommended based on your interests and passions. So I do think drawing inspiration from other companies and businesses is part of innovating. It's part of the game. But it definitely showed us that if we're going to innovate and make new products, we ought to make things that are really difficult to copy — that take a long time to copy, that are really hard to do. Because if you go after really difficult and hard things, it's much harder for these large companies to just copy them.
turns.zh: 完全没有。我是说，这有点像——你肯定知道那句老话：‘伟大的艺术家剽窃。’我认为我们这个行业的一个特点就是人们不断相互启发。很早的时候，我去拜访字节跳动，当时他们只有今日头条，一个本质上基于 AI 和 ML 的新闻推送应用。看到那之后，我深受启发，我们对产品做了重大改变。我们把所有创作者和发布者的内容与朋友的故事分开，并说：‘不像社交媒体那样根据朋友的点赞或评论来展示内容，在 Snapchat 上我们将采用 ML 驱动的推荐。’所以你会看到朋友的内容，但还会有另一个由创作者和发布者组成的世界，根据你的兴趣和热情来推荐。所以我确实认为从其他公司和企业汲取灵感是创新的一部分。这是游戏的一部分。但这确实告诉我们，如果我们要创新、要开发新产品，就应该做那些极难复制的东西——需要很长时间才能复制、非常难做到的事情。因为如果你去攻克真正困难的事情，这些大公司就很难直接抄袭。

transcript.secZh: 给被复制创始人的建议

turns.en: I'm thinking about so many founders I know that have started businesses — from a t-shirt company to a podcast — and they're dealing with people copying their ideas. Sometimes they react really badly, take to social media, post and say, 'Look, this person copied us,' and tag the brand. What advice would you give to founders that are being knocked off?
turns.zh: 我想到了很多我认识的创始人，他们创办了各种企业——从 T 恤公司到播客——都面临别人抄袭创意的问题。有时他们反应很激烈，在社交媒体上发帖说：‘看，这个人抄袭了我们’，然后@那个品牌。你会给那些被抄袭的创始人什么建议？

turns.en: I think it's really important to very quickly evolve from being just a product or a feature to becoming a platform or an ecosystem. What I mean by that is: if I compare the early days of stories, where people just posted stories for one another, it's relatively easy to copy that feature — you can code that up pretty quickly, probably with AI now very quickly. When I look at what we've done with augmented reality, we have a Lens Core, our own rendering engine for AR that runs on the phone and on our glasses. We have a tool called Lens Studio, an incredibly sophisticated tool that developers use to build these AR experiences. We have hundreds of millions of people using these AR experiences on the phone, and hundreds of thousands of developers making all these AR experiences. When you have that sort of very complicated technology that's hard to copy, and an ecosystem of people using it — both developers and creators and our community who love those AR experiences — it becomes very hard to copy the four million lenses that developers have created for our platform, or move the hundreds of millions of people enjoying AR on Snapchat to a new platform. Especially in the technology business, the faster you can evolve from being a feature or a product to a real platform, I think that's where the value is created over the long term.
turns.zh: 我认为非常重要的是，要尽快从仅仅是一个产品或功能，演变成一个平台或生态系统。我的意思是：如果拿早期的故事功能来比较，人们只是互相发布故事，复制这个功能相对容易——你可以很快写出代码，现在用 AI 可能更快。但看看我们在增强现实方面所做的：我们有 Lens Core，这是我们自己的 AR 渲染引擎，可以在手机和眼镜上运行。我们有 Lens Studio 这个工具，一个非常复杂的工具，开发者可以用它来构建这些 AR 体验。我们有数亿人在手机上使用这些 AR 体验，还有数十万开发者制作所有这些 AR 体验。当你拥有这种非常复杂、难以复制的技术，以及一个由开发者、创作者和热爱这些 AR 体验的社区组成的生态系统时，就很难复制开发者为我们平台创建的四百万个镜头，或者将 Snapchat 上享受 AR 的数亿人迁移到一个新平台。尤其是在科技行业，你越快从功能或产品演变为真正的平台，我认为长期价值就越能创造出来。

transcript.sec: Hard to build vs easy to copy

turns.en: So it's almost this relationship between how hard and complicated the thing was to build and create is inversely correlated to how easy it is to copy.
turns.zh: 所以这几乎是一种关系：构建和创造某样东西的难度和复杂程度，与它被复制的容易程度成反比。

turns.en: I think so. And then how much of an ecosystem it is in terms of other people using it — almost more like a marketplace — it makes it very difficult to migrate both sides of the marketplace.
turns.zh: 我想是的。然后还有生态系统的规模，即有多少人在使用它——几乎更像一个市场——这使得迁移市场的双方都非常困难。

transcript.secZh: 大型科技公司是垄断吗？

turns.en: Do you think that the technology companies that exist now — those trillion-dollar ones, those big social platforms — are monopolies and should be broken up? Because there was a big call originally to break these companies up, and you've been, I guess you could say, victim to the strength of a monopoly being able to swoop in, copy, replicate, steal, and innovation.
turns.zh: 你认为现在存在的科技公司——那些万亿美元级别的、大型社交平台——是垄断企业，应该被拆分吗？因为最初有很多呼声要拆分这些公司，而你，可以说，是垄断力量能够突然介入、复制、模仿、窃取和创新的受害者。

turns.en: I think what matters more about whether or not they're monopolies is what we think is going to happen next. If you remember, there was a period when everyone thought Microsoft was going to take over the world. They were caught up in a lot of antitrust legislation, inquiries, and lawsuits. Ultimately, maybe as a result of being distracted by the lawsuits, but also just a function of how they thought about their business at the time, they missed the entire mobile cycle. I think people are looking at Google right now and asking if a similar moment is happening for Google. Google is subject to a lot of these antitrust inquiries. Is ChatGPT, for example, coming along with AI actually going to make it a lot harder for Google's core business to compete? Given the longevity of the regulatory and litigation cycle — you're talking 10-plus years — it almost doesn't matter so much if the government thinks it's a monopoly or not, because there's not much it seems like they're able to do about it. So what matters a lot more for small technology companies is thinking about what fundamental innovations and technologies we can work on and develop that can ultimately help us grow our business and maybe one day catch one of these larger companies on a back foot.
turns.zh: 我认为关于它们是否是垄断企业，更重要的是我们认为接下来会发生什么。如果你还记得，有一段时间大家都认为微软要统治世界了。他们陷入了很多反垄断立法、调查和诉讼。最终，也许是因为被诉讼分散了注意力，但也因为他们当时对业务的看法，他们错过了整个移动周期。我认为现在人们在看谷歌，问谷歌是否也在经历类似的时刻。谷歌正面临很多这样的反垄断调查。例如，ChatGPT 伴随着 AI 的出现，是否真的会让谷歌的核心业务更难竞争？考虑到监管和诉讼周期的长度——你谈的是 10 年以上——政府是否认为它是垄断企业几乎不那么重要了，因为他们似乎也做不了太多。所以对于小型科技公司来说，更重要的是思考我们可以研究和开发哪些根本性的创新和技术，最终帮助我们发展业务，也许有一天能让这些大公司措手不及。

transcript.secZh: 如果你是扎克伯格会复制吗？

transcript.sec: Would you copy if you were Zuckerberg?

turns.en: Want to talk about that. But my last question on copying was: if you were in Mark Zuckerberg's shoes, would you have copied Snapchat?
turns.zh: 我们想谈谈这个。但关于抄袭的最后一个问题是：如果你处在马克·扎克伯格的位置，你会抄袭 Snapchat 吗？

turns.en: I think given their market position, it's a very effective strategy. They basically have an enormous cash pile. I think they're investing $20 billion a year right now just into the AR glasses stuff and some of their VR stuff. The AR glasses stuff is largely copying what we've been doing. And then outside of that, they have tens of billions of dollars in all sorts of other investments, including copying ChatGPT and all the progress on large language models.
turns.zh: 我认为考虑到他们的市场地位，这是一个非常有效的策略。他们基本上拥有巨额现金储备。我认为他们现在每年投资 200 亿美元用于 AR 眼镜和一些 VR 项目。AR 眼镜项目很大程度上是在复制我们一直在做的事情。除此之外，他们还有数百亿美元用于各种其他投资，包括复制 ChatGPT 以及大语言模型的所有进展。

transcript.secZh: 应对模仿功能与团队士气

transcript.sec: Handling Copycat Features and Team Morale

turns.en: So, I think it's quite an effective strategy if you're at that scale generating that much cash to just, you know, deploy that capital across a bunch of different bets and wait and see what companies are successful and what they make and then try to throw a ton of capital and hoping that those companies don't get to scale. Was there a hard day for you amongst all of this copying? Was there a hardest day that you can recall?
turns.zh: 所以，我认为如果你达到那个规模，产生那么多现金，那么将资本分散到各种赌注上，等着看哪些公司成功、它们做出了什么，然后投入大量资金，希望那些公司无法规模化，这是一种相当有效的策略。在这一系列抄袭中，有没有哪一天对你来说特别艰难？你能回忆起最艰难的一天吗？

turns.en: There was a lot of concern when Instagram stories first launched that Snapchat would essentially be obsolete and you know they did a very good job talking about how many people were using stories and we got a lot of questions and pressure about is Snapchat ever going to succeed and people didn't I think they realized at the time that folks were using Instagram mostly for content creators and influencers and that sort of thing and they were mostly using Snapchat for their friends and family and so we had really focused on this friends and family use case that was not really what Instagram was going after. They were much more focused on influencers and I think it was really only because Snapchat had started growing with those influencers. If you remember, DJ Khaled and some of those early snaps, there were influencers who were starting to join Snapchat just to use stories, right? To use it differently than how we had initially designed it for friends and family. And I think that's what really got Instagram's attention. So, it was really frustrating in those moments where people were saying, well, how's Snapchat going to survive because they didn't understand that stories on Instagram is for a totally different purpose than stories on Snapchat?
turns.zh: 当 Instagram 故事功能刚推出时，有很多担忧认为 Snapchat 基本上会过时。他们非常成功地宣传了有多少人在使用故事功能，我们收到了很多问题和压力，问 Snapchat 是否还能成功。我认为当时人们没有意识到，大家使用 Instagram 主要是为了内容创作者和网红之类，而他们使用 Snapchat 主要是为了朋友和家人。所以我们一直专注于这个朋友和家人的使用场景，而这并不是 Instagram 所追求的。他们更关注网红，我认为 Snapchat 开始增长正是因为那些网红。如果你还记得，DJ Khaled 和一些早期的快拍，有网红开始加入 Snapchat 只是为了使用故事功能，对吧？他们以不同于我们最初为朋友和家人设计的方式使用它。我认为这才是真正引起 Instagram 注意的原因。所以，当人们说“Snapchat 怎么生存下去”的时候，真的很令人沮丧，因为他们不明白 Instagram 上的故事和 Snapchat 上的故事用途完全不同。

turns.en: You must see that coming because people start leaking that there's this new feature coming. I was running a social media business at the time and there were hackers that can kind of look into code bases and see what features are about to come and then it's leaked out to the blog. So as a team you're managing the emotions of a group of people and that group of people they're all hearing that the biggest player in the game is about to launch a central feature of your proposition. How does the leader manage the emotions of the people through that?
turns.zh: 你一定预见到了，因为人们开始泄露说有个新功能要来了。我当时经营一家社交媒体公司，有黑客可以查看代码库，看到即将推出的功能，然后泄露到博客上。所以作为团队，你在管理一群人的情绪，而这些人都在听说行业里最大的玩家即将推出你核心功能的一个版本。作为领导者，你如何管理大家的情绪？

turns.en: Well, I think that can be something that is energizing for a team, right? If you have some of the biggest companies in the world validating what you're working on, that can be really energizing if you approach it that way. So, I think rather than just saying, "Oh, no, it's game over, we might as well shut the thing down and give up." I think if you say, "This is really evidence that we're on the right path here, that we're building products that people love, that they're getting the attention of some of the biggest and most powerful companies in the world." You know, let's build on that. Let's continue to go build products that billions of people all over the world will use. And I think ultimately to this point of, you know, can you hire and retain really talented, creative people. I think it's pretty cool if you're a designer at Snap that the things you're making are not just used by the 850 million plus people that use Snapchat, but billions of people that use all sorts of other products because people get so much inspiration from our design team and what they build. I think that's pretty cool.
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为这可以成为激励团队的事情，对吧？如果世界上一些最大的公司验证了你正在做的事情，如果你以这种方式看待，那真的可以激励人心。所以，我认为与其说“哦，不，游戏结束了，我们不如关门放弃”，不如说“这确实证明我们走在正确的道路上，我们在打造人们喜爱的产品，这些产品引起了世界上一些最大、最强大公司的注意。”让我们在此基础上继续努力。让我们继续打造全球数十亿人都会使用的产品。我认为最终，关于你是否能招聘和留住真正有才华、有创造力的人这一点，如果你在 Snap 做设计师，你创造的东西不仅被 8.5 亿多 Snapchat 用户使用，还被使用各种其他产品的数十亿人使用，因为人们从我们的设计团队和他们的作品中获得如此多的灵感，我觉得这很酷。

turns.en: One of the use cases that emerged pretty much out of the blue, I think, for Snapchat was, and this is something I was only thinking about yesterday, is you at some point have to make a decision about adult content on the app and OnlyFans have built this massive business now, and they're basically in the adult content business. At some point you must have been challenged on that by investors or by users whether you were going to allow adult content to be on the platform because that would have been presumably a growing user base.
turns.zh: 我认为 Snapchat 有一个几乎突然出现的用例，这是我昨天才想到的，就是你在某个时候必须对应用上的成人内容做出决定。OnlyFans 现在已经建立了一个庞大的业务，他们基本上就是做成人内容的。在某个时候，你一定被投资者或用户质疑过，你是否会允许成人内容出现在平台上，因为那可能会是一个不断增长的用户群。

turns.en: Interesting. Yeah, we proactively scan for pornography and remove it. I mean it's against our content guidelines. We've been doing that for a really long time. So yeah, I mean that's just not how we think about our core business. And I also think, when you think about self-expression, the importance of self-expression, the environment that you're in really matters, right? And that's why we have content guidelines because we want people to feel like they're in an environment where they can express themselves. And I think some of the conversation about different content guidelines or having content guidelines or not having them has been really interesting because I think people are missing the broader point. If you have a platform with no content guidelines and it's full of people yelling at each other or saying really mean or offensive things or posting a lot of pornography, that's a really uncomfortable thing for most people, right? That's uncomfortable. You say, "Uh, this maybe this platform isn't for me. Maybe I don't feel comfortable expressing myself here because all the stuff I'm seeing isn't really appropriate or aligned with my values." And so one of the things we discovered really early on is if you want to create a platform where people feel comfortable expressing themselves, feel comfortable communicating with their friends and family, having content guidelines is really helpful because it means that the content experience is one that feels more comfortable.
turns.zh: 有意思。是的，我们主动扫描色情内容并将其删除。我的意思是，这违反了我们的内容准则。我们这样做已经很久了。所以，是的，这不是我们思考核心业务的方式。而且我也认为，当你思考自我表达时，自我表达的重要性，你所处的环境真的很重要，对吧？这就是为什么我们有内容准则，因为我们希望人们感觉他们处于一个可以表达自己的环境中。我认为关于不同内容准则、有或没有内容准则的讨论非常有趣，因为我认为人们忽略了更广泛的观点。如果你有一个没有内容准则的平台，上面充满了人们互相大喊大叫、说非常刻薄或冒犯性的话、或者发布大量色情内容，这对大多数人来说是非常不舒服的，对吧？那很不舒服。你会说，“呃，这个平台可能不适合我。我可能不觉得在这里表达自己很舒服，因为我看到的所有东西都不太合适，或者不符合我的价值观。”所以我们很早发现的一件事是，如果你想创建一个让人们感到舒适地表达自己、舒适地与朋友和家人交流的平台，拥有内容准则非常有帮助，因为它意味着内容体验让人感觉更舒适。

turns.en: But isn't that people would say well that censorship? I'm thinking now of the video that Mark Zuckerberg released about Meta's change to their moderation systems moving to Texas realizing that I think he said that they'd overindexed with their moderators in terms of left-leaning politics. So, a lot of the right-leaning content had been censored. What do you make of that argument for content moderation that we don't want to censor people?
turns.zh: 但人们不会说这是审查吗？我现在想到马克·扎克伯格发布的视频，关于 Meta 改变其审核系统，搬到得克萨斯州，意识到我认为他说他们在审核员方面过度偏向左翼政治。所以很多右翼内容被审查了。对于这种“我们不想审查人们”的内容审核论点，你怎么看？

turns.en: I think it's a misunderstanding of the First Amendment and how it applies. If we look at our country, the way, at least here in the United States with the First Amendment that really focuses on the way that the government interacts with content creators or content publishers and it says, hey, it's not okay for the government to interfere with individuals or publishers self-expression, right? That's not allowed. But one of the things the First Amendment also does is say, platforms or individuals can make choices about what sort of content they want to promote or want to have on their platform. That's part of the First Amendment. You can't force the Wall Street Journal to put this article or that article or accept any article from any author all around the world.
turns.zh: 我认为这是对第一修正案及其适用方式的误解。如果我们看看我们的国家，至少在美国，第一修正案真正关注的是政府与内容创作者或内容发布者的互动方式，它说，政府干涉个人或发布者的自我表达是不可以的，对吧？这是不允许的。但第一修正案也规定，平台或个人可以选择他们想要推广或在其平台上展示的内容类型。这是第一修正案的一部分。你不能强迫《华尔街日报》刊登这篇文章或那篇文章，或者接受来自世界各地任何作者的任何文章。

turns.en: The Wall Street Journal as a paper can decide what authors it wants to include on its pages. And that's part of the protected First Amendment expression we have in this country. So this whole notion of censorship doesn't apply to companies that are private businesses that actually have a First Amendment right to decide what content is on their platform. And they may want to decide: we're open to literally anything, anything goes, no problem. And it seems like some platforms are making that choice. But other platforms like ours say, 'Hey, in order to have a healthy set of discourse across our platform, in order to make sure people feel comfortable when they're viewing content on our platform, we don't want people to come across pornography, for example, or violent content or hateful content. That's not something that makes people feel good. And we actually want to make sure that content isn't on our platform because it doesn't comply with our guidelines.' And that may be one of the reasons why in some of these studies it shows that people feel better when they use Snapchat because they're not encountering really violent content when they're using Snapchat.
turns.zh: 《华尔街日报》作为一份报纸，可以决定它想在版面上收录哪些作者。这是我们国家受第一修正案保护的言论表达的一部分。所以整个审查的概念并不适用于那些实际上是私营企业的公司，它们拥有第一修正案赋予的权利，来决定其平台上有什么内容。它们可以选择：我们对任何内容都开放，什么都可以，没问题。似乎有些平台正在做出这样的选择。但像我们这样的其他平台会说：‘嘿，为了在我们的平台上拥有健康的讨论氛围，为了确保人们在浏览我们平台上的内容时感到舒适，我们不希望人们看到色情内容、暴力内容或仇恨言论。这些内容不会让人感觉良好。我们实际上想确保这些内容不在我们的平台上，因为它们不符合我们的准则。’这可能是某些研究显示人们在使用 Snapchat 时感觉更好的原因之一，因为他们不会在使用 Snapchat 时遇到非常暴力的内容。

turns.en: Is there an issue that if you're geographically based in Los Angeles or California, then your content moderation perspective is going to be very left-leaning versus if you're based in a red state and that might not be representative of the world? Or do you just not care? Did you just think, well, these are our values as a company?
turns.zh: 是否存在一个问题：如果你的地理基地位于洛杉矶或加利福尼亚，那么你的内容审核视角会非常左倾，而如果你位于一个红州，那可能就不代表世界？还是你根本不在乎？你只是认为，好吧，这些是我们公司的价值观？

turns.en: I don't think so because I don't think saying extreme violence is not something we want on our platform is political. I think that's a values-based decision. Or saying we don't want to service pornography to our community. I don't think that's a political choice. I think that's a values-based decision. So, I think unfortunately right now in our culture, there's actually a real temptation to politicize things that are actually quite common sensical. And so I think we have to avoid that temptation and instead focus on what are the values or the business choices that people are making.
turns.zh: 我不这么认为，因为我觉得说“极端暴力不是我们想在平台上看到的东西”并不是政治性的。我认为这是一个基于价值观的决定。或者说“我们不想向社区提供色情内容”。我不认为那是政治选择。我认为这是一个基于价值观的决定。所以，我认为不幸的是，在我们现在的文化中，实际上有一种强烈的诱惑，要把那些其实很常识性的东西政治化。因此，我认为我们必须避免这种诱惑，转而关注人们正在做出的价值观或商业选择。

turns.en: Why do you think Meta have rolled back their moderation policies?
turns.zh: 你认为为什么 Meta 收紧了他们的审核政策？

turns.en: I'm not sure. I think there's a moment in time when they seem to have a lot of support to do it. I think it'll be challenging for them in Europe for example where there's a lot of rules and regulations about prohibiting things like hate speech or terrorist content. And I think it'll be interesting to see how they navigate that. It's certainly a lot less expensive to avoid moderating content. It costs money to moderate content and that could be a consideration as well.
turns.zh: 我不确定。我认为有一个时刻他们似乎得到了很多支持来做这件事。我认为这对他们在欧洲来说会很有挑战性，例如那里有很多关于禁止仇恨言论或恐怖主义内容的规则和法规。我认为看看他们如何应对会很有趣。当然，不审核内容要便宜得多。审核内容需要花钱，这也可能是一个考虑因素。

turns.en: If you don't moderate content, does engagement go up?
turns.zh: 如果不审核内容，参与度会上升吗？

turns.en: That's a great question. I've seen some reports and studies that show that if content is moderated, engagement can go down. Certainly there are studies that show that negative content spreads much further and faster on social media for human reasons. But I'm not sure in this particular instance.
turns.zh: 这是个好问题。我看到一些报告和研究显示，如果内容被审核，参与度可能会下降。当然，有研究表明，由于人类的原因，负面内容在社交媒体上传播得更远更快。但在这个具体案例中我不确定。

turns.en: How are you feeling about the social media landscape? It's changed so much in the last six months. It's just not even six months, I'd say 12 months since I think Elon bought Twitter, now called X, that it's almost like this domino effect has happened in terms of content moderation, in terms of the types of voices on social media, in terms of this big movement around censorship and free speech. There's also been this splintering of social media where lots of people are now leaving certain platforms and going to Blue Sky and Threads and you know Rumble was the only sort of big right-leaning platform just a couple of years ago and now I don't know it seems like it's all changing before our eyes.
turns.zh: 你对社交媒体格局感觉如何？在过去六个月里变化太大了。甚至不到六个月，我想说自从埃隆·马斯克收购了 Twitter（现在叫 X）以来，已经 12 个月了，几乎就像多米诺骨牌效应一样，在内容审核、社交媒体上的声音类型、围绕审查和言论自由的这场大运动方面都发生了连锁反应。还有社交媒体的分裂，很多人现在离开某些平台，转向 Blue Sky 和 Threads，你知道几年前 Rumble 是唯一一个大的右倾平台，现在我不知道，似乎一切都在我们眼前变化。

turns.en: I don't read too much into it to be honest with you. To me, it feels like more of a continuation of almost, at least in the case of I think we can use Meta as the example just because they are essentially the social media market. And what's really interesting about their choices is what they've tended to do is sort of follow the political winds. So, when Biden was president, and Mark's been very public about this, they did a lot of very proactive content moderation. And that was something that apparently I guess the White House at the time was asking them to do very proactively. And now it seems like, with the new administration, this new administration has a different approach to content moderation and Meta is following that. And so what I've seen mostly from Meta over time is that they're quite willing to sort of navigate the political landscape and follow the lead of politicians here.
turns.zh: 老实说，我不太在意这些。对我来说，这感觉更像是某种延续，至少以 Meta 为例，因为他们基本上就是社交媒体市场。他们的选择真正有趣的地方在于，他们倾向于跟随政治风向。所以，当拜登担任总统时，马克对此非常公开，他们做了很多非常主动的内容审核。显然，当时白宫要求他们非常主动地这样做。而现在看来，随着新政府的上台，新政府对内容审核有不同的做法，Meta 正在跟随这一点。所以，我长期以来从 Meta 身上看到的主要是，他们非常愿意在政治格局中周旋，并跟随政客的引导。

turns.en: Is that something to be admired?
turns.zh: 这是值得钦佩的吗？

turns.en: No, I think it's a definitely a survivalist approach for sure when you're such a large and powerful company, right? You know, if you look at Meta, they have so much litigation with the government right now. The government is scrutinizing so many different aspects of their business. And so when you're at that scale and you're controlled by a single founder, I think it's a survivalist instinct that means that depending on who is in the White House, you change your policies.
turns.zh: 不，我认为当你是一家如此庞大而强大的公司时，这绝对是一种生存主义做法，对吧？你知道，如果你看看 Meta，他们现在与政府有那么多诉讼。政府正在审查他们业务的许多不同方面。所以当你达到那个规模，并且由单一创始人控制时，我认为这是一种生存本能，意味着根据谁在白宫，你改变你的政策。

turns.en: Are you optimistic about the next four years in America?
turns.zh: 你对美国未来四年感到乐观吗？

turns.en: I'm incredibly optimistic about our country. I love our country so much. I think that Americans across our country have an incredible spirit that has allowed us to overcome extraordinary challenges together. More recently the COVID pandemic, you know, longer ago, things like World War II, where we came together not only as a country but more broadly in the world to confront the horror of the Axis powers. I think our country in very critical and important moments comes together in really powerful ways and that's something that inspires me.
turns.zh: 我对我们的国家非常乐观。我非常热爱我们的国家。我认为我们全国的美国人都有一种不可思议的精神，使我们能够共同克服非凡的挑战。最近的新冠疫情，更早的比如第二次世界大战，我们不仅作为一个国家，而且更广泛地在全世界团结起来，对抗轴心国的恐怖。我认为我们的国家在非常关键和重要的时刻以非常有力的方式团结起来，这让我深受鼓舞。

turns.en: Your oldest child is 14, you said. He's at that age now where he's going to be getting increasing pressures to join social media. Are you going to let him join Instagram?
turns.zh: 你说你最大的孩子 14 岁了。他现在正处于面临越来越多加入社交媒体的压力的年龄。你会让他加入 Instagram 吗？

turns.en: He's on Snapchat. He's on Snapchat. Certainly on YouTube and Roblox, which he likes a lot. So that's sort of the current situation.
turns.zh: 他在用 Snapchat。他在用 Snapchat。当然也在用 YouTube 和 Roblox，他非常喜欢。所以这就是目前的情况。

turns.en: You must have thought about this. There's so much conversation at the moment around the impact that social media has on kids, anxiety, all of the sort of toxic things around comparison and becoming more isolated. Have you developed an agreement with your wife, with your kids, with your family about social media usage going forward?
turns.zh: 你一定考虑过这个问题。目前有很多关于社交媒体对孩子的影响的讨论，焦虑，所有那些关于比较和变得更加孤立的负面事情。你和你的妻子、孩子、家人就未来的社交媒体使用达成协议了吗？

turns.en: I think in general our view, you know, each of our children are so different.
turns.zh: 我认为总的来说，我们的观点是，你知道，我们的每个孩子都非常不同。

turns.en: They're going to develop in different ways. So, I don't think a one-size-fits-all model is the right approach here. I think it really depends on where each of our kids are at any given point and who they are and what they want to do. I think one thing I would really encourage them to be thoughtful about is their privacy, especially as young people. And I think there are a lot of young people at a very young age posting a lot of public content, and I think it's very important to be thoughtful about those sorts of decisions because once you've posted something publicly, you can't get that back. And I think it's really important, as we talk about technology, that we focus on the healthy and constructive ways that Flynn, for example, at 14, can use technology like staying in touch with his friends and family. I think the real watershed moment for us as a family was up until the COVID pandemic, we didn't allow Flynn to have a phone. We really didn't allow him to use a computer. When the pandemic happened, he had to stay in touch with his friends. He had to be connected with his friends. We knew that was vitally important for his well-being, right? And I think the challenge we have is almost the whiplash that young people are experiencing because throughout the pandemic, they were told you can only talk to your friends on the computer. You can only talk to your friends on the phone, right? And then coming out of the pandemic, what they're hearing a lot from adults now is stay off your phone. Don't use your phone at all. I think both extremes are unusual. For us as parents, we think a lot about what's a healthy relationship with technology. Of course, we want you to go run cross country and hang out with your friends, or go for a walk, go to the mall, and just talk. But we know when Flynn's not with his friends, when they're spread out all over the world, or after school trying to meet up, it's helpful to use technology. It's helpful to message your friends. And so I think we have to find this right balance of saying, cultivate a healthy, engaged lifestyle with all of your interests, your hobbies, your passions, and then if you want to use your phone to stay in touch with your friends or watch entertaining content or play a game to relax, that's healthy, too. Even if Finn says, 'I want to watch TikTok, Dad.' That might be a bridge too far. We say, because TikTok, I don't even use TikTok myself personally, because from what I hear, it's like crack cocaine for people, they're just on there for like three or four hours a day scrolling mindlessly. If Finn came home and said, 'I want to use TikTok, Dad,' you'd say no. We would probably say no. We have said no historically, although he hasn't really pressed the issue.
turns.zh: 他们会以不同的方式成长。所以，我不认为一刀切的模式是正确的做法。我认为这完全取决于每个孩子在任何特定时刻的状态，他们是谁，他们想做什么。我想真正鼓励他们思考的一件事是隐私，尤其是作为年轻人。我认为很多年轻人在很小的时候就发布大量公开内容，非常重要的是要慎重做出这类决定，因为一旦你公开了某些东西，就再也收不回来了。而且我认为，在我们谈论技术时，关注健康、建设性的使用方式非常重要，比如 Flynn 14 岁时可以用技术保持与朋友和家人的联系。对我们家来说，真正的分水岭时刻是直到新冠疫情之前，我们都不允许 Flynn 拥有手机。我们真的不允许他用电脑。疫情发生时，他必须与朋友保持联系。他必须与朋友保持连接。我们知道这对他的健康至关重要，对吧？我认为我们面临的挑战是年轻人经历的剧烈反差，因为在整个疫情期间，他们被告知只能通过电脑和朋友聊天。只能通过电话和朋友聊天，对吧？然后疫情结束后，他们从成年人那里听到的很多是：远离手机。根本不要用手机。我认为这两种极端都不正常。作为父母，我们思考了很多关于什么是与技术的健康关系。当然，我们希望你出去越野跑，和朋友一起玩，或者去散步，去商场，只是聊天。但我们知道，当 Flynn 不在朋友身边时，当他们分散在世界各地，或者放学后试图见面时，使用技术是有帮助的。给朋友发消息是有帮助的。所以我认为我们必须找到这个平衡点，说：培养一种健康、充实的生活方式，涵盖你所有的兴趣、爱好和热情，然后如果你想用手机与朋友保持联系，或者看娱乐内容，或者玩游戏放松，那也是健康的。即使 Finn 说：“爸爸，我想看 TikTok。”那可能就太过分了。我们说，因为 TikTok，我自己甚至都不用 TikTok，因为我听说它就像人们的可卡因，他们每天在上面无意识地刷三四个小时。如果 Finn 回家说：“爸爸，我想用 TikTok。”你会说不。我们可能会说不。我们历史上说过不，尽管他并没有真正坚持这个问题。

turns.en: TikTok was going to be banned and then Trump swooped in and seemed to save the day. Is that a good thing? As a CEO of Snapchat, was part of you hoping that it was banned because then maybe more people would come over and use Snap? Did you think about that?
turns.zh: TikTok 本来要被禁，然后特朗普突然出现，似乎挽救了局面。这是好事吗？作为 Snapchat 的 CEO，你是否有一部分希望它被禁，因为那样可能会有更多人过来用 Snap？你有没有想过这个？

turns.en: I think it would be quite good for our business if they were banned. I think the bigger picture that we really have to figure out as a country and in terms of our relationship with China is to figure out the areas where businesses are going to collaborate and do business across the United States and China and areas where they are not. So you're probably familiar, many technology companies cannot operate in China for a variety of reasons. Maybe they don't have a license, they haven't been allowed to operate, etc. But they are allowed to operate here in the United States where we have an open market, a free market. And I think we have to be very thoughtful at this point in time as a country because being an open market has always been a massive strategic advantage for the United States. It's something that free trade and things like that have been massively supportive of our economic growth. But we're now at a moment where I think we need to be thoughtful and say with some countries, free trade in some areas makes a lot of sense. So if we're talking about kids toys or diapers or you name it, right? Let it rip. That's good for both countries and both countries can do business in those areas. But when it comes to other areas, like information services, or maybe it's critical minerals, maybe it's some types of pharmaceutical compounds or ingredients. Those are areas where the countries aren't going to be able to collaborate because ultimately they have very different goals, ideologies, visions for the future. And I think the issue that the business community has right now is there's not enough clarity in that regard. So the more clarity the government can create and say, the United States and China working together can say, hey, we agree these areas are open for business and these areas are areas where we're going to compete and we're not going to collaborate. That would help the business community because I think what's so frustrating, imagine being a Chinese entrepreneur right now building this really successful company and then the US government saying, hey, given our country and our values and the strategic relationship we have with China, this is not going to work.
turns.zh: 我认为如果它们被禁，对我们的业务会相当有利。我认为更大的图景是，作为一个国家，就我们与中国的关系而言，我们必须弄清楚哪些领域的企业可以在美国和中国之间合作开展业务，哪些领域不能。你可能知道，许多科技公司由于各种原因无法在中国运营。也许他们没有许可证，或者不被允许运营等等。但它们可以在美国运营，这里有一个开放的市场，一个自由市场。我认为作为国家，我们现在必须非常深思熟虑，因为成为一个开放市场一直是美国的巨大战略优势。自由贸易之类的东西极大地支持了我们的经济增长。但我们现在到了一个时刻，我认为我们需要深思熟虑，并说对于某些国家，某些领域的自由贸易非常有意义。所以，如果我们谈论的是儿童玩具或尿布，或者任何你能想到的东西，对吧？那就放开吧。这对两国都有好处，两国都可以在这些领域做生意。但当涉及到其他领域时，比如信息服务，或者可能是关键矿产，可能是某些类型的药物化合物或成分。这些领域两国无法合作，因为最终它们有非常不同的目标、意识形态和对未来的愿景。我认为商界现在的问题是，在这方面没有足够的清晰度。所以政府能创造的清晰度越多，说美国和中国合作可以达成一致：嘿，我们同意这些领域是开放的，这些领域我们将竞争，不会合作。这将有助于商界，因为我认为最令人沮丧的是，想象一下现在一个中国企业家正在建立一家非常成功的公司，然后美国政府说，嘿，考虑到我们国家、我们的价值观以及我们与中国的战略关系，这行不通。

turns.en: It sounds like Trump wants to buy it, which was a very interesting suggestion, and it's worrying because it sets a bit of a precedent that potentially an app like Snapchat, the UK might decide, listen, we don't know if we can trust you because you're an American, so we want to buy the UK version in order for you to have Snapchat be in the UK. That could set a worrying precedent around the world.
turns.zh: 听起来特朗普想买下它，这是一个非常有趣的建议，而且令人担忧，因为它开创了一个先例，可能像 Snapchat 这样的应用，英国可能会决定：听着，我们不知道是否能信任你，因为你是美国人，所以我们想买下英国版本，以便你能让 Snapchat 在英国运营。这可能会在世界各地开创一个令人担忧的先例。

turns.en: I think there's already some early flavors of that with folks really focused on data localization and whatnot. And that's sort of my point around I think we need to get really clear about with which countries are we going to have open free flow of data and trade and which countries there are areas where that might not work as effectively.
turns.zh: 我认为已经有一些早期的迹象，人们非常关注数据本地化等等。这差不多就是我的观点，我认为我们需要非常清楚，与哪些国家我们将有开放的数据和贸易自由流动，哪些国家在某些领域可能无法有效运作。

turns.en: Snapchat eventually goes public. Running a public company is difficult, to say the least, because the share price can go up and down really irrespective of what you're doing and what you're building, and it's really a reflection of the broader market, people's emotions and vibes, but you have to manage that as a CEO. Not easy, I imagine.
turns.zh: Snapchat 最终上市了。经营一家上市公司至少可以说是困难的，因为股价可以上下波动，实际上与你正在做的事情和你正在构建的东西无关，它更多地反映了更广泛的市场、人们的情绪和氛围，但作为 CEO，你必须管理这一切。我想这并不容易。

turns.en: You know, a lot of people warned us about going public. And they said there was going to be a lot of pressure to be short-term oriented and this sort of thing. That the quarterly scrutiny would be challenging for our business.
turns.zh: 你知道，很多人警告过我们上市的事。他们说会有很大的压力要注重短期导向之类的事情。季度审查对我们的业务来说将是一个挑战。

transcript.secZh: 从私营到上市公司转型

transcript.sec: Transition from private to public company

turns.en: Ultimately, I think the transition from being a private company to a public company was challenging. It's quite different. But now I really think the discipline and the rigor around the quarterly performance, the need to forecast your business really effectively and then compare how you're tracking to your forecast, helps the company run in a much more effective way. So that sort of scrutiny can be really helpful for the leadership team and the broader team in terms of running the business. Now, where it can get difficult is when it comes to long-term investment and innovation. So, for example, right now interest rates have gone way up. Folks are discounting cash flows at a much higher rate as a result. And so there's a huge focus on profitability for many businesses across all sectors. What we know is true for long-term innovation is that consistency really matters. You can't just flick a switch and turn on and off innovation, turn on and off investments in new products. It's very difficult and disruptive to do that. And so we've made a decision through this period of time, even though we've made some really difficult and painful decisions to shut down some of our projects, we're still investing at a higher rate right now through this period of time, even though we know that that means that our share price might be lower because people are discounting our cash flows differently due to higher interest rates. So I think that's when it gets challenging, the actual reality of continuing to invest through challenging periods of time or periods where interest rates have gone way up.
turns.zh: 归根结底，我认为从私营公司转型为上市公司是充满挑战的，两者截然不同。但现在我确实认为，围绕季度业绩的纪律性和严谨性——即有效预测业务并与实际进展对比——能帮助公司以更高效的方式运营。因此，这种审视对领导团队和整个公司的业务管理都大有裨益。然而，困难之处在于长期投资和创新。例如，当前利率大幅上升，人们因此以更高的贴现率折现现金流，导致各行各业都极度关注盈利能力。我们知道，对于长期创新而言，一致性至关重要。你不能像开关一样随意开启或关闭创新，或中断对新产品的投资，这样做非常困难且具有破坏性。因此，尽管我们做出了关闭一些项目的艰难决定，但我们仍在这一时期保持较高投资率，即使这意味着股价可能更低，因为高利率改变了人们对现金流的折现方式。所以我认为，真正的挑战在于如何在困难时期或利率大幅上升时持续投资。

turns.en: When I think about sitting in your shoes or sitting in your seat, I think about all the things you could do as a public company. I think you could do anything, like you could go after any game, and at some point, as you kind of said there when you used the word painfully, you're going to have to make a decision to focus on something. And even at the level I'm at with the businesses I run and so on, I find the hardest thing for me is, especially when you're somewhat creative, etc., is to pick something and to say no to everything else. And I've looked at your philosophy and I know saying no and focus is so central to your leadership style, but also how you think as an entrepreneur. Tell me about those painful moments where you had to kill something that you didn't want to kill.
turns.zh: 当我设身处地为你考虑时，我会想到作为一家上市公司，你几乎无所不能，可以追逐任何目标。但正如你刚才提到“痛苦”这个词时所说，你终究要做出选择，专注于某件事。即使在我目前经营企业的层面，我也发现最难的事情——尤其是当你有些创意时——就是选定一件事，并对其他所有事情说“不”。我研究过你的理念，知道“说不”和“专注”是你领导风格的核心，也是你作为企业家的思维方式。请讲讲那些你不得不放弃自己不想放弃的东西的痛苦时刻。

turns.en: Yeah, there are a bunch. That piece of advice was so helpful to us, especially in the early days of our business. One of our first venture investors was like, 'Hey Evan, you got to get really good at saying no.' He said, 'You have almost no resources' — I think we were a team of four people at the time — 'and you're going to get all this inbound because the company's growing. People are going to want to do partnerships or do an interview or whatever. If you can just get really good at saying no and stay focused on your community, stay focused on your customers, that's the secret.' And that focus has really helped us over the years. But as you point out, there have been times where we've had to refocus or reassess areas of our business. I think one good example is mini games. We had an amazing hundreds of people using our mini games, and people loved them. It was an amazing platform. You could play real-time multiplayer games together inside of Snapchat. And ultimately, it was just clear that that was not going to be a really, really big business for us, at least at that time. And so we had to make the really painful decision to shut down our mini games business.
turns.zh: 是的，有很多这样的时刻。那条建议对我们帮助很大，尤其是在公司早期。我们的一位早期风险投资人曾说：“嘿，Evan，你必须非常擅长说‘不’。”他说：“你几乎没有任何资源”——当时我们团队只有四个人——“而且随着公司发展，你会收到大量请求。人们会想合作、采访或做其他事情。如果你能擅长说‘不’，并专注于你的社区和客户，这就是秘诀。”多年来，这种专注确实帮助了我们。但正如你所说，有时我们不得不重新聚焦或评估业务领域。一个很好的例子是迷你游戏。我们有数百万人使用我们的迷你游戏，大家都很喜欢。那是一个很棒的平台，你可以在 Snapchat 内玩实时多人游戏。但最终，很明显它不会成为我们的一项大业务，至少在当时不是。因此，我们不得不做出痛苦的决定，关闭迷你游戏业务。

turns.en: So how do you think about what to go after? There's all these new technologies. There's these buzzwords. There's AI now. There's AR. There's VR. There's headsets. There's wearables. There's all these things. How do you decide what bet is your bet?
turns.zh: 那么，你如何决定该追求什么？现在有这么多新技术、热词：AI、AR、VR、头显、可穿戴设备等等。你如何确定哪个赌注值得下？
