turns.en: I think that's a really good question. That is to some degree where intuition plays an important role, but it's also where feedback plays a really important role. And that's why, for example, with our last generation of spectacles that we announced last year, the fifth generation of spectacles, our goal was just to get it into developers' hands as quickly as possible so that we can listen and hear. Okay, so what sort of things do you want to build with spectacles? What tools are available? What isn't there? What do you think would be really interesting? Because the faster that we can learn from people actually using our product, the faster we can make it better and find that product-market fit.
turns.zh: 这是个很好的问题。在某种程度上，直觉很重要，但反馈同样关键。这就是为什么，例如，我们去年发布的第五代 Spectacles，目标就是尽快交到开发者手中，以便我们倾听和了解。比如，你想用 Spectacles 构建什么？有哪些工具可用？缺少什么？你认为什么会很有趣？因为我们从实际用户那里学习得越快，就能越快改进产品并找到产品市场契合点。

turns.en: That's so important. And you also don't know the time horizon for when the world will sufficiently change in the direction that your bet has been placed. I think about Google Glass, which was I don't even know when it was, like a decade ago that people were saying, 'Okay, we're going to be wearing glasses.' And Google had this Google Glass thing and it just seemed to vanish and disappear. And then I think about when Meta bought Oculus and we thought, okay, no, so this is now when everyone's going to be wearing VR headsets, and it's still kind of not really happened. So you could make a bet, you could be right, but you could be 15 years off. You have to be very careful in technology, I think, because things change slowly and then they change very quickly. And I think that was certainly the case with ChatGPT, right? People felt like, wow, this new technology came out of nowhere, but no, they've been working on it for what, a decade? I mean, consistently trying to make progress. And so I think, as long as you find something that you really believe can make a positive impact that people can use in a really compelling way, you're right that sometimes you have to be patient, but other times you can invent new things that bring that timeline in. And so I think a lot of times our team is thinking about like, okay, sure, on the current trajectory that could take a really long time, but what if we thought about it differently or invented some new piece of technology that could help us accelerate our vision to glasses that help people share these experiences that overlay computing on the world? And that's part of the fun too.
turns.zh: 这非常重要。而且你也不知道世界需要多久才会朝着你下注的方向充分改变。想想 Google Glass，大概是十年前吧，人们说：“好了，我们都要戴眼镜了。”Google 推出了 Google Glass，但它似乎很快就销声匿迹了。然后 Meta 收购了 Oculus，我们以为现在大家都会戴 VR 头显了，但至今仍未真正实现。所以，你可以下注，可能判断正确，但可能早了 15 年。我认为在科技领域必须非常谨慎，因为事情变化缓慢，然后突然加速。ChatGPT 显然就是如此，对吧？人们觉得这项新技术横空出世，但实际上他们已经研究了近十年，一直在持续进步。所以我认为，只要你找到自己坚信能产生积极影响、让人们以引人入胜的方式使用的东西，有时需要耐心，但有时你也可以发明新事物来缩短时间线。因此，我们的团队经常思考：在当前轨迹下，这可能需要很长时间，但如果我们换个思路，或者发明一些新技术来加速实现愿景——比如帮助人们分享叠加计算于世界的体验的眼镜——那也很有趣。

turns.en: And Meta launched the Ray-Ban ones which I've heard about. I think I watched a video of it, which seem to be again copying Snapchat. Did that piss you off?
turns.zh: Meta 推出了雷朋眼镜，我听说过，还看过视频，感觉又在抄袭 Snapchat。这让你生气吗？

turns.en: The only thing that frustrated me was that the Luxottica guys had actually come to us probably back in 2017.
turns.zh: 唯一让我沮丧的是，Luxottica 的人大概在 2017 年就找过我们。

turns.en: Who's that?
turns.zh: 那是谁？

turns.en: Luxottica is the company that makes Ray-Ban.
turns.zh: Luxottica 就是生产雷朋的公司。

turns.en: They had come to us in 2017 saying, 'Wow, it's so awesome what you guys are doing with Spectacles. We love it. We should find a way to partner.' So, we talked with them of course all about everything that we were doing. And then they went radio silent and decided not to partner with us and then obviously resurfaced doing this with Meta. So, I think ultimately as a is something like that. I think ultimately you know you learn a lot I think growing a business and really understanding how people do business and I think it shows you a lot about the world and I think it's so important for entrepreneurs to really know you know that if they've got a really compelling idea they've got an amazing service that they can compete that they can build really compelling businesses even though it seems impossible with such giant you know companies whether it's Luxottica which is the giant in the glasses space or or Meta um that I think Snapchat hopefully can be, you know, an example of a company that's been able to stay independent and compete with these really, really large businesses.
turns.zh: 他们在 2017 年找到我们，说：“哇，你们用 Spectacles 做的事太棒了，我们很喜欢，应该找个方式合作。”于是我们当然跟他们聊了我们正在做的一切。然后他们就沉默了，决定不跟我们合作，后来显然又跟 Meta 一起做了这件事。所以，我觉得最终就是这样。我认为，在经营企业和真正理解人们如何做生意方面，你能学到很多，这让你看清世界。对创业者来说，重要的是要知道，如果他们有一个真正有吸引力的想法和一项出色的服务，他们就能竞争，就能建立起真正有竞争力的企业，即使面对像 Luxottica（眼镜领域的巨头）或 Meta 这样的巨无霸看似不可能。我希望 Snapchat 能成为一个保持独立并与这些大公司竞争的榜样。

turns.en: Artificial intelligence has become, I mean, the most talked about technology over the last couple of years as it's in many respects, thanks to ChatGPT. How are you thinking about the future of artificial intelligence in terms of how it's going to fundamentally change human connection? You've got four boys. You must be thinking about, you know, we talked about earlier the kind of jobs that are going to exist in the future. There's a big narrative saying that knowledge jobs like lawyer and accountant aren't going to be the same. In fact, even when you think about how your kids are going to be educated, your youngest child is one year old. Are they going to go to a school or are they going to go to a large language model? Like, how are you thinking about that future? Are you scared?
turns.zh: 人工智能已经成为过去几年里最受热议的技术，这在很大程度上要归功于 ChatGPT。你如何看待人工智能的未来，它将如何从根本上改变人与人之间的联系？你有四个儿子，你一定在思考我们之前聊到的未来工作类型。有一种说法是，律师、会计师这类知识型工作将不复从前。甚至当你想到你的孩子将如何接受教育时——你最小的孩子才一岁——他们会去学校，还是去大语言模型那里？你是如何看待那个未来的？你害怕吗？

turns.en: I really love that you jumped to education because I think it's so profoundly powerful. I mean, even in my own experience, my ability to learn such amazing things in such a short period of time and connect different ideas together, it's an incredible tool for discovery and for learning. And so, I can't wait for our kids to, you know, use these sorts of tools. I'm sure Flynn does to some degree, but as a thought partner, AI is just incredibly powerful. So, I do think especially for creative people, it should be an unbelievably powerful tool to be able to iterate, to get feedback, to explore different ideas, explore different options. Even when I'm writing something and I'm stuck on like, yeah, this just doesn't feel right. And I'm like, give me can you just give me 10 options? It's really helpful to brainstorm, to find that right word.
turns.zh: 我真的很喜欢你跳到教育这个话题，因为我认为它非常强大。即使从我自己的经验来看，我在短时间内学习如此惊人的事物并将不同想法联系起来的能力，这真是一个用于发现和学习的不可思议的工具。所以，我迫不及待想让我们的孩子使用这类工具。我确信 Flynn 已经在某种程度上用了，但作为思考伙伴，AI 简直太强大了。所以，我认为特别是对创意人士来说，它应该是一个难以置信的强大工具，可以迭代、获得反馈、探索不同的想法和选项。甚至当我写东西卡住，觉得“嗯，这感觉不对”的时候，我就会说：“给我，你能给我 10 个选项吗？”它对头脑风暴、找到合适的词非常有帮助。

turns.en: I was wondering the other day when I was using ChatGPT or one of the programs a couple of days ago. I was wondering if I'm going to get worse at writing because this thing's now doing it for me. And writing is such a wonderful way to think and understand. So therefore, am I going to get worse at understanding things because I'm now deferring the process of thinking through something logically to this computer, whereas back in the day, I would have to really think deeply about what I was trying to say myself. I don't know. I think it's going to be really important that obviously people continue to write and oftentimes my first draft is on a piece of paper, right? So I do think that that is going to be important, but I think the bigger question for me is whether or not AI will help people get better at asking questions because ultimately asking a great question and having someone who can help answer it is the key to learning. I mean, that's I think, perhaps the greatest blessing of having a great teacher or a great mentor or a parent is that you get to ask all sorts of great questions, right? And get those answers. And so I think if we're now in a modality that really is all about asking the right question and doing that really repeatedly, if that can train us all to ask questions more effectively, that would be a very big deal.
turns.zh: 前几天我用 ChatGPT 或其他程序时就在想，我是不是会变得更不擅长写作，因为这东西现在替我做了。写作是一种极好的思考和理解的方式。那么，我是不是会变得更不擅长理解事物，因为我现在把逻辑思考的过程交给了电脑，而过去我必须自己深入思考我想表达的东西。我不知道。我认为显然人们继续写作会非常重要，而且我的初稿常常是在纸上写的，对吧？所以我认为那很重要，但对我来说更大的问题是，AI 是否能帮助人们更善于提问，因为最终，提出一个好问题并有人能帮你回答，是学习的关键。我认为，拥有一个好老师、好导师或好父母的最大福气，就是你能提出各种好问题，并得到答案。所以，如果我们现在处于一种真正关乎提出正确问题并反复练习的模式，如果这能训练我们更有效地提问，那将是一件大事。

turns.en: Interesting. I've not thought about that. I don't know if I'm getting better. I don't really know. It's really because there's always a trade-off with new technology. And the problem as we saw with social media is we often don't discover the trade-off until 15 years, 20 years time when it's really reared its ugly head cuz it's slow then it's fast. So I'm trying to understand if you're looking around the corner or looking over the horizon now to think through the trade-off of us hurtling into something which just like social media made something better, faster, cheaper, easier but came with an unintended consequence.
turns.zh: 有意思。我没想过这个。我不知道自己是不是在变得更好。我真的不知道。这是因为新技术总是有权衡。我们在社交媒体上看到的问题是，我们往往要等 15 年、20 年后，当它真正露出丑陋面目时才发现这种权衡，因为它起初很慢，然后突然加速。所以我想知道，你现在是否在拐角处或地平线上张望，思考我们一头扎进某个东西的权衡——就像社交媒体让某些事情变得更好、更快、更便宜、更容易，但带来了意想不到的后果。

turns.en: I think generally speaking as we have looked historically at the evolution of technology these sorts of foundational technologies you're right that they've been disruptive but they ultimately have massively positive and beneficial effects. I mean I think if you look at a foundational technology like the internet, a foundational technology maybe like the motor vehicle, an airplane, these are the sorts of foundational technologies that I think can really change the trajectory of the world and ultimately make people's lives better. I think the key will be how do we navigate that change together and that'll be something that will be really important to do thoughtfully. And I think in many ways the good news about this sort of technological change is it's always governed by people. I mean folks I think almost overly fixate on new technology developments and don't think enough about what is actually the human adoption curve look like? How are we making this something that's easier to use, easier for people to understand, easier for people to integrate into their lives, into their workflows. And so, I think a lot of the work for a big foundational technology like AI is going to be much more around how humans are interacting with it, interpreting it, understanding how it fits in their lives.
turns.zh: 我认为，总的来说，从历史上看技术的演变，这类基础性技术确实具有颠覆性，但最终会带来巨大的积极和有益影响。如果你看看像互联网这样的基础技术，或者像汽车、飞机这样的基础技术，我认为它们能真正改变世界的轨迹，最终让人们的生活变得更好。我认为关键将是我们如何共同应对这种变化，这需要深思熟虑。而且，从很多方面来说，这类技术变革的好消息是，它始终由人主导。我觉得人们几乎过度关注新技术的发展，而没有充分考虑人类采用曲线的实际情况。我们如何让这东西更容易使用、更容易理解、更容易融入人们的生活和工作流程？所以，我认为对于像 AI 这样的大型基础技术，很多工作将更多地围绕人类如何与之互动、解读它、理解它如何融入他们的生活。

turns.en: If you spend most days on your computer, you'll probably want to hear this. Some recent brain imaging studies have found that handwriting fires up more parts of the brain associated with creativity and critical thinking. But in digital workplaces, it's easy to lose the cognitive edge of handwriting. So, I want to introduce you to GoodNotes, a solution that combines both. I've used GoodNotes for a long, long time, well before they became a sponsor of this podcast. It's what I'm using every single time I'm sat here in the D of Sio on the iPad in front of me during every recording that I've pretty much ever done in the last couple of years.
turns.zh: 如果你大部分时间都坐在电脑前，你可能会想听听这个。最近一些脑成像研究发现，手写能激活大脑中与创造力和批判性思维相关的更多区域。但在数字工作场所，很容易失去手写的认知优势。所以，我想向你介绍 GoodNotes，一个将两者结合的解决方案。我使用 GoodNotes 已经很久很久了，远在他们成为本播客赞助商之前。在过去几年的每一次录制中，我坐在这里，面前的 iPad 上用的就是它。

turns.en: It holds my research, which I can annotate in real time, as well as jot down new ideas as they come to me as the guest is talking. Outside of the studio, I use it to mark up design concepts in meetings whenever I'm brainstorming and to capture other insights with my team. GoodNotes is like my second brain. It's the tool that helps me to unlock original ideas and sharper thinking. And their built-in AI lets me build on those ideas, too. For collaborative work, your team can get a business license or get it yourself. It only costs £9.99 a year. And they're offering our community a 30-day free trial. Just head to goodnotes.com/doac to get started.
turns.zh: 它存放着我的研究笔记，我可以实时批注，也能在嘉宾说话时随手记下新想法。在录音室外，我在会议中用脑暴时用它标注设计概念，并与团队捕捉其他洞见。GoodNotes 就像我的第二大脑，是帮助我激发原创想法和更锐利思考的工具。他们内置的 AI 还能让我在这些想法上继续构建。对于协作工作，你的团队可以购买商业许可证，或者你自己购买也行，每年只需 9.99 英镑。他们还为我们的社区提供 30 天免费试用。只需访问 goodnotes.com/doac 即可开始。

turns.en: No matter where I am in the world, it seems like everyone is drinking matcha. And there's a good chance that that matcha you're drinking is made by a company that I've invested more than seven figures in who are a sponsor of this podcast called Perfect Ted because they're the brand used globally by cafes like Blank Street Coffee and Joe in the Juice and many, many more. Not only can you get Perfect Ted Matcha in cafes, but you can now also make it at home much cheaper in seconds using our flavored matcha powders that I have here in front of me. Perfect Ted Matcha is ceremonial grade and sourced from Japan. It is smooth. It is naturally sweet, not like those bitter grassy matchas that I tried before. Perfect Ted. And if you are one of those people that have told yourself you don't like matcha, it's probably because you haven't tried our Perfect Ted Matcha. And you can find Perfect Ted Matcha in the UK in Tesco, Sainsbury's, Holland and Barrett and in Waitrose or Albert Heijn if you're in the Netherlands and on Amazon in the USA or get the full range online at perfectted.com where you can get 40% off your first order with code Steven40.
turns.zh: 无论我在世界哪个角落，似乎每个人都在喝抹茶。而你喝的那杯抹茶，很有可能就是由我投资了七位数的一家公司生产的，这家公司也是本播客的赞助商，叫 Perfect Ted，因为它们是 Blank Street Coffee、Joe in the Juice 等众多咖啡馆全球使用的品牌。你不仅可以在咖啡馆买到 Perfect Ted 抹茶，现在还可以用我面前这些调味抹茶粉，在家几秒钟内以更便宜的价格制作。Perfect Ted 抹茶是仪式级品质，源自日本，口感顺滑，天然微甜，不像我以前尝过的那些苦涩的草味抹茶。Perfect Ted。如果你曾告诉自己不喜欢抹茶，那很可能是因为你还没试过我们的 Perfect Ted 抹茶。你可以在英国的 Tesco、Sainsbury's、Holland and Barrett、Waitrose，荷兰的 Albert Heijn，美国的亚马逊找到 Perfect Ted 抹茶，或者访问 perfectted.com 选购全系列产品，使用优惠码 Steven40 首单可享 40% 折扣。

turns.en: What season is Snapchat in in terms of its company's life? You know, like you were in that startup phase, you're in your dad's bedroom phase where you're scrappy and you're growing quickly. Then you went to the blue office, you know, the meteoric growth, you had the IPO. What season is Snapchat in as we sit here today in 2025? How would you like to summarize it if you had to poetically describe the psychology of the business now?
turns.zh: 从公司生命周期来看，Snapchat 现在处于什么季节？你知道，你们经历过创业阶段，在父亲卧室里那种拼劲十足、快速成长的时期。然后搬进了蓝色办公室，经历了爆发式增长，完成了 IPO。那么到 2025 年的今天，Snapchat 处于什么季节？如果要用诗意的方式描述当前业务的心理状态，你会怎么总结？

turns.en: I mean, in some ways, it feels like we're emerging from like a two-year winter into an early spring. The last two years have been really challenging. We had to rebuild our entire ad platform, change the way that we go to market, you know, really help advertisers find more success and at the same time do a lot of that for creators as well. We've seen a tremendous growth in terms of, you know, I think last quarter the creators posting grew something like 40% year-over-year. There was a billion public posts a month on Snapchat. And that's an area we've invested in a lot as well. But it's been a very challenging last two years. So, I would almost say maybe like very very early spring you're starting to see uh you know some some green shoots but you know um and the frost is melting.
turns.zh: 从某些方面来说，感觉我们正从长达两年的寒冬步入早春。过去两年非常艰难，我们不得不重建整个广告平台，改变市场策略，真正帮助广告主取得更大成功，同时也要为创作者做很多类似的工作。我们看到了巨大的增长，上个季度创作者发帖量同比增长了大约 40%，Snapchat 上每月有 10 亿条公开帖子。这也是我们大力投资的领域。但过去两年确实充满挑战。所以，我几乎会说，也许是非常非常早的春天，你开始看到一些绿芽，霜冻正在融化。

turns.en: Have you had any acquisition offers since that conversation with Mark Zuckerberg once upon a time? Do people still try and buy the company like these days?
turns.zh: 自从当年和马克·扎克伯格那次谈话之后，你收到过收购要约吗？现在还有人试图收购这家公司吗？

turns.en: No. I think given the voting structure of the company, you know, Bobby and I have voting stock and non-voting stock is what's publicly traded. I think generally, you know, sometimes people will say, "Hey, if you guys ever, you know, want to retire or something, keep us in mind." But I think in terms of kicking an offer over the sill or something that doesn't make a ton of sense given our company structure.
turns.zh: 没有。考虑到公司的投票结构，鲍比和我持有投票权股票，而非投票权股票是公开交易的。一般来说，有时人们会说：“嘿，如果你们哪天想退休什么的，记得考虑我们。”但就正式提出收购要约而言，鉴于我们的公司结构，这没什么意义。

turns.en: Another thing that I admired when I was reading about the way you run Snapchat is this idea of having a council. Oh, amazing. Wow. Okay. Can you tell me about this because I might want to steal. I shouldn't. Please take it. Okay. Good.
turns.zh: 我在阅读你管理 Snapchat 的方式时，另一个让我钦佩的是设立“议事圈”这个想法。哦，太棒了。哇。好吧。你能给我讲讲这个吗？因为我可能想借鉴一下。我不该这么说。请尽管用。好的。

turns.en: Council is something that I stole from the school that I went to growing up called Crossroads School for Arts and Sciences, which is quite a unique school. And one of the things that they have at Crossroads is a council. And you know, basically starting in middle school, you get together with a group of let's call it 10 or 12 classmates and you sit in a circle and there are three rules. It's you know, speak from the heart, listen from the heart and be spontaneous. And it's essentially turn-based storytelling where you go around the circle and you know it can be anything as simple as like how was your weekend or you know what's a rose bud and a thorn from you know the last week and it really creates an opportunity for people to listen to one another because you're taking turns going around the circle but also you get to know people in a very very different way. I saw how powerful it was in middle school. Middle school is a tough time was a tough time for me, but in council I was able to connect with my classmates in a really thoughtful and maybe deeper way that you wouldn't just kind of around the water cooler or whatever at the office. And so for us ultimately when we came to LA and we were in the blue house, one of the big decisions we were confronting at the time was whether or not we should move the company from LA to the Bay Area. And there was a lot of pressure, you know, hey, all the tech talent is in the Bay Area. It's really important for you guys to be up there for the talent. And so we just got our team together and had our first council and everyone went around the circle and shared their thoughts. Should we stay in LA or not? How did they feel about that? What did they think? And what was so clear coming out of that conversation we didn't even need to make a decision. It was just obvious that we believed in LA and wanted to be in LA and the team thought it was actually something really important to our business, to our identity, to actually the way that we hired talent because people had to really commit to moving to LA to be a part of the company. So that was the first time we used council at Snap and I saw how effective it was in the workplace setting. Then as the business got a lot bigger and it became much more important to connect with people who were new to the company or worked in a different area of the company, council became just a really useful tool for doing that. And so at Snap we have council facilitators whose job is to run these councils and now many more companies are interested in doing this and we also help train companies or offer sessions for other companies to help bring their team members together.
turns.zh: 议事圈是我从我小时候上的学校——十字路口艺术与科学学校——偷来的，那是一所非常独特的学校。他们在十字路口学校就有议事圈。基本上从中学开始，你会和大约 10 到 12 个同学聚在一起，坐成一个圆圈，有三条规则：发自内心地说话，发自内心地倾听，以及保持自发。这本质上是一种轮流讲故事的方式，大家围着圆圈轮流发言，内容可以很简单，比如周末过得怎么样，或者上周的“玫瑰、花蕾和刺”（即好事、期待的事和难事）。它创造了一个机会，让人们互相倾听，因为你们轮流发言，同时也能以非常不同的方式了解彼此。我在中学时就看到了它的力量。中学对我来说是一段艰难的时期，但在议事圈里，我能够以一种深思熟虑的、可能更深入的方式与同学建立联系，这是你在办公室饮水机旁做不到的。所以，当我们最终来到洛杉矶，住在蓝色房子里时，我们面临的一个重大决定是是否要把公司从洛杉矶搬到湾区。压力很大，因为所有科技人才都在湾区，对你们来说，为了人才搬到那里非常重要。于是我们召集团队，举行了第一次议事圈，每个人围坐一圈分享想法：我们应不应该留在洛杉矶？他们对此感觉如何？他们怎么想？那次谈话的结果非常清晰，我们甚至不需要做决定。很明显，我们相信洛杉矶，想留在洛杉矶，团队认为这对我们的业务、我们的身份、甚至我们招聘人才的方式都非常重要，因为人们必须真正承诺搬到洛杉矶才能成为公司的一员。那是我们第一次在 Snap 使用议事圈，我看到了它在工作环境中的有效性。后来业务变得更大，与新人或公司不同部门的人建立联系变得更加重要，议事圈就成了一个非常有用的工具。所以在 Snap，我们有议事圈引导师，他们的工作就是主持这些议事圈。现在很多公司对此感兴趣，我们也帮助培训其他公司，或者为他们提供会议，帮助他们凝聚团队成员。

turns.en: What is the essence of it? It's just get a small team in a certain department around a table and let everybody speak from the heart, listen from the heart and be spontaneous.
turns.zh: 它的本质是什么？就是让某个部门的一个小团队围坐在桌子旁，让每个人都发自内心地说话、发自内心地倾听，并且保持自发。

transcript.sec: Sitting in a circle fosters equality

turns.en: And it's usually not sitting around a table. It's usually sitting on the floor in a circle, which again I think helps create that feeling. You know, when you're sitting in a circle, everyone is situated equally, which I think is a really important thing. As you mentioned, companies have a lot of hierarchy. I think it feels really different in a company when everyone sees that seat around a circle. And everyone's voice is important and everyone's voice is heard. You know, whether it's just saying, 'Wow, I you know, that was a really tough weekend' or 'Actually I had an amazing time. I went out to dinner with my wife. It was fabulous.' And I think people find new points of connection that they maybe wouldn't have found otherwise without it.
turns.zh: 而且通常不是围坐在桌子旁，而是坐在地板上围成一个圈，这我认为有助于营造那种感觉。你知道，当你围坐成一圈时，每个人的位置都是平等的，我认为这非常重要。正如你提到的，公司里有很多层级。我觉得在公司里，当每个人都看到那个圈里的座位时，感觉真的很不一样。每个人的声音都很重要，每个人的声音都会被听到。无论是说‘哇，那个周末真难熬’，还是‘其实我过得很棒，和妻子出去吃了晚餐，太棒了’。我认为人们会找到新的连接点，否则可能不会发现这些。

turns.en: Interesting. As a leader, how do you know in those situations whether to listen to your team or not to listen to your team? Because it sounds quite risky for a founder, I'm not saying this is what you do, but for a founder to run their company on consensus, i.e. making sure everybody agrees on something. And we're actually seeing this in the post-pandemic world with this whole remote work debate where some companies originally were okay everybody's going to be remote and then it went back to a lot of companies are like no come back into the office. I mean if you ask a team they're probably not going to all say let's run back to the office but as a CEO you have to make a call. And what is the remote policy with Snap at the moment? We are more than four days a week in the office on average. Is that by policy or is that just what's happening?
turns.zh: 有意思。作为领导者，在那些情况下，你怎么知道是该听从团队还是不该听从团队？因为对创始人来说，这听起来风险很大——我不是说你会这样做——但靠共识来运营公司，也就是确保每个人都同意某件事。我们在后疫情时代也看到了这一点，围绕远程工作的争论中，一些公司最初允许所有人远程，后来很多公司又要求回到办公室。如果你问团队，他们可能不会都说‘我们回办公室吧’，但作为 CEO，你必须做出决定。目前 Snap 的远程政策是什么？我们平均每周在办公室超过四天。这是政策规定还是实际发生的情况？

turns.en: That is by policy and also what's happening.
turns.zh: 这是政策规定，也是实际发生的情况。

turns.en: Okay. And did you ever move on that? Was there ever a moment in the pandemic where you thought during the pandemic I thought I would never go back to the office.
turns.zh: 好的。你在这件事上动摇过吗？疫情期间有没有某个时刻，你觉得自己再也不会回办公室了？

turns.en: I was like, you know, going into the pandemic, I was waking up before our kids woke up. I was getting home after they were asleep. There was a moment I was like, what am I doing with my life? I'm never seeing our kids. What am I going to do? And the pandemic happened and it was like a miracle. I'm like, oh my god, I get to see our kids every single day. I get to wake up and see our kids. And I have an open door policy. If I'm working from home in my home office, our kids can come in anytime. It was only a problem once when one of our boys came in fully nude with two Oreos, which actually prompted me to consider going back to the office. But I really thought it was important for our kids that, hey, if I'm at home, I'm not like shut away in my office. You can come in anytime, you know, with anything and I'll help you out. Sometimes it meant they spent a lot of time sitting on my lap in meetings. But in any case, there was a period of time in the pandemic where I was like, why would I ever go back to the office? I'm here with my family. But I think that the adrenaline and the teamwork that happened during the pandemic when we were all able to work together really effectively remotely was only possible because we had been working together physically for such a long period of time. We had all of that trust built. We had all that shorthand built. We had that many times long roadmaps of ideas we had come up with to get physically together and that really sustained the company through that period of time. And it became clear to me that the culture was starting to fray. People don't learn the culture as quickly when they're alone and remote, separated all around the world. And I was really worried about our ability to consistently be creative, which is so important to our business, if we weren't physically together. So ultimately, especially after that Oreo incident, we thought it was pretty important to get back to the office.
turns.zh: 我当时想，进入疫情前，我总是在孩子醒来前起床，在他们睡着后才回家。有那么一刻，我心想：我到底在干什么？我几乎见不到孩子。我该怎么办？然后疫情来了，就像奇迹一样。我心想：天哪，我每天都能见到孩子了。我醒来就能看到他们。而且我实行开放政策，如果我在家办公，孩子们随时可以进来。只有一次出了问题，一个儿子全裸着拿着两块奥利奥走进来，这促使我考虑回办公室。但我真的认为对孩子来说，如果我在家，我不应该把自己关在办公室里。你可以随时进来，带着任何东西，我会帮你。有时这意味着他们在会议中长时间坐在我腿上。但无论如何，疫情期间有一段时间，我心想：我为什么要回办公室？我和家人在一起。但我认为，疫情期间我们能够高效远程协作的那种激情和团队合作，之所以成为可能，是因为我们之前有很长时间的面对面合作。我们建立了信任，建立了默契，我们多次共同制定长期路线图，这些都是在面对面时产生的，这支撑了公司度过那段时期。我逐渐意识到文化开始瓦解。当人们独自远程、分散在世界各地时，他们学习文化的速度会变慢。我真的很担心如果我们不在一起，我们持续创新的能力——这对我们的业务至关重要——会受到影响。所以最终，尤其是在奥利奥事件之后，我们认为回办公室非常重要。

turns.en: How was that received?
turns.zh: 这个决定反响如何？

turns.en: One of the things that we tried to do that helped team members is just give a pretty long runway. We made that decision pretty early on and then gave team members quite an extended period of time. I think it was like six or nine months for folks who had extenuating circumstances. We would grant exceptions and over time that allowed people to adapt their lives. Sometimes they'd rented a house or bought a house somewhere else and needed to move back to one of our hub office locations. And so, we wanted to give people enough flexibility to do that, not just have them wake up one morning and say, 'Come back to the office.' That's not super thoughtful.
turns.zh: 我们尝试做的一件事是给团队成员一个相当长的缓冲期。我们很早就做出了决定，然后给了团队成员相当长的时间。我认为对于有特殊情况的人，大概是六到九个月。我们会批准例外，随着时间的推移，这让人们能够调整自己的生活。有时他们在别处租了或买了房子，需要搬回我们的一个中心办公室。所以我们想给人们足够的灵活性来做到这一点，而不是让他们某天早上醒来就说‘回办公室’。那样不太体贴。

turns.en: For any entrepreneurs that are out there now listening to our conversation and they're at the very beginning of their journey and they are thinking about so many different things, so many different problems, their products aren't working, their customers are complaining. When you think about the principles of being successful as an entrepreneur that are transferable across all industries, have you defined what those principles are in your mind to be successful in any endeavor? We talked about some of them already. We said about culture, we said about hiring. Is there anything else that you've come to learn in your wisdom that entrepreneurs like me should be thinking a lot about as fundamental principles of success?
turns.zh: 对于现在正在听我们对话的创业者，他们处于旅程的最初阶段，思考着各种各样的事情和问题，产品不奏效，客户在抱怨。当你思考作为创业者成功的、可跨行业迁移的原则时，你心中是否已经定义了那些在任何努力中成功的原则？我们已经谈了一些，比如文化和招聘。你还有什么智慧心得，认为像我这样的创业者应该作为成功的基本原则来深思？

turns.en: To me, it seems like the biggest differentiator is how much you care. I mean that just seems day in and day out as I meet entrepreneurs and people working on businesses. How much do you care about your business, your team, your customer? And those are the entrepreneurs I think that are really successful. They go that extra mile and that care can come from different places, right? It can be about the impact that people want to make in the world. It can be about something that people really want to invent. It can be their love of their customers and seeing the smile on their customers' faces. But how much people care about what they do seems to me to be quite a large predictor, if not the predictor of success.
turns.zh: 对我来说，最大的区别在于你在乎的程度。我日复一日地见到创业者和做生意的人，你在乎你的业务、你的团队、你的客户吗？我认为那些真正成功的创业者就是这样。他们愿意多走一步，而这种在乎可以来自不同的地方，对吧？可以是人们想对世界产生的影响，可以是人们真正想发明的东西，可以是他们对客户的爱以及看到客户脸上的笑容。但人们对自己所做之事的在乎程度，在我看来是成功的一个很大预测因素，甚至可能是最重要的预测因素。

turns.en: Can you care too much?
turns.zh: 会不会在乎得太多？

turns.en: I don't think so.
turns.zh: 我不这么认为。

turns.en: Sounds stressful though caring that much. I thought in your book you talk about...
turns.zh: 不过那么在乎听起来压力很大。我记得你在书里谈到……

turns.en: Don't talk about my book.
turns.zh: 别谈我的书。

turns.en: I'm not disagreeing. I'm just playing devil's advocate. That's one of the things I loved about your book is you said, 'Hey, people are thinking about stress wrong.' Which I thought was really powerful. Like I wish more people talked about it that way because I think you know... Anyways, you wrote it. You don't even need to.
turns.zh: 我不是不同意，我只是在唱反调。你的书里有一点我很喜欢，你说‘嘿，人们对压力的理解是错误的。’我觉得这非常有力。我希望更多人能这样谈论它，因为我觉得……总之，你写了它，你甚至不需要……

turns.en: No, but it's a good point. Something I was going to ask you about is the stress of being you and do you have techniques to manage that stress, especially running a public company? I just think it's psychotic.
turns.zh: 不，但这是个好问题。我本来想问你的是，作为你，压力很大，你有没有管理压力的技巧，尤其是经营一家上市公司？我只是觉得这很疯狂。

turns.en: Well, what I thought was hilarious. So, this has been one of my hilarious findings from my ring over the last couple of days trying it out. I finally had enough days that it like gave me a stress score or whatever, and I'm just not stressed during the day, which really lines up with like how I experience work. I don't find work to be very stressful. I think a lot of it has become very normal because you know over the years we've grown our business and encountered all sorts of wild situations that at this point it's just a daily normal thing.
turns.zh: 嗯，我觉得好笑的是——这是我最近几天试用戒指时发现的一个搞笑结果。我终于攒够了天数，它给了我一个压力分数之类的，而我白天根本不觉得有压力，这和我对工作的感受非常一致。我不觉得工作有多大压力。我觉得很多事都变得非常平常了，因为你知道，多年来我们发展了业务，遇到了各种疯狂的情况，到现在这已经是日常了。

turns.en: Um, do you celebrate? Do you get really happy when you have professional moments where I don't know, you launch a new feature and it's well received. Do you get really happy?
turns.zh: 嗯，你会庆祝吗？当你有职业上的高光时刻，比如推出一个新功能并且反响很好，你会非常开心吗？

turns.en: No, it's something that I need to work on, especially celebrating our team as well. Like I, you know, just providing more of that really positive feedback. That's not something I do a ton, especially around outcome focused goals. When I see a great idea, if I see a great new idea, then I get really happy and excited. I love it. But, you know, to me, uh, you know, some of these big corporate milestones, like the growth of the community is cool. I was talking to someone the other day. They were like, "You should throw a party when you guys reach a billion people." I was like, "Oh my god, what a great idea." Like that. Why didn't I think of it? Um, so I think we should celebrate things like that.
turns.zh: 不会，这是我需要改进的地方，尤其是庆祝团队成就。比如，你知道，多给一些真正积极的反馈。这不是我特别擅长的事，尤其是在以结果为导向的目标上。当我看到一个好主意，一个很棒的新想法时，我会非常开心和兴奋。我喜欢那样。但是，你知道，对我来说，一些大的公司里程碑，比如社区的增长，很酷。前几天我和一个人聊天，他们说：“你们应该在有十亿用户的时候开个派对。”我说：“天哪，真是个好主意。”就像那样。我怎么没想到呢？嗯，所以我觉得我们应该庆祝这类事情。

turns.en: I wonder if there's an element of defense to this because I was speaking to a lot of founders recently and they were telling me how they've over time and with maturity, they almost just developed this calm within all the chaos where they're not moved up or down. And some of them make the case to me that if you are moved up by something that happens externally, it's impossible therefore not to be moved down when something bad happens externally. So founders develop this almost like coldness to them. That would be a real problem for me because so much of creating products is about connecting with people and listening to people and being able to empathize with them. So like I absolutely under no circumstances can cut off my emotional response.
turns.zh: 我在想这是否有一种防御成分，因为我最近和很多创始人聊过，他们告诉我，随着时间的推移和成熟，他们几乎在混乱中培养出了一种平静，情绪不会大起大落。他们中有些人向我论证，如果你因为外部事件而情绪高涨，那么当坏事发生时，你就不可能不情绪低落。所以创始人几乎变得有些冷漠。这对我来说会是个大问题，因为创造产品很大程度上是关于与人连接、倾听他们并与之共情。所以我绝对不能在任何情况下切断我的情绪反应。

turns.en: I think, you know, I pick the things or, I you know, the things that make me feel really happy are things like being with our children or something or, you know, Hart did really well on his math test the other day and I was like, "Awesome." You know what I mean? I got super excited about that. But I think to your point, one of the things I do regret at some point is not celebrating some of those great moments. I think, you know, sometimes as an entrepreneur when everything is like going up to up and to the right and going super well, you're always like, "What's going to go wrong?" You know, what could go wrong? And so you don't think about celebrating that great moment because you're thinking about, you know, the next day or or what you could be doing differently to make sure the business can keep growing. And I think breaking out of that like, you know, what could go wrong, which actually is quite helpful. That paranoia is probably pretty helpful. But celebrate those moments is important. So that's a good takeaway from our chat.
turns.zh: 我觉得，你知道，我选择那些让我真正开心的事，比如和孩子们在一起，或者哈特前几天数学考得很好，我说：“太棒了。”你懂我的意思吗？我对此超级兴奋。但我想，就你的观点而言，我确实后悔没有庆祝一些伟大的时刻。你知道，有时候作为创业者，当一切都在上升、向右、进展顺利时，你总是想：“会出什么问题？”你知道，可能出什么问题？所以你不会想着庆祝那个伟大时刻，因为你想着第二天，或者你可以做些什么不同的事来确保业务持续增长。我认为打破那种“会出什么问题”的思维很重要，实际上它很有帮助。那种偏执可能很有用。但庆祝那些时刻很重要。所以这是我们聊天的一个好收获。

turns.en: Was there a hardest day for you? A day when you were challenged the most as the CEO of Snapchat that comes to mind when I say that?
turns.zh: 对你来说，有没有最艰难的一天？作为 Snapchat 的 CEO，你最具挑战性的一天是什么？

turns.en: I think some of the hardest days, the painful days have been when we've had to make changes to our company structure, things like layoffs. I mean I feel like just a huge sense of responsibility to our team members and so when we let them down like that, you know that's those days are the worst. I mean that's you know of course in many cases worse for them and I you know but as a leader the sense of shame I feel when we have to make a decision like that that's you know that's sucks.
turns.zh: 我认为一些最艰难、最痛苦的日子是当我们不得不改变公司结构时，比如裁员。我觉得对团队成员有巨大的责任感，所以当我们让他们失望时，那些日子是最糟糕的。当然，在很多情况下，对他们来说更糟，但作为领导者，当我们不得不做出那样的决定时，我感到的羞耻感真的很糟糕。

turns.en: Do you ever have imposter syndrome because I think about the odds of you the odds of launching a social media communications application as we said earlier like a billion in one or something great I don't know this it's staggering and I guess it's not a billion there's not been a billion of them but the odds are just against you. So when that happens and it explodes and it becomes this major global app, is there not any feelings of imposter syndrome?
turns.zh: 你是否有过冒名顶替综合征？因为我想，你推出一个社交媒体通讯应用的概率，就像我们之前说的，十亿分之一之类的，我不知道，这太惊人了。我猜不是十亿，因为并没有十亿个这样的应用，但概率确实对你很不利。所以当它发生并爆发，成为全球性的大应用时，难道没有冒名顶替的感觉吗？

turns.en: I don't like the word imposter syndrome because it doesn't sound very nice. And I think imposter syndrome is actually a good thing in the sense that it means that you feel like there's more to learn, right? And so like for me, you know, as I approach any situation or, you know, any meeting or, you know, anything that we're trying to do out in the world, I'm always trying to think like what else could I learn here? I obviously, you know, this is an opportunity for me to really listen to learn to figure out how I can grow. And so like I never want to feel like, oh, I you know, I've got this. I always want to feel like what else could I learn? What could I be doing differently? You know, how could I grow? And I think sometimes when we call it imposter syndrome, that's not super helpful. I think we should be telling people, hey, it's a good thing if you feel like you've got more to learn. It's a good thing if you feel like, hey, maybe this isn't totally normal to be running a big company, right? Maybe it's a good thing to stay open to different ideas or ways of doing things.
turns.zh: 我不喜欢“冒名顶替综合征”这个词，因为它听起来不太好。而且我认为冒名顶替综合征实际上是一件好事，因为它意味着你觉得还有更多东西要学，对吧？所以对我来说，当我面对任何情况、任何会议或我们在世界上做的任何事情时，我总是想：我还能在这里学到什么？显然，这是一个真正倾听、学习、弄清楚如何成长的机会。所以我从不想觉得“哦，我搞定了”。我总是想：我还能学到什么？我能做些什么不同的事？我如何成长？我认为有时我们称之为冒名顶替综合征，这并没有太大帮助。我们应该告诉人们：嘿，如果你觉得自己还有很多要学，那是好事。如果你觉得经营一家大公司并不完全正常，那也是好事。也许对不同想法或做事方式保持开放是件好事。

turns.en: If Snapchat goes away today, what does Evan end up doing?
turns.zh: 如果今天 Snapchat 消失了，Evan 最终会做什么？

turns.en: Starting a new company. I would probably continue a lot of the work that we've been doing as a family to give back. I mean, I think that's been like the greatest blessing of this whole SNAP experience is being able to give back. Um, you know, we've done a lot as a family. We've done a lot with SNAP and the Snap Foundation. And like that to me is like, you know, hopefully the rest of my life is that story.
turns.zh: 创办一家新公司。我可能会继续我们作为家庭所做的很多回馈工作。我的意思是，我认为整个 Snap 经历中最大的福气就是能够回馈。嗯，你知道，我们作为家庭做了很多。我们通过 Snap 和 Snap 基金会做了很多。对我来说，那将是我余生希望讲述的故事。

turns.en: You wouldn't want to start another tech company?
turns.zh: 你不想再创办一家科技公司吗？

turns.en: Never in a million years.
turns.zh: 一百万年也不会。

turns.en: Really? No chance.
turns.zh: 真的吗？没机会？

turns.en: No chance. Why? Um, it's way too hard. Way too hard. I told you it was psychotic. I could have told you that when you started it. You should have asked me.
turns.zh: 没机会。为什么？嗯，太难了。太难了。我告诉过你这是疯狂的。我本可以在你开始时就告诉你。你应该问我的。

turns.en: Whenever I meet a serial entrepreneur, I'm like, "What?" What when you say it's hard? I asked this question. I paused on it because I actually posted about this on my Instagram and my Snap this morning about how hard it is and how nobody talks about that. And so when you experience the hardship as a founder, you kind of look in the mirror and think it's you. Do you know what I mean? You think, "Oh, this is evidence of my inadequacy." But it really I mean, why do you say that? Because it sounds like you have PTSD.
turns.zh: 每当我遇到连续创业者，我都会想：“什么？”你说很难是什么意思？我问过这个问题。我停顿了一下，因为我今天早上在 Instagram 和 Snap 上发了帖子，说这有多难，以及没人谈论这个。所以当你作为创始人经历困难时，你会照镜子，觉得问题出在自己身上。你懂我的意思吗？你会想：“哦，这证明我不够格。”但真的，我是说，你为什么这么说？因为听起来你有创伤后应激障碍。

transcript.secZh: 将压力转化为积极成长

turns.en: No, it's more I think like the hard kind of to your point about how do you turn stress into something positive, right?
turns.zh: 不，我觉得更像是你说的那种——如何把压力转化为积极的东西，对吧？

turns.en: The hard is a good thing in the sense that like what makes it so fun but also so challenging is the rate at which you have to change and grow. Like that is what has been so unbelievably hard, right? That you know the business at four people is really different than the business at 100 people. The business when we're supporting a million people is different than the business supporting 850 million people using our service. And to have to change so much over that period of time, to have to grow so much over that period of time, like that's what's hard. Like, because you just have to force yourself to change and grow and think about what you you know, how do I how do I need to adapt to to be the the person that our business needs six months from now, which inevitably will be different than than who I am today.
turns.zh: 困难是件好事，因为让这件事既有趣又充满挑战的，正是你必须改变和成长的速度。那才是真正难以置信的困难，对吧？你知道，四个人的公司和一百人的公司完全不同。当我们支持一百万人时的业务，和支持八亿五千万用户时的业务也不一样。在这段时间里必须改变那么多、成长那么多，那才是困难所在。因为你必须强迫自己改变和成长，思考如何适应，成为公司六个月后需要的那个人——那必然和今天的我不同。

turns.en: So, do you think you could run Snap for the rest of your life?
turns.zh: 那么，你觉得你能一辈子经营 Snap 吗？

turns.en: I would certainly be an honor. I mean, I'd love that. Maybe you'd leave Snap and then you'd get bored and then you'd start some new company. Who knows?
turns.zh: 那当然是一种荣幸。我是说，我很乐意。也许你会离开 Snap，然后觉得无聊，再创办一家新公司。谁知道呢？

turns.en: And we have a closing tradition on this podcast where the last guest leaves a question for the next guest not knowing who they're leaving it for. And the question that has been left for you interesting. Feel like I may have asked this before, but you're going to have to do your very best. What is the hardest thing you ever had to overcome?
turns.zh: 我们播客有一个结束传统，上一位嘉宾会给下一位嘉宾留一个问题，不知道留给谁。留给你的这个问题很有意思。感觉我可能以前问过，但你必须尽力回答。你曾经克服过的最困难的事情是什么？

turns.en: Yeah, I think that the hardest thing, maybe this is a good segue from what we were just talking about. The hardest thing I've ever had to overcome is myself, right? I've constantly had to force myself at every stage to grow and change and be different and evolve to meet the needs of our business and our community or my family. And I think, you know, that that's the that's the the battle with yourself to become a better version of yourself every day. That that that's a tough one.
turns.zh: 是的，我觉得最困难的事情——也许这正好承接我们刚才聊的——我克服过的最困难的事情就是我自己，对吧？我一直在每个阶段强迫自己成长、改变、变得不同、进化，以满足我们业务、社区或家庭的需求。我认为，那就是与自己的斗争，每天成为更好的自己。那确实很难。

turns.en: Self-awareness. I was thinking about that as you were just saying that about the idea of self-awareness as a CEO and how you develop that because it's such an important thing when there's so much counting on you being aware. So I don't know how you think about self-awareness as a leader and if there's any system you've had to cultivate that awareness that's been productive.
turns.zh: 自我意识。你刚才说的时候我就在想，作为 CEO 的自我意识，以及如何培养它——因为当有那么多事情依赖你的觉察时，这非常重要。所以我不知道你作为领导者如何看待自我意识，以及是否有任何系统帮助你培养这种意识，并且是有效的。

turns.en: I love that you said that. I think um it's so challenging and it becomes harder and harder I think as the business grows and you grow as a leader a because you become busier. So it's harder to tune in and really connect with people right in the way that you really need to to understand how they really feel or what they're thinking and to create a trusted relationship so they feel like they can tell that to you. As the company grows, you know, I think people become very focused on curating the information that you're receiving. So you're constantly getting a lot of reporting that you know shows leaders and their teams in a very positive light. And so you have to think about proactively breaking that because that will be the default that the organization I think will do. All of a sudden they will just try to make sure you're receiving information, right? That that shows them in a in a great light because they want to be successful. It makes perfect sense. But I think you have to really do a lot of work to break that and to get out deeper in the organization and just talk to people and I think there's no substitute. I wish there was, but there's really no substitute to just walking around and talking to people. And I found that that's an unbelievable way in our organization to just get great information really quickly. And then you see as a CEO, someone's working on a presentation, they're like, "Oh, yeah, this presentation will get to you in like six weeks, you know." Um, but yeah, sure. I can show you a little bit bit of it right now. You know, because the way that uh you know, calendars work as a a as a CEO and business reviews work and that sort of thing, all this sort of information, I think, you know, ends up flowing in a way that's just slower than it than it did in the in the beginning of the company. So, I think, you know, really taking the time to connect with people and form those trusted relationships. Uh being really proactive about breaking the information system that will form around you, right, if you're not more deliberate about going and getting other sources of information. That's that's really important. And then you know I think just you know that empathy and intuition really helps because sometimes people feel uncomfortable saying how they really feel and it's only because you know you just notice something in their eye or their affect or whatever it is that you're like is that you know is that really how you feel you know we should or should we really be doing this differently and I think you know the ability to really understand how how people feel and create a space for them to actually share their perspective it just you know is so so valuable.
turns.zh: 我很高兴你提到这个。我认为这非常具有挑战性，而且随着业务增长和作为领导者的成长，它会变得越来越难，因为你变得更忙了。所以更难去真正调频、与人建立联系，以你需要的方式理解他们的真实感受或想法，并建立信任关系，让他们觉得可以告诉你这些。随着公司壮大，人们会非常专注于筛选你接收的信息。所以你不断收到很多报告，这些报告把领导者和他们的团队描绘得非常正面。你必须主动打破这种局面，因为我认为组织会默认这样做。突然间，他们只会确保你收到的信息是让他们看起来很好的，因为他们想成功。这完全可以理解。但我认为你必须做很多工作来打破它，深入组织内部，直接与人交谈。我认为没有替代品。我希望有，但真的没有比四处走动和人聊天更好的方法了。我发现这在我们的组织中是获取优质信息非常快的一种不可思议的方式。然后你作为 CEO 会看到，有人在准备演示文稿，他们会说：“哦，这个演示文稿大概六周后能到你手上。”但当然，我现在就可以给你看一点。因为作为 CEO，日历安排、业务评审之类的事情，所有信息最终都会以比公司初期更慢的方式流动。所以，我认为花时间与人建立联系、形成信任关系非常重要。主动打破围绕你形成的信息系统——如果你不刻意去获取其他信息来源，那真的很关键。然后，我认为同理心和直觉也很有帮助，因为有时人们不愿意说出真实感受，只有当你注意到他们眼神或情绪中的某些东西时，你才会问：“那是你真实的感受吗？我们是不是应该换个方式做？”我认为真正理解他人感受、为他们创造分享观点的空间，这非常非常有价值。

turns.en: Do you ever find yourself feeling a little bit impatient with your team? Cuz I get this a lot. I'm always trying to make things move faster. And I think maybe there's a point of privilege where as the leader of an organization, you know, you can just break everything to make things happen, but maybe the intern in the office doesn't feel like they've got that permission, but urgency as a leader, speed. You talked about increasing the learning speed of the organization. Do you ever feel impatient as a leader?
turns.zh: 你有没有发现自己对团队有点不耐烦？因为我经常这样。我总是想让事情进展更快。也许作为组织领导者，你有一种特权，可以打破一切来推动事情，但办公室里的实习生可能觉得他们没有那个权限。但作为领导者，紧迫感、速度——你提到要提高组织的学习速度。你作为领导者会感到不耐烦吗？

turns.en: I'm extraordinarily impatient. Like, and I think it's in my DNA. I mean like my father would not like the idea of waiting like if you want to just like punish my father you put him in a line for anything like he will go ballistic like it's just like he the thought of waiting in a line for him would just drive him crazy because he's a very impatient person. I think I have some of that some of that impatience.
turns.zh: 我极其不耐烦。我觉得这刻在我的 DNA 里。我父亲就受不了等待——如果你想惩罚他，就让他排队，他会暴跳如雷。对他来说，排队的念头就会让他发疯，因为他是一个非常不耐烦的人。我想我也有点那种不耐烦。

turns.en: You know if I asked your team members what's Evan like as a leader what do you think they'd say?
turns.zh: 你知道，如果我问你的团队成员，埃文作为领导者是什么样的，你觉得他们会怎么说？

turns.en: Oh my goodness. Um I don't know. They might all have different perspectives because I really try to like bring out the best in our team members by showing different parts of myself. I'm not the same leader to every individual. That would be terrible. Uh I think so much of being a leader is trying to figure out for each individual and each person what sort of communication style will bring out the best in them and their unique abilities. So, you know, they engage the the way I engage with our CEO, Derek, is different than the way I engage with Betsy, who's our chief brand officer, is different than the way I engage with our our design team. And that that's important.
turns.zh: 哦，天哪。嗯，我不知道。他们可能都有不同的看法，因为我真的试图通过展现自己不同的侧面来激发团队成员的最佳状态。我对每个人不是同样的领导方式——那会很糟糕。我认为领导力很大程度上在于为每个人找到最能激发他们独特能力的沟通风格。所以，我与我们的 CEO Derek 互动的方式，不同于与首席品牌官 Betsy 互动的方式，也不同于与设计团队互动的方式。这很重要。

turns.en: If I asked them what you're good at, what would they say?
turns.zh: 如果我问他们你擅长什么，他们会怎么说？

turns.en: Um, I think I'm good at a at a couple things.
turns.zh: 嗯，我觉得我擅长几件事。

transcript.secZh: 理解用户需求与产品直觉

transcript.sec: Understanding user needs and product intuition

turns.en: I think I'm quite good at really understanding human needs and wants and figuring out how to reflect that in our product. Oftentimes, one of the competitions I like to have with our team is that a lot of times in engineering, people like to run A/B tests. They'll run four A/B tests and pick the one that performs best, like for a text string or something. If they want to put text in the app, they'll write four different variants. What I really like to do is figure out if I can write the variant that will win the A/B test without them having to run it. That intuition of what people will respond to, what makes sense to them, what's clear in terms of communicating through our product and features — I think that's something I can offer the team. Part of that is just because I've been doing it for 13 years, every week or almost every day, working with our team to figure out what will resonate with the people that use our products. So I think I'm good at that.
turns.zh: 我觉得自己很擅长真正理解人的需求和愿望，并想办法将其反映到我们的产品中。很多时候，我喜欢和团队进行一种竞赛——工程人员经常喜欢跑 A/B 测试，对吧？他们会跑四个 A/B 测试，然后选出表现最好的那个，比如针对一段文本字符串。如果要在应用里放一段文字，他们会写四个不同的版本。而我很喜欢做的事情是，看看我能不能写出那个不用跑测试就能赢的版本。那种对人们会如何反应的直觉，什么对他们来说有意义，什么在通过我们的产品和功能进行沟通时是清晰的——我觉得这是我能为团队带来的东西。部分原因是我已经做了 13 年，每周甚至几乎每天，都和团队一起努力，想清楚什么能引起我们产品用户的共鸣。所以我在这方面挺擅长的。

turns.en: I also think, maybe to my earlier point, I really work hard to bring out the best in people. Hopefully, if I've done my job really well, people say, 'Wow, I didn't think I could do that,' or 'I didn't know I could do that,' or 'I didn't know I was a really creative person, but you showed me that I'm actually a really creative person. That's so cool. Thanks.' How? I think oftentimes it's by giving people the courage and the space to be creative, and also showing them the different ways that creativity applies. For example, a lawyer might think, how could I be creative as a lawyer? But if you have a conversation with a lawyer, as we did early on in our business, and say, 'The problem today is all these privacy policies that are written make no damn sense. What if we were creative and actually wrote a privacy policy that people could understand? Wouldn't that be cool? How could we solve this problem differently and have a different set of expectations?' Then people say, 'Wow, that's interesting, let's try doing that.' And they do it, and they're like, 'Oh wow, I am creative. We can solve this problem differently. We don't need to just have another privacy policy like everybody else. We can work really hard to put it in human terms, and that would be better.' So I think showing all sorts of team members across our company the way that their work can be creative in service of our community or our advertising partners — that's something I hope to bring to our team.
turns.zh: 我还觉得，也许回到我之前的观点，我真的很努力去激发人们最好的一面。如果我把工作做得很好，人们会说，‘哇，我没想到我能做到’，或者‘我不知道我能做到’，或者‘我不知道自己是个很有创造力的人，但你让我意识到我其实很有创造力。太酷了，谢谢。’怎么做到的呢？我认为通常是给人们勇气和空间去发挥创造力，同时向他们展示创造力的不同应用方式。比如，一个律师可能会想，作为律师我怎么能有创造力呢？但如果你和律师聊一聊，就像我们公司早期做的那样，说：‘现在的问题是，所有这些隐私政策写得毫无意义。如果我们发挥创造力，写一份人们能理解的隐私政策，那不是很酷吗？我们怎么能用不同的方式解决这个问题，并建立一套不同的期望？’然后人们会说，‘哇，这很有意思，我们试试看。’他们做了之后就会说，‘哦，我确实有创造力。我们可以用不同的方式解决这个问题。我们不需要像其他人那样再写一份隐私政策。我们可以努力用通俗的语言来表达，这样会更好。’所以，向公司里各种团队成员展示他们的工作如何能够为我们的社区或广告合作伙伴服务，并发挥创造力——这是我希望带给团队的。

transcript.secZh: 对弱点的自我认知与谦逊

turns.en: And conversely, what are you not good at? If I asked all of them, I said, 'What's Evan not good at?'
turns.zh: 反过来，你不擅长什么？如果我问他们所有人，我说：‘Evan 不擅长什么？’

turns.en: Almost everything else. I mean, that's the challenge. There's got to be some defining traits that you're like — if you ask my team after this, they'll tell you what I'm not good at. They'll tell you what I'm good at, but they'll also tell you what I'm not good at, and they'll all agree. They'll have total consensus. No, but I do mean that seriously. In almost every area of our business, whether it's HR or legal or finance, whatever, I'm certainly far from the best. Our team members are extraordinarily talented at what they do across our business, and by nature I'm just not very good at those things. So I think for me, the real secret — or not a secret, but the focus of what I've tried to do over the years, as so many entrepreneurs do — is say, 'How can I spend more of my time doing what I'm good at?' Collaborating with our team, trying to create new products, be creative, and then have a team around me that's so much better at everything else that I couldn't possibly be better at. Especially as a younger CEO, I think that's probably even more important — to be able to have that self-awareness and humility to say, 'I don't know all the answers.'
turns.zh: 几乎其他所有事情。我是说，这就是挑战所在。肯定有一些定义性的特质——如果你之后问我的团队，他们会告诉你我不擅长什么。他们会告诉你我擅长什么，但也会告诉你我不擅长什么，而且他们会完全一致。他们会达成共识。不，我是认真的。在我们业务的几乎每个领域，无论是人力资源、法律还是财务等等，我肯定远非最好。我们的团队成员在他们各自的领域都非常有才华，而我天生就不太擅长那些事情。所以对我来说，真正的秘诀——或者不是秘诀，而是我多年来一直努力的重点，就像很多创业者一样——就是问自己：‘我怎么能把更多时间花在我擅长的事情上？’与团队合作，尝试创造新产品，发挥创造力，然后身边有一个团队，他们在其他所有事情上都比我强得多，我根本不可能比他们更好。尤其是作为一个更年轻的 CEO，我认为这一点可能更重要——能够有那种自我意识和谦逊，说‘我不知道所有答案’。

turns.en: Because you started this company at what, 21 or 22 years old, so you've never done running a public company before. So I think humility is probably even more important for someone like you at that stage.
turns.zh: 因为你创办这家公司时才 21 或 22 岁，所以你以前从未经营过一家上市公司。所以我认为，对于像你这样处于那个阶段的人来说，谦逊可能更加重要。

turns.en: Interestingly, it is the strategic advantage, right? To be 20 years old and to not know anything, so that you can ask any question and not look like an idiot, is the greatest gift in the world. I was almost always the youngest person in the room, and that was such a blessing because everyone's like, 'Oh, what are you working on?' 'Oh, an app. That's cool.' And I'd be like, 'Yeah, actually, would you mind talking to me about the best ways to prepare your company to be public?' And people are like, 'Sure.' So I think being able to use that naivete as the fundamental advantage to be able to learn quickly is so important.
turns.zh: 有趣的是，这恰恰是战略优势，对吧？20 岁，什么都不知道，所以你可以问任何问题而不会显得像个白痴，这是世界上最好的礼物。我几乎总是房间里最年轻的人，这真是一种福气，因为每个人都会说：‘哦，你在做什么？’‘哦，一个应用。很酷。’然后我就会说：‘是啊，实际上，你介意跟我聊聊准备公司上市的最佳方法吗？’人们就会说：‘当然可以。’所以我认为，能够利用那种天真作为快速学习的基本优势，是非常重要的。

turns.en: But have you lost that now?
turns.zh: 但你现在失去那种优势了吗？

turns.en: Hopefully never. I mean, that's the whole thing — what I love to do is the curiosity, the asking questions.
turns.zh: 希望永远不会。我是说，这就是全部——我喜欢做的就是保持好奇心，不断提问。

turns.en: Evan, this is my last question. What is the most important question for entrepreneurs that are listening to this conversation now, based on everything that you know and have done, that will help them that I didn't ask?
turns.zh: Evan，这是我的最后一个问题。基于你所知道和所做的一切，对于正在听这次对话的创业者来说，最重要的一个问题是什么？这个问题能帮助他们，而我没有问到。

turns.en: I think they should really ask themselves if they love what they're doing. And if they really love what they're doing, that will be the fuel that will carry them the whole way. But there are so many people who are trapped building businesses or in jobs that don't really love what they do, who haven't found how to use their special gifts in a way that applies to the business world. I think so much of life is trying to figure out what is that thing that I can do that I just love, that brings out the best in me and my talents. And I think not giving up in pursuing that is just so important.
turns.zh: 我认为他们应该真正问问自己，是否热爱自己正在做的事情。如果他们真的热爱，那就会成为支撑他们一路走下去的动力。但有很多人被困在创业或工作中，并不真正热爱自己所做的事，他们还没有找到如何将自己的特殊天赋以适用于商业世界的方式发挥出来。我认为生活中很大一部分就是努力弄清楚那件我热爱、能激发出我和我的才华最佳状态的事情是什么。而且我认为，不放弃追求这一点至关重要。

turns.en: Evan, thank you so much. Thank you so much for doing this today. I know you don't do a ton of podcasts, so I was particularly honored that you'd come and sit here with me. And hopefully it wasn't a nerve-wracking experience. I had a lot of fun and thank you for helping me with my 2025 resolution. Going to try and make some progress here. I'm so keen. I'm so keen to know who in your life has been nudging you to get out there more, because there must be someone.
turns.zh: Evan，非常感谢你。非常感谢你今天来做客。我知道你不太做播客，所以你能来和我坐在这里，我倍感荣幸。希望这不是一次令人紧张的经历。我玩得很开心，谢谢你帮我实现 2025 年的决心。我会努力取得一些进展。我很好奇，你生活中是谁一直在推动你多出来露面？因为肯定有这么一个人。

turns.en: Unfortunately, like everybody, which is why I've caved.
turns.zh: 不幸的是，和所有人一样，这就是我屈服的原因。

turns.en: So, well, thank you so much and it's so wonderful to get to know you more and understand how you're thinking about all of these things. And thank you for the wisdom that all of the entrepreneurs, the founders, the listening to this conversation have gained from you. And I do encourage you to do more of this kind of thing because there's so many of us that are so curious about the ups, the downs and everything in the middle of being an entrepreneur, building a company like you have in a world that is changing at absolute light speed. So it's a real service to all of us to get to know you, to get to know the thinking of the company. But also to be able to learn from the experience you've had. And I'm really excited now to go and try these spectacles. Awesome. Let's do it.
turns.zh: 那么，非常感谢你，能更深入地了解你和你的思考方式真是太棒了。感谢你让所有收听这场对话的企业家、创始人们获得了智慧。我确实鼓励你多做这类分享，因为我们中有太多人对创业者的起起落落以及中间的一切充满好奇，尤其是在这个以光速变化的世界里打造像你这样的公司。所以，认识你、了解公司的思考方式，以及能从你的经历中学习，对我们所有人来说都是一份真正的礼物。我现在非常兴奋要去试试这些眼镜了。太棒了，我们开始吧。

turns.en: We launched these conversation cards and they sold out. And we launched them again and they sold out again. We launched them again and they sold out again because people love playing these with colleagues at work, with friends at home, and also with family. And we've also got a big audience that use them as journal prompts. Every single time a guest comes on the diary of a CEO, they leave a question for the next guest in the diary. And I've sat here with some of the most incredible people in the world. And they've left all of these questions in the diary. And I've ranked them from one to three in terms of the depth. One being a starter question. And level three, if you look on the back here, this is a level three, becomes a much deeper question that builds even more connection. If you turn the cards over and you scan that QR code, you can see who answered the card and watch the video of them answering it in real time. So, if you would like to get your hands on some of these conversation cards, go to the diary.com or look at the link in the description below. Heat. Heat. N.
turns.zh: 我们推出了这些对话卡片，它们售罄了。我们再次推出，又售罄了。我们又推出一次，再次售罄，因为人们喜欢和同事、朋友、家人一起玩这些卡片。我们还有大量用户把它们当作日记提示。每次有嘉宾来《CEO 日记》，他们都会在日记里为下一位嘉宾留下一个问题。我和一些世界上最了不起的人坐在这里，他们在日记里留下了所有这些问题。我按深度把它们从 1 到 3 分级。1 级是入门问题。而 3 级，如果你看背面，这是一个 3 级问题，会变成更深层的问题，建立更多连接。如果你把卡片翻过来扫描二维码，就能看到谁回答了这张卡片，并实时观看他们回答的视频。所以，如果你想获得这些对话卡片，请访问 thediary.com 或查看下方描述中的链接。热度。热度。N。
